title: ops-light-secrets-server v0.1 — project charter & root
description:
# Charter — ops-light secrets server v0.1

**Objective.** Build an ops-light, self-hosted secrets server in Rust — one binary, no platform team — implementing a published subset of the Vault KV v2 + auth wire protocol so pinned, UNMODIFIED fnox / `vault` CLI / `bao` CLI versions work against it by configuration only. Open source, MIT, whole tree, no held-back tier (R19/KTD13). Also a professional-development project: the operator wants to genuinely understand sealing, identity, audit chaining, and envelope encryption; the primary success criterion is a written explainer produced without consulting the implementation, read back by a second person.

**Problem.** The pain is rotation, not storage: shared SaaS API keys (Populi/Canvas at a small non-profit) cannot be rotated because nobody can enumerate consumers (research: 64% of secrets exposed in 2022 were still active). Vault/OpenBao solve it with an ops surface (unseal ceremony, HA, policy DSL) that dwarfs the problem. Current best case: age-encrypted fnox.toml in git — safe on disk, not shareable, not rotatable without a commit.

**Session-settled key decisions (do not relitigate):**
1. Ops-light IS the product. The refusals (no HA, no clustering, no unseal ceremony, no policy language) are the wedge; the single-node ceiling is positioning.
2. Compose proven crates (axum, redb, age, chacha20poly1305, blake3, ed25519-dalek, rustls, clap, zeroize/secrecy). No crate ships a Vault KV v2 surface, an identity/token model, or a tamper-evident audit chain — that assembly is where the learning lands.
3. age recipient model over hand-built KEK/DEK: server holds an age identity supplied at boot (no unseal ceremony); age wraps a compact fixed-purpose keyring (KTD3); records use XChaCha20-Poly1305 AEAD bound to logical context so ciphertext cannot be transplanted.
4. Vault-API-compatible first. fnox-native protocol is phase 2, gated on a use-case-first design discussion with jdx (fnox discussion #615 — answered: direct-HTTP rewrite of fnox's vault provider is welcome upstream). AI agents are phase 3; identity schema carries kind (human|workload) from day one so agents land without migration.
5. Management surface = bundled CLI over an owner-only local Unix socket plus offline subcommands. NEVER remotely reachable (R34). One process, one binary, two routers.
6. Complexity budget — hard rules for the first two releases: no server-side process execution; no server-initiated network calls; no remotely reachable management routes; no second authorization model; no policy/validation expression language; no durable job queue or in-daemon scheduler; no client-process supervision; no new compatibility endpoints without U0/U11 evidence.

**Architecture.** Client →(TLS + X-Vault-Token)→ raw-target guard → canonical Resource parse → single transaction coordinator (bounded blocking storage executor owning one redb file) → authn (accessor + keyed blake3 MAC, constant time) → authz (segment-prefix grants over a closed capability enum) → KV/rotation/mgmt services → XChaCha20-Poly1305 records + MAC-authenticated clear records. Every mutation commits state+audit atomically; every read withholds its response until its audit entry commits (R26). Audit = blake3 hash chain over encrypted payloads, anchored by externally signed ed25519 checkpoints mirrored off host — the daemon never holds the signing key (KTD4).

**Design gates:** G0 client-contract freeze (U0) · G1 redb proof spike (U2) · G2 clear-record/crypto-encoding/audit-schema freeze (U2/U6) · G3 logical-backup-format freeze (U10). Storage-format fixtures, the final data-plane router, and public compatibility claims wait on their gates; U1 scaffolding/config/control-socket work does not.

**Milestones:** M0 contract freeze → M1 vertical slice (real fnox read E2E) → M2 recovery-ready alpha → M3 rotation-ready v0.1.

**Threat-model digest.** Protected in v0.1: offline theft of store/backup without the age identity (values, credential verifiers, audit payloads are ciphertext; DB alone can neither disclose nor validate/mint credentials); authz bypass via ambiguous path encodings; ciphertext transplant; offline clear-record edits/rollback (MACs + checkpointed state digest); crash atomicity; audit tamper through last retained checkpoint; restore-based credential resurrection (epochs); clock-rollback TTL extension; unauthenticated resource exhaustion. NOT protected: live root/debugger on the unlocked process; holder of store+boot key; client-side leaks after an authorized read; upstream SaaS compromise; RPO to last backup/checkpoint; structural-metadata disclosure (paths, identity names, grants, version numbers readable to whoever holds the DB — deliberate, documented); destroyed-version ciphertext surviving in old backups (destruction is logical, not cryptographic erasure).

**Deferred — do NOT build in v0.1:** KTD16 blind audit query index (its purpose key is minted at init but stays dormant); segmented audit retention/archive/prune (all events stay in the active DB; R27 capacity thresholds guard); Vault-CLI shim (BUSL exit ladder instead: (1) point fnox at `bao` CLI, (2) upstream direct-HTTP fnox provider, (3) documented `vault` install with honest licensing note); client-side discovery/import (v0.2); rotation campaigns beyond R33's status view + closeout guard; OIDC, metrics, operator-side rotation adapters, signed event outbox (v0.3, evidence-driven). Outside identity entirely: HA/clustering, unseal ceremony/KMS/TPM, policy DSL, web UI, hosted tier, dynamic secrets, plugins, in-daemon schedulers, plaintext bulk export, server-side secret-equality oracles.

**Supported v0.1 runtime:** Linux x86_64 + aarch64, host service (systemd reference deployment), local ext4/XFS with working advisory locks. Release claims apply only to the tested matrix (R38).

**Conventions binding every bead:** test-first on authorization, token-lifecycle, and audit-chain paths; all state flows through the transaction coordinator (module visibility enforces it); secrets never in argv/logs/traces/errors/panics (R25, R37); every acceptance example (AE1–AE13) lands as an automated test; fail-closed outranks throughput at every margin. Test observability: every integration/e2e suite logs scenario begin/end, each step, and failure context through the shared harness (U11.9); detailed logs are mandatory AND canary-scanned — logging never becomes the leak (R25).

Source plan (provenance; beads are the execution source of truth): docs/plans/2026-07-16-002-feat-ops-light-secrets-server-plan.md. Naming: working name `ops-light-secrets-server` until a real name is chosen — required before ANY public artifact (OQ3).

title: U0: Client characterization & compatibility contract capture
description:
**Goal:** Replace assumptions about fnox and Vault/OpenBao client behavior with reproducible recorded evidence from pinned versions, BEFORE the API router is finalized. This unit is the evidence base for the entire compatibility story (R1 endpoint matrix, R2 config-only guarantee, KTD14 pinned matrix) and for the BUSL exit ladder.

**Why it exists:** fnox's Vault provider currently shells out to the BUSL-1.1-licensed `vault` CLI (`Command::new("vault")` running `vault kv get -field=<field> <path>`, failing with an install hint when absent) — but the provider surface has grown configuration (`address`, `token`, `credential_command`) that may change transport across releases. So the compatibility target is recorded behavior of pinned versions, never folklore. The mount-discovery preflight (`sys/internal/ui/mounts/<path>`) is a Vault-internal endpoint and the historically fragile compat joint — capture it exactly. R15's single-binary claim covers the server, not its clients: whether a consumer host needs the BUSL `vault` binary is one of this unit's named questions.

**Scope (children):** pin the matrix, capture pinned-client behavior against a recording stub, normalize + commit fixtures, draft docs/compatibility.md, close Gate G0.

**Done-when:** the named questions are answered with committed evidence — (a) which endpoints each pinned client actually hits (preflight? seal-status? health? lookup-self?), (b) how tokens and TLS roots are supplied, (c) whether a fresh host runs fnox without the BUSL `vault` binary — NOT when the harness is beautiful.

**Files:** research/compat/, tests/fixtures/client-traces/, docs/compatibility.md (initial draft).

**Sequencing:** no dependencies; runs in parallel with U1. Gates the FINAL data-plane router (U5) and public compatibility claims (G0) — it does not gate scaffolding, configuration, local init design, or the control socket.

**Verification:** fixtures committed; matrix reviewed; R1/AE8 updated where captured behavior contradicts plan assumptions.

title: U1: Scaffold, config, local init, control socket, fail-closed startup
description:
**Goal:** A single binary whose `init` creates a valid store locally and exactly once, whose `serve` loads and validates configuration and refuses to start in ANY unsafe configuration, and whose management surface is an owner-only local Unix control socket. Plus the clock model and graceful-shutdown skeleton.

**Requirements:** R15 (single binary, no cluster/external DB/platform team); R16 (task-oriented CLI, human + stable JSON output); R17 (first management credential: disclosure-before-commit ordering); R18 (fail-closed startup refusal matrix — every refusal exits non-zero naming the setting, validation completes before the listener binds); R30 (initialization local, exclusive, one-time); R34 (management only via owner-restricted local socket with SO_PEERCRED, or offline commands); R35 (serve requires lifecycle=ready and known schema version); R36 (graceful shutdown: stop admission, drain, flush, final unsigned chain-head/shutdown event, zeroize); R37 (secret values via stdin/FD/credentials-dir/hidden TTY prompt — never argv; env dev-only behind an explicit unsafe flag); R6 (data-dir, single-node store); KTD1 (two axum routers, one process); KTD2 caveat (redb's internal lock is silently skipped on no-lock filesystems → startup takes its own explicit OS-level exclusive lock via a lockfile and refuses when it cannot be acquired).

**Approach:** `clap` CLI (`init`, `serve`, later subcommands); config from environment + file with unknown keys REJECTED; secret values accepted only through typed secret-source descriptors. `init` takes the exclusive lock, refuses non-empty/already-initialized directories, creates store id, schema version, keyring, audit genesis, and the first management identity in ONE transaction — in R17's failure-safe order: validate credential output sink → generate → write+flush credential to sink → only then commit. (Keyring/audit-genesis/first-identity internals are wired in as U2/U6/U3/U4 land — this unit owns the command, ordering contract, locking, and refusal behavior.) `serve` validation runs before bind: key material present and keyring decryptable, store initialized, schema known and lifecycle=ready, safe data-dir ownership/permissions/symlinks, exclusive lock, wall clock vs persisted high-water mark within tolerance, no non-loopback listener without TLS.

**Execution note:** test-first on the refusal matrix — each unsafe configuration is a failing test before the check exists.

**Files:** src/main.rs, src/config.rs, src/startup.rs, src/control/mod.rs, src/cli.rs, tests/startup.rs.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test startup` green; manual `--help` shows documented settings.

**Sequencing:** no dependencies; starts in parallel with U0.

title: U2: Keyring and encrypted single-node store
description:
**Goal:** A versioned key-value store in ONE redb file, persisting only AEAD ciphertext for secret values under keys held in an age-wrapped fixed-purpose keyring, with first-class per-secret metadata and format/lifecycle stamps.

**Requirements:** R4 (encryption at rest under purpose-separated keys; every ciphertext authenticated against immutable logical context — store, record type, path, version, key id — so transplanted ciphertext fails; structural metadata clear-but-MAC-authenticated); R5/KTD7 (plaintext only in bounded zeroize-on-drop buffers; allocator/kernel/TLS copies documented out of scope); R6 (single-node, no external DB); R11 (versioned writes, storage side); R21 (age identity never on disk/argv/history); R35 (format_version + lifecycle stamps); R39/KTD15 (keyed MACs on every security-relevant clear record; MAC failure ⇒ readiness false, never warning-only); KTD2 (redb, proof spike, explicit fd-lock); KTD3 (keyring + XChaCha20-Poly1305 + canonical binary encoding + fresh-nonce discipline); KTD11 (secret_meta first-class record).

**Opens with the KTD2 proof spike = Gate G1** (child bead). SQLite fallback triggers there or never — before higher units are shaped around the store.

**Tables:** `meta` (store id, format_version, lifecycle per R35, wall-clock high-water mark); `system_keyring` (the age envelope as ONE opaque value — not a sidecar file, so keyring changes commit atomically with their audit events); `secret_meta` (KTD11); `secrets` (versioned records: version number, created_time, key id, nonce, ciphertext). Later units add identities/grants/credentials/rotation/audit tables through the same schema module.

**Boot behavior:** server age identity loads into `secrecy::SecretBox`; the `system_keyring` envelope is read and decrypted exactly once; its embedded store id is compared with the clear store id BEFORE any other record is accepted — a transplanted envelope is rejected before readiness.

**Files:** src/store/mod.rs, src/store/crypto.rs, src/store/keyring.rs, tests/store.rs.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test store` green; hexdump spot-check shows ciphertext; spike results recorded against KTD2's quantitative gate.

**Depends:** U1 (scaffold). Feeds Gate G2 via checked-in crypto fixtures/vectors.

title: U6: Transaction coordinator and tamper-evident audit
description:
**Goal:** THE single commit boundary through which every state change and audit entry flows atomically, plus a hash-chained confidential audit log with externally signed, registered checkpoints — never records secret material, fails closed on write failure.

**Requirements:** R13 (one canonical, schema-versioned audit event per externally visible operation — event id, request id, audit schema version, identity, credential accessor, optional consumer-instance id, auth method, canonical resource, requested capability, operation, outcome, reason code, effective time, wall-clock observation, served/written version; NEVER values or credential material; flood drops aggregate into bounded entries); R14 (two-tier tamper evidence: local chain verification catches edit/remove/insert/reorder; verification against an independently retained signed checkpoint additionally catches wholesale re-chaining and truncation; live-forgery-before-anchor is the documented exclusion); R23 (observed-consumer queries scan primary events over a documented lookback window; v0.1 retains ALL events); R25 (secrets/credentials appear in NO server output — logs, traces, metrics labels, errors, panics; secret paths and identity names additionally banned from metrics labels); R26 (state+audit commit atomically; a read's response is withheld until its audit entry commits; audit-commit failure ⇒ client gets an error, no secret bytes); R27 capacity (warning + stop-admitting thresholds; preallocated recovery.reserve); R28 (admin changes linearized through the transaction boundary); KTD4 (blake3 per-entry chain over ENCRYPTED payloads; plaintext envelope = sequence, timestamp, previous hash, ciphertext digest — chain verifies without decrypting; ed25519 checkpoints signed EXTERNALLY, daemon never holds the private key, no in-daemon scheduler); KTD8 (one durability domain; one bounded blocking storage executor with a data lane + reserved control/recovery lane; explicit cancellation semantics; module visibility makes the coordinator the only commit path).

**Sequencing:** lands BEFORE U5 handlers are considered done — KTD8 makes audit a storage concern, not a late add-on. KTD16's index and segmented retention are deferred post-v0.1: the primary-event scan is the only query path and is always authoritative.

**Accepted cost (do not "optimize" away):** every audited operation, reads included, is a durable write serialized on redb's single writer. That is the single-node ceiling, named so nobody fixes the fail-closed design into a fast broken one. Batching is permissible later only if it still fails closed before the response.

**Files:** src/txn.rs, src/audit/mod.rs, src/audit/chain.rs, src/audit/checkpoint.rs, src/cli.rs, tests/audit.rs, tests/fault/txn_crash.rs.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test audit --test txn_crash` green; `<binary> audit verify` detects a hand-tampered log.

**Depends:** U2.

title: U3: Identity, capability, resource, and token model
description:
**Goal:** Distinct typed identities, canonical-resource authorization with segment-prefix grants over a fixed capability set governing EVERY KV path form, and TTL-bearing, revocable, capability-thin tokens.

**This is the authorization boundary — an authz bug is a full-store disclosure.** Test-first; property tests mandatory. age recipients never enter this path: R8 scoping is a server-side check (Product Contract).

**Requirements:** R7 (every human/app/integration is a distinct identity carrying kind: human|workload, extensible — display/audit only in v0.1, no kind-specific enforcement); R8 (one canonical resource = configured mount + validated logical segments, constructed exactly once per request; every API form — data, metadata, list, delete, undelete, destroy, versioned read — converts to it BEFORE the grant check; parser REJECTS rather than normalizes: encoded separators, dot segments, empty middle segments, repeated separators, control chars, invalid UTF-8, double-decoding; grants are exact-path or segment-prefix subtree matches — no globs, no regex, no deny rules, no policy language); R9 (TTL + revocation before expiry); R22 (closed, versioned capability set: read-current, read-history, list, write, soft-delete/undelete, destroy, destroy-all[local-only]; management capabilities each distinct: identity/grant mgmt, credential issue/revoke, rotation mgmt, consumer enumeration, audit read/export, backup, key rotation, diagnostics; built-in bundles like admin/auditor are aliases, not a policy language); R28 (disable/reduction/revocation linearized through the storage transaction boundary — tokens are capability-thin: identity, expiry, revocation state, NEVER a grant snapshot; every authorized request reloads current grants, which is what makes R28 implementable without an invalidation bus; every resulting rejection audited); KTD5 (thin tokens); KTD9 (RawTargetGuard before route extraction — axum's Path extractor percent-decodes before handlers run, so validation after extraction cannot tell a literal separator from an encoded alias); KTD10 (matcher returns {allow, resource, operation, matched_grant | deny_reason} — hot path uses allow only; full decision feeds audit and a management-gated `authz explain`; never echoed to unauthorized clients).

**Files:** src/identity/mod.rs, src/authz/resource.rs, src/authz/grant.rs, src/identity/token.rs, tests/identity.rs.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test identity` green, property tests included.

**Depends:** U2 (MAC-authenticated records), U6 (linearization runs through the coordinator).

title: U4: Vault-compatible auth surface
description:
**Goal:** Unmodified Vault clients obtain and present tokens via token auth (X-Vault-Token), the AppRole login endpoint, and lookup-self — behind request-rate limits — with NO bootstrap route anywhere on the network (Flow F1 is U1's local `init`).

**Requirements:** R31 (the ENTIRE supported auth surface: X-Vault-Token, POST /v1/auth/approle/login, GET /v1/auth/token/lookup-self; v0.1 tokens non-renewable — login advertises renewable:false, renew-self returns R3's explicit unsupported error, workloads re-login via AppRole; AppRole secret_ids and their tokens carry audience `data`; the Vault listener accepts only data-audience tokens, the control socket only control-audience credentials); R27 (request-size + attempt-rate limits with bounded limiter state on the unauthenticated surface — this router is where it lives); R9 (TTL/revocation); R24 (raw credentials disclosed once at creation, never persisted/logged/recoverable; public accessor selects exactly one record; secret validated against a keyed non-recoverable verifier under a keyring-held key — DB alone cannot verify guesses or mint credentials; verifier domain includes store id, kind, audience, accessor, issue epoch); KTD5 (structured opaque credentials, keyed blake3 MAC, constant time, dummy verifier for unknown accessors, NO password KDF anywhere — Argon2 deliberately dropped: server-generated 256-bit secrets are full-entropy, and a KDF on the per-request token path would be a CPU-burn DoS amplifier); Flow F3 (workload boots, presents role_id+secret_id, gets TTL token, reads scoped paths — no static key on disk).

**Execution note:** start from a failing test asserting the AppRole login response envelope byte-shape against U0's captured fixtures.

**Files:** src/auth/token.rs, src/auth/approle.rs, src/api/router.rs, tests/auth.rs.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test auth` green; envelope shape asserted against captured Vault responses.

**Depends:** U3.

title: U5: Vault KV v2 API surface + vertical slice (M1)
description:
**Goal:** Serve the declared Vault KV v2 endpoint matrix — read, write with CAS, list, soft-delete/undelete/destroy, versioned read, metadata (custom_metadata, max_versions, cas_required), mount-discovery preflight, seal-status, health — so an unmodified `vault` CLI uses the server as a backend. Closes with the first product milestone (M1): a REAL pinned fnox read end-to-end over TLS.

**Requirements:** R1 (the generated method/path/status/body matrix in docs/compatibility.md is the normative artifact; prose never adds endpoints absent from contract tests); R3 (everything outside the declared surface — PATCH included — returns an explicit unsupported error naming the endpoint; never empty success, never silent approximation; DELETE /v1/:mount/metadata/:path is in the unsupported set — full purge is local-only per R29); R11 (cas=0 create-only; positive cas must equal current version; cas_required rejects unconditional writes); R29 (soft-delete reversible ≠ destroy; destroy removes a version's ciphertext from every read path — logical destruction, never a cryptographic-erasure claim; max_versions bounds history but NEVER retires the protected prior version of an incomplete rotation → retention_deferred_by_rotation); R27 (bounds: a secret version is ONE bounded encrypted JSON object — total bytes, field count, name length, nesting depth, list cardinality); Flow F2 (read authorized → durable audit commit → value returned; plaintext never intentionally persisted).

**Known fragile joint:** `sys/internal/ui/mounts/<path>` must return options.version:"2" exactly — assert against U0 fixtures. Error envelopes {"errors":[...]}, status codes, and field-presence rules are GENERATED from the captured/differential contract (U0/U11) — no hard-coded 404/empty-list folklore.

**KTD12:** default mount `secret/`, reported KV v2; one mount in v0.1; other mounts or nonempty Vault namespaces fail explicitly. KTD6: secret-bearing responses carry Cache-Control: no-store and are never compressed.

**Files:** src/api/kv.rs, src/api/sys.rs, src/api/errors.rs, tests/api_kv.rs, tests/vertical_slice.rs.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test api_kv --test vertical_slice` green; U11 exercises the real CLIs.

**Depends:** U0 (captured contract, Gate G0), U2, U4, U6 (every handler commits through the coordinator).

title: U9: TLS transport, limits, and plaintext refusal
description:
**Goal:** Serve over rustls TLS with certificates reloadable WITHOUT restart (a restart costs an attended key ceremony — R36), bounded requests and timeouts, refusal of plaintext remote connections, reverse-proxy as an explicit listener type, and graceful-shutdown wiring.

**Requirements:** R20 (credentials/secrets never traverse the network cleartext; server refuses plaintext remote; reverse proxy supported only with loopback/Unix backend hop and explicit boundary config; forwarded identity headers ignored by default); R18 (non-loopback bind without TLS fails startup — seam laid in U1, TLS path + runtime refusal here; loopback 127.0.0.0/8 and ::1 may run plaintext); R27 (body/header limits, concurrency cap, handshake/request/drain timeouts via tower-http); R36 (TLS reload without restart; graceful shutdown: stop admission → drain bounded in-flight → flush → final unsigned shutdown event → zeroize); KTD6 (bearer tokens over rustls — exactly what unmodified Vault clients expect; no mTLS, no Tailscale-only posture; Cache-Control: no-store, no compression on secret-bearing responses); KTD1 adoption gate (`axum-server` community adapter is ADOPTED only after this unit proves TLS reload + graceful shutdown work — treat it as an independent dependency in review).

**Middleware ordering is security-significant and covered by an integration test:** raw-target guard → header-count/size bounds → sensitive-header marking → tracing/request-id → rate/concurrency limits → authentication → handler. X-Vault-Token (and any future credential-bearing header) is marked sensitive via tower-http BEFORE tracing is invoked (KTD9, R25).

**Reverse-proxy mode** is an explicit listener type, not a boolean relaxation: binds only a configured Unix socket or loopback address; forwarded source headers accepted only from the exact configured proxy peer in one allowlisted syntax — otherwise ignored; rate-limit source buckets use the verified proxy-derived source only in this mode.

**Files:** src/transport.rs, src/startup.rs, tests/transport.rs.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test transport` green.

**Depends:** U1.

title: U7: Consumers and rotation surfaces
description:
**Goal:** The reason the product exists (Flow F4): maintain the declared-consumer registry, reconcile declared/authorized/observed consumer views, surface a secret's age against a rotation interval, run the begin → cutover → closeout rotation record, and show post-write version adoption — so a key is rotated with the consumer list known BEFORE the change, not discovered by an outage.

**Requirements:** R10 (consumer view reports two separately labeled facts never collapsed: AUTHORIZED = every identity whose current grants permit read; OBSERVED = audited reads with last-read time + version served, inside R23's window; recency ANNOTATES, never filters); R12 (secret age = time since last completed rotation, else creation; per-secret interval lives in server-owned rotation metadata writable ONLY via the local control plane — Vault custom_metadata stays client-owned and cannot alter rotation policy; values written outside a rotation are labeled changed_since_last_completed_rotation); R23 (documented lookback window; scan primary audit events — KTD16 index deferred); R29 (a rotation-protected prior version may exceed max_versions → retention_deferred_by_rotation; closeout/cancel/supersession re-evaluates pruning); R33 (post-write status per snapshotted consumer: on-current / on-prior / silent-since-write, from audited read versions at declared-consumer, identity, AND stable consumer-instance granularity — one identity's replicas can disagree; language is "fetched version N", never "using"; completion guarded: every snapshotted consumer on-current OR explicit --acknowledge-unverified with an audited reason; expired/revoked instances = retired-without-proof, need a recorded reason or the override; redacted closeout report rides the audit chain — no separate signature scheme; beyond the one guard, observation only — no auto-revocation, no campaign state machine); R40 (registry record: stable consumer id, canonical resource, owner, environment, source, optional identity id, optional instance id, lifecycle status, last-verified, non-secret note — NEVER a secret/command/script/webhook; reconciliation states: declared-but-unauthorized, authorized-but-undeclared, declared-but-unobserved, observed-but-undeclared; rotation snapshots = union of declared + authorized, observed as evidence; an unresolved declared consumer blocks closeout unless overridden).

**Rotation lifecycle (R11):** begin snapshots consumers + current version + expected CAS without writing; cutover atomically CAS-writes the target and transitions prepared→cutover (CAS conflict leaves it prepared — never a silently changed snapshot); states prepared | cutover | completed | cancelled-before-cutover | superseded; NO post-cutover abort — recovery is explicit copy-forward rollback to a new higher version; the current-version pointer never moves backward.

**Files:** src/consumer.rs, src/rotation.rs, src/api/kv.rs, tests/rotation.rs.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test rotation` green (AE3, AE11, AE13 scenarios).

**Depends:** U5, U6.

title: U8: Key rotation — recipient rewrap, record re-encrypt, credential epoch
U10: Backup and restore
description:
**Goal:** A single operator takes an encrypted, transactionally consistent backup of the RUNNING server and restores it onto a fresh host by a documented, tested sequence that can neither resurrect revoked credentials nor activate a corrupt store.

**Requirements:** R32 (application-level logical archive from ONE consistent read transaction — never a copy of the live redb file as normative format; versioned canonical frames per table, encrypted record bytes unchanged, authenticated clear-record bytes, current keyring envelope, ALL audit events, per-table counts/digests, signed manifest with store id, schema version, keyring generation, chain head, latest included checkpoint, checksums; recovery keyring rewrapped to configured backup recipients with fingerprints recorded — fresh-host restore needs the archive recipient identity, NOT the failed host's active identity; archive NEVER contains a private age identity or the checkpoint signing key — the documented recovery package pairs archive + offline keys, and a store backup without them is not a recovery; restore builds in a temp sibling, verifies every frame/MAC/ciphertext/audit relationship/schema/count, fsyncs, atomically installs, and increments current_credential_epoch so every pre-restore token/secret_id fails epoch validation; identities/grants/secrets/audit survive, bearer credentials are reissued — restore mints and discloses a replacement emergency control credential via R41's primitive (U8.3, R17 ordering), because the epoch bump kills every pre-restore credential including control ones and a restored store must never be unauthenticatable); R14 (chain verifies against the last off-host checkpoint); R35 (restore under lifecycle=restoring); KTD17 (backup format versioned independently of redb — release-fixture restores stay stable across redb file-format changes; rollback recovery = restoring behind the newest independently retained checkpoint: requires explicit --start-recovery-epoch + reason, increments credential AND audit epochs, opens the new epoch with a fork-record event — verification reports the historical anchored epoch and the recovery fork separately, never presenting the chain as continuous).

**`backup verify --full` is the standing disaster-recovery rehearsal:** restore into an isolated temp dir, validate schema/tables, authenticate and decrypt EVERY encrypted record without emitting plaintext, verify chain + checkpoint relationship, tear down, record the success so doctor reports rehearsal age. A backup that has never survived a rehearsal is treated as unproven, and the docs say so.

**Owns Gate G3:** the logical backup format + restore/audit-epoch rules freeze here (child bead) — one of M0's four gates.

**Files:** src/backup.rs, src/cli.rs, docs/operating.md, tests/backup.rs.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test backup` green; documented sequence exercised end-to-end (AE7).

**Depends:** U2, U6.

title: U11: Compatibility harness, acceptance, fault, fuzz, differential suites
description:
**Goal:** Every acceptance example (AE1–AE13) runs as an automated test; KV reads are proven against the REAL pinned `fnox`, `vault`, and `bao` binaries; the invariants the design leans on — crash atomicity, parser safety, no secret leakage — are attacked continuously, not assumed; a differential corpus keeps the server honest against a pinned OpenBao reference rather than its own fixtures alone.

**Requirements:** R1 (matrix fidelity), R2 (config-only compat, the actual fnox binary is the acceptance subject — not a stand-in CLI), R3, R25, R26; AE1–AE13; KTD14 (pins in CI so a client upgrade cannot silently break R2).

**Standing suites:**
- **compat harness:** starts a server instance, drives it with pinned fnox (primary client, first-class gate), `vault` CLI, and `bao` (the non-BUSL path); asserts reads/writes/lists/preflight succeed configuration-only.
- **differential:** replays a normalized request corpus (reads, writes, CAS conflicts, deletes/undeletes/destroys, metadata, lists, missing paths, auth errors) against BOTH a pinned OpenBao reference server and this implementation; compares status class, error envelope, field presence; volatile fields normalized; deliberate divergences (R3's unsupported surface) allowlisted WITH stated reasons.
- **acceptance:** AE1–AE13 as integration tests asserting product-level outcomes.
- **fault:** child-process kill points around write/revoke/backup/re-encrypt; disk-full and short-write injection (feeds AE9).
- **property:** canonical path/grant equivalence models (with U3).
- **fuzz:** token strings, request paths, JSON bodies, encrypted record + checkpoint decoders — scheduled in CI.
- **canary scan:** seeded secret values must appear in NO log, trace, panic, or test artifact (R25) — includes U0 fixtures.
- **benchmark:** audited-read/write baseline on a named reference host, durability settings stated; later changes cannot materially regress it without explicit review.
- **e2e gauntlet:** scripted from-scratch run of the assembled system — build → init → serve → pinned clients → rotation → checkpoint → backup/restore → doctor — with per-stage detailed logging and a collected run directory (U11.9).

**Files:** tests/compat/mod.rs, tests/acceptance/mod.rs, tests/fault/, tests/differential/, fuzz/, benches/, scripts/e2e.sh, .github/workflows/ci.yml.

**Verification:** compat + acceptance + fault suites green in CI against pinned clients; fuzz corpus checked in.

**Depends:** U5, U6, U7. Runs continuously once U5 exists; complete only when every acceptance example passes.

title: U12: Operator experience — doctor, docs, licensing, release integrity
description:
**Goal:** A first-time operator reaches a first successful secret read by ONE documented sequence; recurring operations have coherent CLI workflows and a `doctor`; the project ships MIT-licensed with everything in the open tree and attestable release artifacts.

**Requirements:** R16 (CLI command families as a coherent surface: init, serve, doctor, identity, grant, token, approle, secret, consumer, rotation, authz explain, audit, key, credential epoch rotate, clock repair, backup, restore — each with human-readable AND stable JSON output); R17/R21/R37 (secret input never via argv; key-custody documentation); R19/KTD13 (MIT, entire tree, no ee/ carve-out — revised from MPL-2.0 at wayfinder T13, Robert's call; closed forks knowingly accepted; anti-BUSL posture expressed by openness, not copyleft); R38 (attestable releases: signed checksums, SBOM, build provenance; MSRV pinned; Cargo.lock committed + --locked enforced; cargo-deny licenses/advisories in CI; SECURITY.md reporting process; upgrade notes per storage-format change; release smoke test consumes the ACTUAL published binary including restoring a store fixture from at least one prior release; claims name supported OS/arch/filesystem/deployment — cross-compilation alone promotes nothing); Flow F1.

**README leads with the settled positioning paragraph** (working name until OQ3's before-any-public-artifact deadline): single-binary Vault-KV-v2-compatible secrets server for teams too small to run Vault; exists for one workflow — rotating a credential without an outage; every read audited atomically; declared/authorized/observed never collapsed; ops-light claim is a list of refusals; management on a local socket; works with unmodified vault/bao CLIs and fnox; MIT, whole tree. README also says "outgrow it → OpenBao" out loud.

**Files:** README.md, docs/operating.md, docs/compatibility.md, docs/cli.md (generated from --help, U12.6), src/cli.rs (doctor), LICENSE, CONTRIBUTING.md, SECURITY.md, deny.toml, examples/systemd/, .github/workflows/release.yml.

**Verification:** AE8 green; `cargo deny check` passes; release artifacts carry checksums, signatures, SBOM, provenance; a second reader can follow F1 unaided (Success Criteria).

**Depends:** U1–U11 (closes the release).

title: Gate G2: freeze clear-record format, canonical crypto encoding, audit event schema
description:
**Review-and-freeze decision gate.** Before persistent-format fixtures become binding, review and FREEZE the cross-release-verifiable formats together (they share the canonical-encoding discipline):

1. **Authenticated clear-record format (KTD15/R39):** fixed binary encoding + keyed MAC under the keyring's metadata-integrity key; MAC domain = store id, table id, primary key, schema version, record generation, canonical value bytes. Covers identities, grants, credential metadata, secret metadata (incl. the version-set summary — U2.2), rotation state, consumer declarations, schema metadata, keyring metadata (U2.3's clear record).
2. **Canonical crypto encoding (KTD3):** record headers + AEAD associated data = magic bytes, format version, cipher_suite_id, store id, record-domain id, key id, length-prefixed mount and canonical path segments, logical record id, secret version where applicable, created_time. NO JSON, debug formatting, string concatenation, platform endianness, or map iteration order in any authenticated encoding.
3. **Audit event schema + chain/anchor encodings (R13/KTD4/KTD15):** canonical, schema-versioned event fields (event id, request id, schema version, identity, accessor, consumer-instance id, auth method, canonical resource, capability, operation, outcome, reason code, effective time, wall-clock observation, served/written version) + the plaintext chain envelope (audit-epoch id, epoch-scoped sequence, timestamp, previous hash, ciphertext digest — U6.3's KTD17-ready form) + the checkpoint canonical descriptor encoding (U6.4) + the state-digest tuple encoding (U2.5). The last two are cross-release verifiable formats — R14 requires OLD checkpoint files to re-verify after upgrades — so they freeze here, not at some later gate.
4. **Recovery-family event types (KTD17):** the restore recovery event, the rollback fork-record event, and the emergency-credential-issuance record are defined JOINTLY with U10.1 (this gate depends on it) so the frozen schema includes them — G3 must not need a post-freeze schema change.

**Freeze means:** checked-in test vectors and cross-version fixtures become binding — specifically U2.7's header/AAD/MAC/keyring vectors, U6.3's audit-event encoding vectors + chain-envelope fixtures, and U6.4's checkpoint-descriptor + state-digest vectors; any later change is an R35 forward-only schema migration shipping upgrade notes (R38). Explicitly NOT in this gate: KTD16's audit query-index format — deferred post-v0.1; the index is rebuildable from primary events, so its format is never load-bearing (wayfinder T08).

**Exit:** formats reviewed against the recorded designs, all named vector sets committed, sign-off noted here. Feeds milestone M0.

title: M0: Contract freeze (gates G0–G3 closed)
description:
**Milestone M0 — Contract freeze.** Reached when: U0 is complete (client contract captured, Gate G0 closed); U2's redb storage spike has passed with quantitative thresholds recorded (Gate G1 — or the SQLite fallback was triggered and recorded); the authenticated clear-record, crypto-header, and audit-event formats are reviewed and frozen (Gate G2); and the logical-backup format + restore/audit-epoch rules are frozen (Gate G3).

**Why it matters:** these four gates affect persistent formats and security boundaries, not ordinary implementation detail. After M0, storage-format fixtures are binding, the final data-plane router may be built against captured evidence, and public compatibility claims may reference the pinned matrix. Before M0, U1 configuration/CLI/control-socket/transport work may proceed — storage-format fixtures, the final API router, and public compatibility claims may NOT.

**Checklist:** G0 closed · G1 spike passed + thresholds recorded (or fallback recorded) · G2 formats frozen with committed vectors · G3 backup format frozen.

title: M1: Security-kernel vertical slice — real fnox read E2E
description:
**Milestone M1 — Security-kernel vertical slice.** `init` → `serve` over TLS → the pinned fnox binary resolves a secret end-to-end — through audience-bound credentials (R24/R31), canonical authorization (R8/KTD9), authenticated state (R39), atomic audit (R26), and bounded storage admission (KTD8). A real fnox read at the close of U5.

**A development milestone, not a release.** It proves the security kernel composes: raw-target guard, resource parse, token verify, grant reload inside the transaction, decrypt, audit-before-transmit, TLS. Everything after this layers rotation, recovery, and hardening onto a proven core.

**Evidence:** tests/vertical_slice.rs green — server up over TLS, fnox configured by documentation only, secret round-trips, audit chain carries the read with served version.

title: M2: Recovery-ready alpha
description:
**Milestone M2 — Recovery-ready alpha.** Reached when ALL pass: recipient rewrap (U8); the credential-epoch incident command R41; logical backup; `backup verify --full` rehearsal; restore onto a fresh host; rollback recovery audit epochs; the crash/fault suite (kill points, disk-full, short-write); R27's disk-reserve fail-closed behavior — ON TOP OF the M1 vertical slice (explicit dependency: an alpha that cannot serve a KV read over TLS to a pinned client is not an alpha).

**Why it matters (Success Criteria):** NO public v0.1 artifact may be described as recovery-ready or suitable for real non-production credentials until M2 passes on an ASSEMBLED BINARY. Production adoption stays separately gated on independent review of the threat model, crypto format, authorization boundary, and recovery evidence (the lx_data_lake credentials reach FERPA-protected student records — that is why production is not a v0.1 gate).

**Assembled-binary evidence (defined so the gate is satisfiable):** the CLI-drivable scenarios — backup; restore with epoch bump; `backup verify --full`; `key recipient rewrap`; `credential epoch rotate`; rollback restore with `--start-recovery-epoch` — are proven by executing the e2e gauntlet's recovery-stage block (U11.9, an explicit dependency) against a release-profile build. Kill-point crash tests remain SOURCE-LEVEL evidence (test instrumentation does not ship in release binaries — the fault suite gates M2 from CI, not from the binary). The PUBLISHED-binary run of the same stages is U12.5's release smoke, which gates M3/the release rather than M2.

**Checklist:** recipient rewrap atomic under kill points · `credential epoch rotate` ends every remote credential and mints an emergency control credential with R17 ordering · backup/restore round-trip with epoch bump (AE7) · rollback restore forks the audit epoch explicitly · rehearsal (`backup verify --full`) passes and records its age (both live-path and recovery-path kinds — U10.5) · crash tests green · reserve allowance lets checkpoint/backup/shutdown run after the data plane fails closed · gauntlet recovery stages green on a release-profile build.

title: M3: Rotation-ready v0.1 release
description:
**Milestone M3 — Rotation-ready v0.1.** The release milestone. Reached when: declared-consumer reconciliation (R40), begin/cutover/closeout rotation with the guarded completion (R33), the full declared KV surface (R1), the current compatibility matrix (KTD14), the published-binary smoke test, documentation, and release attestations (R38) all pass.

**Definition of Done (global):**
- Every requirement R1–R41 advanced by at least one unit and its cited tests.
- AE1–AE13 pass in CI against the pinned client matrix — the fnox binary included, not only the Vault CLI.
- The full Verification Contract passes: cargo test --locked --all-targets · compat harness (pinned fnox/vault/bao) · clippy -D warnings · fmt --check · cargo deny check · audit-verify vs hand-tampered log · crash/fault suite · property/model suite · scheduled fuzz (incl. archive/export-bundle targets, U11.5b) · secret-output canary scan · differential vs pinned OpenBao (divergences allowlisted with reasons) · performance baseline · clear-state integrity suite · raw-target parser corpus · storage backpressure suite · recovery-epoch suite · consumer-reconciliation matrix · aarch64 cross-arch job · compatibility-doc regeneration drift gate · release integrity · release smoke (the e2e gauntlet incl. doctor, rotation, recovery stages, and audit export — on the published binary, both architectures).
- age identity, first management credential, and workload secret_ids never touch disk as plaintext, shell history, or argv — asserted by tests and reviewed in the diff.
- Fail-closed postures hold: R18 startup refusals (clock cases included), R20 plaintext refusal, R26 audit atomicity, R39 MAC-failure readiness, R32 restore epoch, R17 disclosure-before-commit, R33 closeout guard.
- No handler commits state or audit outside the transaction coordinator (module visibility + crash suite prove it).
- Fresh-nonce discipline proven under deterministic-RNG suites; no deterministic nonce construction anywhere.
- Benchmark baseline recorded with durability settings named. Abandoned-attempt code removed.

**Falsifiable ops-light claim:** one operator cycle — provision identity, issue + revoke credential, rotate a secret, verify the audit chain — through one documented workflow: no external service, no restart, no policy language.

title: U0.1: Pin the client matrix with verified provenance (KTD14)
description:
**Build:** Resolve and pin, at execution time (KTD14): the latest stable HashiCorp `vault` CLI, the latest stable OpenBao `bao` CLI, and the two most recent fnox releases. One legacy Vault line is retained ONLY if it represents a documented deployed consumer or captures a materially different request contract — otherwise skip it.

**Provenance discipline:** verify every downloaded artifact checksum; record exact versions, artifact checksums, and download provenance. Pins are committed to CI and docs/operating.md so a client upgrade can never silently change what R2's config-only guarantee was tested against.

**Why:** compatibility is versioned evidence, not a permanent assumption. fnox's provider surface (`address`, `token`, `credential_command`) may change transport across releases; the guarantee holds for tested versions, not all future CLIs. The mount-discovery preflight is Vault-internal and may change per release — pinning is what makes the claim honest.

**Output:** research/compat/ notes + pinned-version manifest consumed by U11's CI and by the capture runs (sibling bead).

**Acceptance:** matrix of pinned versions exists with checksums + provenance; CI can fetch exactly these artifacts; the legacy-line decision (include or not) is recorded with its reason.

title: U0.2: Recording stub + capture pinned client behavior (incl. bao rung proof)
description:
**Build:** A recording stub server plus capture runs for every pinned client (fnox ×2, vault, bao), using SYNTHETIC high-entropy canary credentials only (they must be scannable later — R25 canary discipline applies to fixtures too).

**The stub is a RESPONDING recorder, not a passive one** — a passive recorder cannot elicit the behavior this bead must capture: clients abort after the mounts preflight unless the stub returns a plausible KV v2 mount response, so later endpoints would never appear; retry/error behavior only exists if scripted failure responses exist; TLS-root supply is only observable if the stub serves TLS. Therefore: (a) serve minimally valid canned responses per endpoint (mounts preflight, KV read, seal-status, health, lookup-self — shapes cribbed from a live pinned `bao` dev server and themselves recorded as provenance); (b) serve TLS from a locally generated test CA so each client's root-supply mechanism (VAULT_CACERT / env / flag) is exercised and recorded; (c) run scripted fault passes per client (403, 404, 500, 503-sealed, connection reset, timeout) to capture retry and error handling.

**Capture per client:** method, path, query, headers, body shape, TLS configuration, subprocess execution (does this fnox version shell out to the `vault` CLI or speak HTTP itself? record the exact `vault` subcommands fnox invokes AND the HTTP requests those CLI versions emit), retry behavior, status/error handling.

**Path-encoding capture matrix (feeds G0's segment-character contract and KTD9's parser corpus):** for each pinned client, request secret paths containing spaces, UTF-8, percent-encoded characters (including %2F and %25), `.` and `..` segments, leading/trailing/double slashes, `+`, and an over-long segment; record the exact bytes each client puts on the wire (and where fnox/CLI reject client-side instead). The charter's threat model protects against authz bypass via ambiguous path encodings — G0 must not freeze a segment contract with zero recorded evidence behind it.

**Named questions this bead must answer with evidence:**
1. Which endpoints does each client actually hit? (mount preflight? sys/seal-status? sys/health? auth/token/lookup-self?)
2. How are tokens and TLS roots supplied per client?
3. Can a fresh host run fnox WITHOUT the BUSL `vault` binary — in particular, does pointing fnox at the `bao` CLI work (BUSL exit-ladder rung 1: OpenBao's CLI is a vault-CLI fork; zero code — this capture proves or kills the rung)?
4. What path-segment characters do the pinned clients emit, and how are they encoded on the wire? (The resulting encoding table is a G0 freeze input.)

**Why:** the captured contract — not the KV v2 API docs — is the compatibility target (R1 declares the preflight and seal-status precisely because the CLI needs them). Everything U5 builds and U11 asserts starts from these recordings.

**Acceptance:** each pinned client's capture replays cleanly against the recorded set (deterministic enough to assert against); the four named questions have recorded answers, including the per-client path-encoding table and per-client fault-pass (retry/error) recordings.

title: U0.3: Normalize + commit client-trace fixtures (canary-clean)
description:
**Build:** Fixture normalization pipeline + committed fixtures in tests/fixtures/client-traces/ as first-class artifacts (KTD14).

**Normalization removes, before ANY artifact is committed:** tokens, credential-command output, TLS private material, host paths, usernames, and nondeterministic identifiers. The secret-output canary scan (U11) covers these fixtures — the synthetic canary credentials used during capture must be absent from committed artifacts.

**Why:** these fixtures are what U4 asserts auth envelopes against, what U5 builds the router against, and what U11 replays forever. They are the frozen half of Gate G0. Committing raw captures would leak capture-host details and make the suite nondeterministic.

**Acceptance:** fixtures committed and replayable; normalization is a repeatable script (rerunning capture + normalize yields stable fixtures); canary scan over the fixture tree is clean.

title: U0.4: docs/compatibility.md draft + BUSL exit-ladder verdict
description:
**Build:** Initial docs/compatibility.md — a human-readable matrix naming, per client version: every endpoint required and every prerequisite binary. Plus the recorded BUSL exit-ladder verdict.

**BUSL exit ladder (Scope Boundaries, wayfinder T03) — cheapest rung first:**
1. Point fnox at the `bao` CLI (zero code; U0.2's capture proves or kills this rung).
2. Contribute a direct-HTTP provider upstream to fnox — CONFIRMED welcome by jdx (fnox discussion #615): replace the existing vault provider's CLI-backed internals in place, preserving configuration/auth/TLS behavior, with compat tests against both Vault and OpenBao.
3. Document `vault` CLI installation with an honest licensing note — never claiming a fully-OSI toolchain while it is the only path.
A shim is NOT a v0.1 gate; it is post-v0.1 and evidence-gated (built only if all three rungs fail AND someone is actually blocked).

**Also:** update R1's endpoint matrix, AE8's documented onboarding sequence, and U5/U11 fixture expectations wherever captured behavior contradicts plan assumptions — evidence wins over prose memory. Record contradictions explicitly in this bead when closing it.

**Acceptance:** matrix reviewed; per-client endpoint + prerequisite table complete; ladder verdict recorded; any plan-contradiction noted and propagated to the affected beads.

title: Gate G0: client contract freeze
description:
**Review-and-freeze decision gate (G0).** Freezes the versioned client contract and client prerequisites: which endpoints each pinned client needs (R1's declared surface), the exact request/response shapes (fixtures), token/TLS supply mechanisms, and the supported segment-character contract that KTD9's parser will publish — the latter frozen FROM U0.2's recorded path-encoding table (per-client wire bytes for spaces, UTF-8, percent-encoding incl. %2F/%25, dot segments, slash variants), never from assumption.

**What G0 blocks:** the FINAL data-plane router (U5) and public compatibility claims. What it does NOT block: U1 scaffolding, configuration, local init design, control socket, transport work.

**Done-when (from the unit's own definition):** U0's named questions are answered with committed evidence — which endpoints each pinned client hits, how tokens and TLS roots are supplied, whether a fresh host runs fnox without the BUSL `vault` binary, and what path-segment encodings each client emits — not when the harness is beautiful.

**Exit:** R1's endpoint matrix, AE8's documented sequence, the segment-character/encoding table, and U5/U11's fixtures updated from captured behavior. Feeds milestone M0.

title: U1.1: Cargo scaffold, clap CLI, config loading, typed secret-source descriptors
description:
**Build:** Cargo workspace scaffold (single binary crate), `clap` CLI skeleton (`init`, `serve`; later subcommands slot in per unit), configuration loading, and typed secret-source descriptors.

**Config rules (R18/R37):**
- Sources: environment + a config file. UNKNOWN KEYS ARE FATAL — refuse to start naming the key (typo'd security settings must never be silently ignored).
- Secret values (age identity, TLS key passphrases, any credential) are accepted ONLY through typed secret-source descriptors: stdin, inherited file descriptor, systemd-style credentials directory (`LoadCredential`/`LoadCredentialEncrypted` layout, read at boot), or hidden interactive TTY prompt. Environment-variable delivery is development-only behind an explicit unsafe flag. Secret material via argv is refused (argv is world-readable via /proc and lands in shell history).
- Every refusal exits non-zero naming the offending setting.

**Also:** pin MSRV, commit Cargo.lock (CI enforces --locked from day one, R38), rustfmt/clippy config, module layout per plan file map (src/config.rs, src/startup.rs, src/control/, src/cli.rs). Dependency baseline from the fnox-proven stack: tokio, axum, clap, rustls; add redb/age/chacha20poly1305/blake3/ed25519-dalek/zeroize/secrecy as their units land.

**Acceptance:** binary builds; `--help` documents settings; unknown-config-key and argv-secret refusal tests pass (seed tests/startup.rs); secret-source descriptor parsing round-trips each channel.

title: U1.2: init — exclusive one-time local initialization, disclosure-before-commit (R17/R30)
description:
**Build:** `init` — local, exclusive, one-time initialization (R30) with R17's failure-safe credential disclosure ordering.

**Behavior:**
1. Take the explicit OS-level exclusive lock (fd-lock on a lockfile in the data directory — KTD2 caveat: redb's internal lock silently no-ops on no-lock filesystems, so we never trust library-internal locking). Refuse a non-empty or already-initialized directory; refuse a filesystem that cannot hold the lock. **"Non-empty" means: contains any entry other than recognized incomplete-init artifacts — the lockfile itself, and a store file containing no committed initialization transaction.** A rerun over such artifacts is a clean re-init (it truncates/replaces the uncommitted store file); anything else (foreign files, `lost+found`, an initialized store) refuses with an error naming the offending entry. Without this definition the lockfile makes the directory non-empty and a literal implementation refuses its own happy path — and every abort-before-commit rerun.
2. Create store id, schema version, encrypted keyring, audit genesis, and the first management identity in ONE transaction. (Internals wired in as units land: keyring creation = U2.3, audit genesis = U6.3, first identity + credential verifier + bootstrap grant = U3/U4 — see U3.2's init wiring: identity, admin-bundle grant, and credential must commit atomically or the bootstrap credential authenticates but is denied everything, recovery.reserve preallocation = U6.5 (R27). This bead owns the command frame, locking, refusals, transaction shape, and disclosure ordering.)
3. **R17 ordering — disclosure BEFORE durability:** open and validate the credential output sink (TTY or caller-supplied file descriptor) FIRST → generate the credential → write and flush it to the sink → only then commit the initialization transaction containing its verifier. A crash or broken pipe before commit leaves NO initialized store and a harmless disclosed string (init reruns cleanly); a crash after commit leaves an operator already holding the credential. A failed sink write ABORTS initialization.
4. The first management credential carries audience `control` and a short bootstrap TTL — **default 24h, overridable via an init-time `--bootstrap-ttl` flag**; it is stored only as a keyed verifier — never recoverable afterward (R24). No bootstrap route exists on any network listener, ever. **The disclosure output prints the credential's expiry and the expected next step** ("use this credential over the control socket to mint a durable admin credential — U4.5 commands"). **Expiry/loss recovery:** the offline R41 emergency control-credential command (U8.3 — the same primitive clock repair uses); docs/operating.md documents it as the bootstrap-expired path. An initialized store with an expired bootstrap credential is recoverable, never a designed lockout.
5. Repeated or concurrent `init` cannot create a second first-administrator; re-init of an initialized store is refused. **Audit split (R30):** attempts against an initialized, UNLOCKED store are refused AND recorded in that store's audit chain; attempts while another process holds the lock, or before any store exists, are refused with a non-zero exit and stderr report only — no chain write is possible in those states, and the acceptance tests assert exactly this split.

**Acceptance:** broken/unwritable sink → abort before commit, no store, rerun succeeds; kill between sink flush and commit → retryable uninitialized dir; kill after commit → store exists, disclosed credential authenticates; two concurrent inits → exactly one first identity; rerun over leftover lockfile + uncommitted store file succeeds; init into a directory containing any foreign file refuses, naming it; re-init attempt against an initialized unlocked store is refused AND appears in the audit chain; disclosure output includes bootstrap expiry + next-step text.

title: U1.3: serve fail-closed startup validation matrix + shutdown skeleton (R18/R35/R36)
U1.4: owner-only control socket + SO_PEERCRED + control router skeleton (R34)
description:
**Build:** The owner-restricted local control socket + control-plane router skeleton (R34, KTD1).

**Rules:**
- Management operations — identity, grant, credential, rotation, consumer, audit-query, backup, key ops — are reachable ONLY here (or via offline CLI commands). They are ABSENT from the remote router: a remote client with a valid management-capable token gets explicit route-not-found, not an authorization failure (AE12 seam).
- Unix socket with owner-only permissions. On Linux, each accepted connection must ALSO present a kernel-reported peer uid (SO_PEERCRED) equal to the configured service owner or an explicitly allowed administrative uid. Socket mode bits, the peer-uid check, and the control credential are ALL required — none substitutes for another (defense in depth: mode bits can be raced/misconfigured, uid can be shared, credentials can leak; requiring all three means one failure is not a compromise).
- Control actions remain authenticated (control-audience credential) and audited — attributable even though local.
- Served with `axum::serve` on a tokio UnixListener (axum's own Unix-listener support — KTD1); same tower middleware family as the data plane where applicable.

**Why local-only:** the highest-privilege operations are simply not remotely reachable, and no incompatible pseudo-Vault management endpoints get invented. Remote administration, if it ever exists, is a later phase with its own authentication design.

**Acceptance:** management routes 404/absent on the remote router and succeed over the socket; disallowed peer uid → rejected and audited; wrong-audience credential on the socket → rejected (with U4).

title: U1.5: clock model — high-water mark, tolerance, readiness, offline clock repair
description:
**Build:** The clock model (Assumptions — clock model) + offline `clock repair` command.

**Model:** wall clock is trusted for token expiry (R9), rotation age (R12), and audit display timestamps; hash-chain order is COMMIT SEQUENCE, not wall time. At startup record a (wall_clock, monotonic_clock) anchor; effective time advances from the monotonic clock while periodically checking wall-clock agreement; small drift reconciles within a documented tolerance. The store `meta` table persists a high-water mark of the highest accepted wall-clock time (updated inside transactions that are durable writes anyway — KTD8, so it costs nothing extra). EFFECTIVE TIME NEVER MOVES BACKWARD while the server runs.

**Failure behavior:**
- Startup: clock behind the persisted mark beyond tolerance → refuse (exit non-zero); persisted mark implausibly ahead of current clock → refuse. An explicit audited local override may start the server once. **Override semantics:** the override is a one-invocation CLI flag consumed at boot, its use written to the audit chain at open; the boot clamps effective time to max(wall clock, persisted mark) — the mark is PRESERVED, never lowered — so TTL conclusions never extend (a rollback-attack boot with the override still cannot resurrect or stretch outstanding bearer credentials; effective time stands at the mark until wall clock catches up). Repairing a genuinely wrong mark is exclusively `clock repair`'s job, with its epoch bump — the override never substitutes for it.
- Runtime: forward or backward discontinuity beyond tolerance → readiness false BEFORE the anomalous value is persisted (a rolled-back clock cannot silently extend bearer credentials; an NTP/virtualization fault cannot poison the store into a durable startup lockout). **Readiness auto-recovers** once wall and monotonic clocks re-agree within tolerance for a documented settle period — no restart required.

**`clock repair` (offline):** for a mark known to be wrong. Requires the exact old mark, a replacement time, and an audited reason; increments the credential epoch before the store returns to ready — prior TTL conclusions can no longer be trusted, so every outstanding bearer credential dies (R41 primitive). Because the epoch bump kills the operator's own control credential too — and no bootstrap route exists on any listener — clock repair reuses R41's FULL primitive (owned by U8.3, bead olss-charter-qul.10.3): it mints and discloses a replacement emergency control credential with R17's sink-validated disclosure-before-commit ordering. An epoch-bumping command must never lock the operator out. (The epoch-bump + replacement-credential portion completes when U8.3's R41 primitive lands — same land-as-ignored pattern as U1.6's note; until then `clock repair` must NOT bump the epoch, because a bump without replacement-credential minting is exactly the lockout this design forbids.) Per U1.3's lifecycle rule, repair executes as a single atomic store transaction — the store stays `ready`; crash mid-repair leaves the prior mark intact.

**Explicitly rejected:** monotonic-only TTLs (do not survive restart usefully); auto-fixing operator clocks. Operators without NTP accept that TTL enforcement tracks their clock.

**Acceptance:** boot-behind-mark refusal + one-shot audited override; override boot clamps effective time to the mark (token issued pre-rollback still expires on schedule — no TTL extension) and the mark is unchanged after the override boot; runtime future-jump → readiness false, mark NOT persisted; readiness auto-recovers after clocks re-agree for the settle period; restart after injected poisoned future mark → refused; clock repair resets mark, bumps epoch, old credentials invalid, replacement emergency control credential disclosed exactly once and authenticates.

**Note:** persistence of the mark lands with U2.2's meta table — this bead depends on it.

title: U1.6: startup/init/shutdown test suite (tests/startup.rs, AE4 + refusal matrix)
description:
**Build:** tests/startup.rs — the startup/init/shutdown suite, covering AE4 and the full R18 refusal matrix. Test-first discipline: these exist before/alongside the checks. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a) — scenario begin/end, per-step, failure-context dump.

**Scenarios (each one a named test):**
1. AE4: boot with no encryption key material → exits non-zero naming the missing setting; does NOT generate a throwaway key.
2. `serve` on an uninitialized store → non-zero; `init` on an initialized store → refused AND recorded in that store's audit chain (U1.2 audit split); two concurrent `init` runs → exactly one first management identity (R30).
3. Non-loopback listener without TLS → non-zero (R18/R20 seam).
4. Second server instance on the same data directory → non-zero on the explicit OS lock (KTD2 caveat).
5. Any non-ready lifecycle (reencrypting/restoring/migrating/compacting) or schema newer than binary → non-zero (R35).
6. Secret via argv or unknown config key → non-zero (R37/R18).
7. `init` with broken/unwritable credential sink → aborts before commit, no store, rerun succeeds; kill points between sink flush and commit → retryable uninitialized dir; kill after commit → disclosed credential authenticates (R17).
8. Key material via systemd-style credentials directory (LoadCredential layout) loads; same content via argv refused (R21/R37) — this test also backs U12's shipped example units.
9. Boot with clock behind persisted mark beyond tolerance → non-zero; audited local override starts once (R18); override boot leaves the persisted mark unchanged and clamps effective time (no TTL extension — U1.5).
10. Runtime far-future wall-clock jump with normal monotonic → readiness false; future observation NOT persisted as new mark; readiness auto-recovers after clocks re-agree for the settle period (U1.5).
11. Restart after injected poisoned future mark → refused; `clock repair` resets, bumps credential epoch, old credentials invalid (R18/R41); the replacement emergency control credential is disclosed via R17 sink ordering and authenticates — clock repair never locks the operator out.
12. Management routes absent from remote router, served on control socket (feeds AE12).
13. Valid config → listener binds, health responds.
14. World-writable or foreign-owned data directory, and a symlinked store path → non-zero naming the offending path (R18 rows with no prior scenario).
15. Keyring embedded store id ≠ clear store id → non-zero (KTD3).
16. Lock-acquisition failure path → non-zero (unit-level fault injection on the fd-lock call — a no-lock filesystem is impractical to mount in CI).
17. `init` while a running `serve` holds the exclusive lock → refused (non-zero + stderr only; no chain write — U1.2 audit split).
18. SIGTERM with in-flight requests → admission stops, drain completes, final UNSIGNED shutdown event committed and the chain verifies to it, zeroize hook runs (R36).
19. Secret via environment variable without the explicit unsafe development flag → non-zero naming the setting; with the flag → accepted and a warning logged (R37/U1.1).
20. Key material delivered via stdin and via inherited FD → loads and serve proceeds (U1.1's remaining delivery channels, exercised end-to-end).
21. TTY-prompt delivery channel exercised via a pty harness (or, if a pty harness proves disproportionate, this bead documents the exclusion in one line and the channel is verified manually per release).

**Un-ignore ownership (nothing stays ignored forever):** U2.3 (olss-charter-qul.3.3) completes scenarios 7–8 and 15; U4.1 (olss-charter-qul.6.1) completes the credential-authenticates tails; U8.3 (olss-charter-qul.10.3) completes scenario 11 (it owns the R41 primitive). Each of those beads is done ONLY when its scenarios run un-ignored. Scenario 18's chain-verification tail completes with U6.3.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test startup` green.

title: U2.1: KTD2 redb proof spike — store-facts + thresholds (Gate G1)
U2.2: store schema — meta/system_keyring/secret_meta/secrets tables, lifecycle stamps (R35/KTD11)
description:
**Build:** The store schema module — one redb file, multiple tables, because KTD8's atomicity demands ONE durability domain.

**Tables (initial set; later units add identities/grants/credentials/rotation/audit through this module):**
- `meta`: store id, format_version, lifecycle state (`ready | reencrypting | restoring | migrating | compacting` — R35), and the wall-clock high-water mark (clock model; updated inside transactions that are durable writes anyway).
- `system_keyring`: the age-encrypted keyring envelope as ONE opaque value — deliberately NOT a sidecar file, so keyring changes commit atomically with their audit events in the same transaction (KTD3/KTD8).
- `secret_meta`: first-class, schema-versioned per-secret metadata record (KTD11): KV custom_metadata, max_versions, cas_required, server fields (last_completed_rotation_at, rotation state, R12 rotation interval — control-plane-writable only, metadata schema version). Separate from version ciphertext so listing/age/status queries NEVER decrypt value blobs. **Also carries a MAC'd version-set summary (KTD15): current version number, max version number, and per-version soft-delete/destroy markers, generation-bumped on EVERY version append/delete/undelete/destroy.** This is load-bearing twice over: it gives U5.2's deletion-state markers (R29) a MAC'd home, and it makes offline deletion of the newest version row digest-visible — without it, an offline editor who deletes the latest `secrets` row silently serves the prior version as "latest" past every integrity check (the rotation-staleness attack the charter's threat model claims to stop).
- `secrets`: keyed by logical path; versioned records (version number, created_time, key id, nonce, ciphertext). **Integrity note:** per-version `created_time` is authenticated via the record AEAD's AAD (U2.4) — it feeds R12 rotation-age math and must not be offline-editable.

**Rules:** `serve` requires lifecycle=ready and refuses schema versions newer than the binary (enforced in U1.3; the stamp lives here). Unknown format_version in meta → open refused. Migrations are forward-only, take a verified backup first, record the transition in the audit log; CI retains a restore-and-migrate fixture for every released schema (R35 — enforcement lands with U10/U12; the version stamp is what makes deferred binary-rollback safe).

**Acceptance:** open/create round-trips; unknown format_version refused; lifecycle stamp readable pre-keyring (it gates startup before decryption — see U2.5's provisional-read rule); high-water-mark field persists; version-set summary MAC verifies and its generation bumps on each append/delete/undelete/destroy.

title: U2.3: age-wrapped fixed-purpose keyring — init generation, boot decrypt-once, store-id binding (KTD3)
description:
**Build:** Keyring lifecycle (KTD3): generation at init, the age envelope, boot-time decrypt-once, store-id binding.

**Contents (compact fixed-purpose keyring — independent random 256-bit symmetric keys, each with a key id):**
- record-encryption key (decrypt-only predecessors retained after rotation),
- credential-verifier MAC key,
- metadata-integrity MAC key (R39/KTD15),
- audit-payload key,
- audit-index key — minted at init like the others (32 random bytes; keeps the keyring format stable) but DORMANT in v0.1: KTD16's index is deferred, the key exists unused so the index later needs no keyring migration (wayfinder T10).
Keyring plaintext also carries: store id, keyring format version, keyring generation, recipient metadata.

**Envelope:** age v0.12-encrypted to the boot-supplied server recipient, optionally plus ONE documented offline recovery recipient. Stored as one opaque value in `system_keyring` (not a sidecar). The age crate is pre-1.0: pin the exact version; decrypt fixtures must survive upgrades.

**Clear keyring-metadata record (created in the same init transaction as the envelope):** keyring generation, keyring format version, recipient fingerprints (non-secret), and last-rewrap audit sequence — KTD15-MAC'd like other clear metadata, and cross-checked against the decrypted envelope's own fields at boot; mismatch → readiness false. Without this record there is nothing MAC'd/digest-visible for "keyring metadata" (named in U2.5's record class list and G2 item 1) to mean: the envelope is opaque, so rewrap rollback detection (U8.1), doctor's keyring-generation report (U12.1), and the state digest all key off THIS record.

**Boot:** open DB → read envelope → decrypt EXACTLY ONCE into `secrecy` types → compare embedded store id with clear store id BEFORE any other record is accepted — a transplanted envelope is rejected before readiness. **Provisional-read closure (mirrors U2.5):** meta fields read before the keyring opens (lifecycle, format_version, store id) are provisional gate inputs; immediately after keyring open, their KTD15 MACs are verified BEFORE readiness — failure → readiness false. The server age identity itself arrives only via approved channels (R21/R37): never disk, shell history, or argv.

**Why a keyring instead of per-blob age (planning-revised, KTD3):** recipient rotation becomes an atomic envelope rewrap instead of a full-store re-encrypt; an offline recovery recipient costs nothing per record; per-record overhead drops; purpose separation keeps token verification, metadata integrity, and audit confidentiality off the secret-encryption key. Recipients stay OUT of the authorization path; age remains the only asymmetric mechanism and the seal boundary — no seal state machine, no KEK/DEK policy hierarchy.

**Wire into init (U1.2):** keyring creation joins init's single transaction.

**Un-ignore ownership:** this bead completes tests/startup.rs scenarios 7–8 and 15 (they run un-ignored before this bead closes).

**Acceptance:** wrong/absent identity → fails closed, no partial plaintext; transplanted envelope rejected on store-id mismatch; keyring opens once and only once per process (assert via instrumentation/test hook); keyring-metadata record MAC verifies and matches envelope fields; post-open provisional-meta re-verification failure → readiness false.

title: U2.4: record AEAD — XChaCha20-Poly1305, canonical binary header/AAD, fresh-nonce discipline (KTD3/R4)
description:
**Build:** Record-level AEAD (KTD3): XChaCha20-Poly1305 (RustCrypto `chacha20poly1305` — NCC-audited; `aes-gcm-siv` noted unaudited in review) with the canonical binary header/AAD encoding.

**Canonical cryptographic encoding — this is half of Gate G2, get it exactly right:** record headers and AEAD associated data use a FIXED binary format: magic bytes, format version, cipher_suite_id, store id, record-domain id, key id, length-prefixed mount and canonical path segments, logical record id, secret version where applicable, and the record's `created_time`. NO JSON, debug formatting, string concatenation, platform endianness, or map iteration order may enter an authenticated encoding (any of those makes authentication depend on accidents).

**Why created_time is authenticated:** it is immutable per version (safe in AAD) and it feeds U7.6's rotation-age computation ("age since creation when never rotated") — left unauthenticated, an offline editor rewrites it to mask an overdue rotation, defeating R12 for exactly the never-rotated secrets the project exists to expose. Tamper vector lands in U2.7 and tests/store.rs.

**Binding:** AAD binds each ciphertext to store id, record type, mount, canonical path, secret version, created_time, and key id — valid ciphertext copied to another path, version, or store FAILS authentication rather than decrypting (R4; threat model: ciphertext substitution).

**Cipher identity:** fixed `cipher_suite_id` in the record header; NOT operator-configurable. Adding another suite is a storage-format migration (R35), never a config toggle.

**Nonce lifecycle:** every AEAD encryption draws a FRESH 192-bit CSPRNG nonce (the extended XChaCha nonce) stored in the record header. Every rewrite and re-encryption draws a new nonce EVEN WHEN PLAINTEXT IS UNCHANGED. Deterministic or counter-derived nonce construction is PROHIBITED everywhere. At 192 bits, random collision is negligible at any plausible scale, so correctness/readiness never depend on a collision-budget counter — the keyring may keep an informational per-key operation count for doctor (KTD3).

**Acceptance:** encrypt/decrypt round-trip; AAD-mismatch (path/version/store/domain/key-id/created_time swap) → authentication failure; deterministic-test-RNG harness proves every encryption path requests a fresh nonce (writes, rewrites, identical-plaintext re-encryption); header decodes identically across supported architectures.

title: U2.5: KTD15 clear-record MACs + readiness-fatal verification (R39)
description:
**Build:** KTD15 authenticated clear-state records (R39).

**What:** every security-relevant clear record — identities, grants, credential metadata, secret metadata (including the version-set summary, U2.2), rotation state, consumer declarations, schema metadata, keyring metadata (the clear record defined in U2.3) — is encoded canonically (fixed binary encoding, same discipline as U2.4) and protected by a keyed MAC under the keyring's metadata-integrity key.
