**MAC domain:** store id, table id, primary key, schema version, record generation (monotonically increasing per record), canonical value bytes. So an offline editor who can WRITE the database but cannot READ the keyring can neither alter a grant nor transplant a valid record to another key or store.

**Fail-closed rule:** reads verify the MAC BEFORE using the record; a MAC failure takes readiness FALSE and prevents data-plane admission — records are NEVER accepted on a warning-only path (R39; Definition of Done names this posture explicitly). **Sole, explicit exception (pre-keyring bootstrap):** meta fields that gate startup before the keyring opens (lifecycle, format_version, store id — U2.2/U1.3) are read as PROVISIONAL inputs; immediately after keyring open their MACs are verified before readiness — failure → readiness false (mirrored in U2.3's boot section). No other record class has a provisional window.

**Runtime MAC-failure operator experience:** the readiness flip emits a stable reason code plus the failing record's table and primary key (both deliberately clear metadata — R25/canary-safe to log); the triggering request receives the stable integrity-failure error envelope; the control plane and doctor remain admissible via the reserved lane so the operator can diagnose; the documented recovery is investigate-or-restore (U10.3). tests/store.rs asserts the record-identifying log line.

**Deletion/rollback detection:** per-record MACs alone cannot detect deletion or replacement with an older, formerly valid record. Checkpoint creation (U6.4) therefore computes a deterministic state digest from one consistent read snapshot and signs it with the audit head — removal or rollback becomes detectable through the anchored sequence; the unanchored tail retains R14's documented live-compromise limitation. **Digest coverage:** sorted (table, key, generation, mac) tuples for MAC'd clear records, PLUS (table, primary key including version, blake3(header ‖ nonce ‖ ciphertext)) tuples for encrypted-record tables from the same snapshot — without the second class, offline deletion of a secret's newest version row is invisible to the digest (the "serve stale credential as latest" rollback; belt-and-suspenders with U2.2's MAC'd version-set summary).

**Digest-vs-key-rotation rule:** the digest is metadata-key-dependent by design (tuples include the MAC). A metadata-integrity-key rotation (U8.3) re-MACs every clear record, so the pass completes ONLY with an immediate `audit checkpoint prepare` + register anchoring the new digest — otherwise doctor/`audit verify --state` sit red against the last checkpoint after a recommended operation (mirrored in U8.3).

**Verification cadence:** per-record MAC on every use; full-state verification in doctor --full, backup verification, restore, migration, and checkpoint preparation.

**Why clear-but-authenticated instead of encrypted (documented offline-theft boundary, R4/KTD11):** logical paths, identity names, grants, version numbers/timestamps stay readable to whoever holds the DB file — chosen over opaque-identifier indirection for v0.1; values, credential material, and audit payloads (which reveal access patterns, not mere topology) stay encrypted. If path/identity topology is itself regulated data, this boundary is disqualifying until a post-v0.1 opaque-identifier option exists.

**Acceptance:** direct DB edit of an identity/grant/credential/secret-metadata record → MAC fails, readiness false, log line names table+key; valid record+MAC copied to another primary key or store → verification fails; generation regression detected by state digest; offline deletion of a secret's newest version row detected by state digest (full tests with U6.4).

title: U2.6: versioned secret records, decrypt-free metadata queries, zeroizing types (R11/KTD11/KTD7)
description:
**Build:** Versioned secret records + per-secret metadata semantics + zeroizing secret types.

**Versioning (R11 storage semantics):** a write appends a new version; both versions retained; versioned read returns each; unversioned read returns latest. Monotonic version numbers; created_time per version; key id + nonce + ciphertext per record. (CAS enforcement lives in the API/coordinator layer — U5/U6; the store provides current-version read + append primitives that make CAS checkable inside the transaction.)

**Metadata (KTD11):** secret_meta is first-class and schema-versioned; metadata queries (versions, ages, custom_metadata, listings) read it WITHOUT touching the record-decrypt path; the store is never batch-decrypted at boot; no plaintext secret cache exists across requests (Performance & Capacity).

**Zeroization (KTD7/R5):** server-owned plaintext — the age identity, the opened keyring, decrypted values before serialization — lives in `SecretBox`/`SecretString` with PRE-SIZED backing so reallocation never leaves stale copies. Zeroize on drop. The guarantee is bounded honestly: copies inside allocator/kernel/HTTP/TLS stacks and the client are excluded by R5's wording; best-effort process-dump hardening is documented separately.

**Acceptance:** write→read round-trip with on-disk bytes being ciphertext (byte-inspect: no plaintext secret substring in the redb file); write twice → both versions readable, unversioned read = latest; metadata queries complete without invoking decrypt (assert via test hook/counter); secret-owning types implement + invoke zeroize-on-drop, prohibit accidental Clone/Debug formatting (compile-time + runtime checks) — without claiming to inspect allocator or kernel copies.

title: U2.7: cross-version crypto fixtures + canonical AAD test vectors (freeze artifacts for G2)
description:
**Build:** Checked-in cross-version crypto fixtures + canonical AAD/header test vectors — the artifacts that FREEZE the encoding across releases (feeds Gate G2).

**Contents:**
- Fixed record-header and AAD test vectors: encode/decode across supported architectures (x86_64, aarch64) byte-identically; altered lengths, domains, paths, versions, key ids, and created_time each REJECT (created_time is authenticated — U2.4/R12).
- Keyring envelope decrypt fixtures pinned to the exact age crate version (pre-1.0: fixtures must survive crate upgrades — an upgrade that breaks decrypt is a release blocker, caught here).
- Clear-record MAC vectors (KTD15 domain tuples), including the secret_meta version-set summary record (U2.2).
- Encrypted-record round-trip fixtures usable by U10 restore tests and R38's prior-release smoke fixture.

**Rule:** once Gate G2 closes over these, any change to an authenticated encoding is an R35 forward-only migration with upgrade notes (R38) — never a silent re-emit of fixtures.

**Acceptance:** vectors committed under tests/fixtures/; cross-arch CI job (or documented local run — the standing aarch64 CI job in U11.8 supersedes the local-run allowance once it exists) proves identical decode; each tamper case rejects; fixtures referenced by G2's freeze review.

title: U2.8: store test suite (tests/store.rs)
description:
**Build:** tests/store.rs — the U2 suite. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a). Scenarios (each a named test):
1. Write→read round-trip; on-disk bytes are ciphertext, not plaintext (byte-inspection of the redb file for plaintext substrings).
2. Write twice → both versions retained; versioned read returns each; unversioned returns latest (R11).
3. Keyring open with wrong/absent identity → fails closed, no partial plaintext.
4. Valid ciphertext copied to different path/version → AAD mismatch → decrypt fails (R4).
5. Deterministic test RNG: every encryption path requests a fresh nonce — writes, rewrites, re-encryption of identical plaintext (KTD3).
6. Fixed header/AAD vectors decode identically across supported architectures; altered lengths/domains/paths/versions/key-ids/created_time reject (KTD3).
7. Direct DB modification of identity/grant/credential/secret-metadata record → MAC fails → readiness false (R39); the failure log line names the offending table + primary key (U2.5's operator experience).
8. Valid clear record + MAC copied to another primary key or store → MAC verification fails (KTD15).
9. Record rolled back to an older valid generation → `audit verify --state` fails against signed checkpoint or reconstructed tail (KTD15 — completes with U6.4; land as ignored/failing until then).
10. Metadata queries (versions, custom_metadata, age inputs) never touch the record-decrypt path (KTD11).
11. Zeroize wrapper contract verified; accidental Clone/Debug prohibited (KTD7) — no claims about allocator/kernel copies (R5 wording).
12. Unknown format_version in meta → open refused (R35).
13. Storage-executor bounded-queue saturation → memory bounded, excess rejected in order, reserved control lane stays live (KTD8 — executor test moved OUT of the KTD2 spike by wayfinder T04; implemented against U6.1's executor, referenced from both suites; land as ignored/failing until U6.1 lands).
14. Keyring envelope from store B installed in store A → rejected on store-id mismatch BEFORE readiness; no record accepted (KTD3 — the U2-era regression test; tests/reencrypt.rs later covers the rewrap-specific variant).
15. Bit-flipped and truncated keyring envelope → fails closed with a distinct error (corruption vs wrong-identity are distinguishable to the operator).
16. Two sequential reads of the same secret → the decrypt path is invoked twice (decrypt-counter hook from scenario 5's instrumentation) — proves no cross-request plaintext cache exists (U2 rule).

**Un-ignore ownership:** U6.6 (olss-charter-qul.4.6) removes the #[ignore] from scenarios 9 and 13 and makes them pass against the real U6.1 executor and U6.4 checkpoint flow — that bead is done only when these run un-ignored.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test store` green; hexdump spot-check recorded.

title: U6.1: bounded blocking storage executor — lanes, backpressure, cancellation (KTD8)
description:
**Build:** The dedicated blocking storage executor (KTD8) — the redb `Database` is owned by ONE named OS thread (initially), NOT by tokio request tasks.

**Protocol:** routers submit typed coordinator commands over a bounded channel and await typed results. The executor serializes write transactions in an explicit order, performs the synchronous database + fsync work, and responds only after commit. redb permits one writer at a time — this design makes that ceiling EXPLICIT instead of blocking async workers on writer acquisition.

**Admission lanes:** separate bounded lanes — a data-plane lane and a SMALL RESERVED control/recovery lane. Queue saturation rejects new data-plane work BEFORE decryption with a stable overload error and bounded aggregate accounting (R13); the reserved lane retains admission for control-plane operations: checkpoint preparation, backup, diagnostics, audit query/export (U6.7 — incident investigation happens exactly when the data plane is failing), reserve release, and orderly shutdown (R27) — recovery must work while the data plane is failing closed.

**Overload-aggregate durability (R13):** saturation rejections accumulate in a bounded in-memory map (bucket count capped; overflow collapses into a catch-all bucket) and are flushed by piggybacking on the next transaction committed through EITHER lane — no timer, no in-daemon scheduler (complexity budget). Counts pending at a crash are lost; this bounded loss window is documented as part of R13's aggregate posture and asserted in U6.6.

**Cancellation semantics (explicit, R26):** cancellation before a command begins prevents execution; once a transaction begins, the coordinator completes or aborts by the TRANSACTION result even if the client disconnects — the audit outcome records committed state, never inferred response delivery. CAS remains the client's protection against ambiguous retries.

**Batching posture:** the protocol is batch-READY (a later bounded micro-batch could commit independently ordered commands in one write transaction) but v0.1 ships with batching DISABLED; any future batching must preserve per-command linearization, audit ordering, response-after-commit, bounded secret retention, and revocation ordering (Performance & Capacity).

**Acceptance:** saturation test — memory bounded, excess rejected in order, reserved lane still serves control work; cancellation-before-begin vs after-begin behave as specified (fault-injection harness); overload aggregates flush on next commit and their crash-loss window is bounded as documented.

title: U6.2: transaction coordinator — atomic state+audit, read-audits-before-transmit (R26/R28)
description:
**Build:** The transaction coordinator — the SINGLE commit boundary owning the R26/R28 invariants. Built FIRST in this unit; everything else composes onto it.

**Mutation path:** KV write/delete/destroy, identity/grant/credential/rotation lifecycle, and keyring-envelope changes commit the state change AND its audit entry in ONE multi-table write transaction; the response is sent only after commit. Neither becomes visible without the other (AE9).

**Read path:** revalidate the token and CURRENT grants inside the transaction boundary (this is what makes R28 hold without an invalidation bus — tokens are capability-thin), decrypt, prepare the bounded response, append the read audit entry (with served version), COMMIT, and only then transmit. If the audit commit fails, the client receives an error and NO secret material (AE10). The audited outcome for a read means "authorized and response prepared" — no server can attest the client received the bytes.

**Rejected alternatives (do not reintroduce):** a separate audit file, an async audit queue, best-effort dual-writes — each reintroduces the crash window between state and record that R26 exists to close.

**Boundary enforcement:** no handler, middleware, background task, or application service owns a raw database handle or a standalone audit writer — module visibility (pub(crate) discipline) makes the coordinator the ONLY commit path. The Definition of Done requires this to be structurally true, and the crash suite proves the atomicity.

**Linearization (R28/KTD8):** identity disable, grant reduction, credential revocation, and authorized requests linearize through the storage transaction boundary — any request whose authorization transaction begins after an administrative change commits observes the new state, independent of token TTL; an already-linearized in-flight request may complete. Every resulting rejection is audited.

**Acceptance:** typed command API compiles handlers against services only; a leaked-handle attempt fails to compile (visibility test); AE9/AE10 kill-point + fault-injection tests land in U6.6.

title: U6.3: audit events — canonical schema, encrypted payloads, blake3 chain (R13/R25/KTD4)
description:
**Build:** The audit event model + hash chain (R13/R25/KTD4).

**One externally visible operation = ONE canonical, schema-versioned audit event** containing nested authentication and authorization results — never an unbounded number of rows for internal matcher steps. Fields: server-generated event id, request id, audit schema version, identity, public credential accessor (never the secret), optional stable consumer-instance id (KTD5), auth method, canonical resource, requested capability, operation, outcome, reason code, effective time, wall-clock observation, and — for successful secret reads/writes — the version served or written (this field is what makes R33 rotation adoption observable). NEVER secret values or credential material.

**R25 (global output ban):** secret values and credential material appear in NO server output — audit entries, app/access logs, traces, metrics labels, error responses, panic output, crash diagnostics, header/body echoes. Secret paths and identity names are additionally banned from metrics labels. Enforced here structurally (typed redaction at the event/serialization layer) and attacked by U11's canary scan.

**Chain (KTD4):** per-entry blake3 hash chain over ENCRYPTED payloads: entry payloads encrypt under the keyring's audit-payload key (paths and identity names reveal topology — confidential at rest, readable only under audit capabilities R22); the plaintext envelope carries only **audit-epoch id, epoch-scoped sequence, timestamp, previous hash, and ciphertext digest** — so the chain VERIFIES WITHOUT DECRYPTING. **Epoch semantics (the KTD17 hook, decided NOW so G2 freezes it):** sequence is scoped to the audit epoch; each epoch opens with a genesis entry that binds the previous epoch's terminal chain head, so `audit verify` can distinguish two chains with overlapping sequence ranges after a recovery fork (U10.4) without a schema change. **Envelope timestamp domain:** the envelope timestamp is EFFECTIVE TIME from U1.5's high-water clock (monotone, never regresses — this is what makes envelope-level window pruning sound for R23 scans and U6.7's time-range filters); the wall-clock observation lives only inside the encrypted payload. This is the Certificate-Transparency signed-tree-head pattern reduced to single-node scale; full Merkle inclusion proofs would be over-building.

**Flood accounting (R13):** requests dropped by the pre-verifier rate limiter aggregate into bounded entries (source bucket, count, window) — an unauthenticated attacker cannot fill the disk one audit row at a time.

**Scan path (KTD16 deferred):** status/observation queries scan primary events inside R23's lookback window; the scan is the ONLY query path and primary events are ALWAYS authoritative — status can never be wrong for index reasons.

**Audit genesis:** wire the genesis event into U1.2's init transaction.

**G2 freeze artifacts (this bead produces them):** canonical audit-event encoding vectors and chain-envelope fixtures — including tamper/reject cases and a schema-version round-trip — committed under tests/fixtures/; named in Gate G2's exit criteria (item 3 has nothing to bind without them).

**Acceptance:** event round-trip with schema version; chain verifies without decrypt; epoch-genesis entry binds the prior terminal head (unit-level; full fork coverage in U10); envelope timestamps are non-regressing under a wall-clock rollback test; canary strings absent from serialized events/errors/panics; genesis lands in init; encoding vectors + envelope fixtures committed.

title: U6.4: external ed25519 checkpoints + state digest + audit verify (KTD4/KTD15/R14)
description:
**Build:** Externally signed checkpoints (KTD4) + state digest (KTD15) + `audit verify`.

**Trust design:** the daemon stores only checkpoint PUBLIC keys and key-lineage metadata — never the private key — and has NO checkpoint scheduler (complexity budget: no in-daemon timers). A systemd timer or the operator invokes the CLI. This keeps encryption-recovery and audit-signing authority as distinct trust roles and removes the signing key from the long-running process: a live attacker can fabricate events and misreport chain heads only until an external checkpoint anchors them (R14's documented exclusion — detection of offline/post-checkpoint tampering, not prevention of live forgery).

**Flow:**
1. `audit checkpoint prepare` — commits an audited canonical descriptor: store id, audit epoch, sequence range, chain head, KTD15 state digest, timestamp, signing-key id, previous-checkpoint digest. **Prepare-boundary rule (no off-by-one):** the prepare event itself is the LAST entry of the anchored range — descriptor and its audit event commit in one transaction, and the descriptor's chain head is computed INCLUDING that entry (otherwise every checkpoint leaves an eternally unanchored prepare event and two implementers disagree at every boundary). `previous_checkpoint_digest` always references the last REGISTERED checkpoint; a prepared descriptor abandoned by a crash is superseded by the next prepare and reported by doctor until superseded.
2. The local CLI loads the private signing key from an approved credential channel (R37) for the duration of the operation, signs (ed25519-dalek), fsyncs the checkpoint file, zeroizes the key.
3. `audit checkpoint register` — verifies the signature against the stored public key and records the checkpoint digest.
A crash between steps leaves an unsigned prepared descriptor or a signed-but-unregistered file — both recoverable, both visible to doctor. Checkpoints form their own signed chain via previous_checkpoint_digest; signing-key rotation retains the old public key and records a trust transition, so old checkpoints never become unverifiable. Backup manifests (U10) reuse this signing flow under a DISTINCT domain-separation label.

**Descriptor + digest encodings are G2-frozen:** both are cross-release verifiable formats (R14 requires old checkpoint files to re-verify after upgrades); this bead commits canonical descriptor-encoding and state-digest-tuple vectors under tests/fixtures/, named in Gate G2's exit criteria.

**State digest (KTD15):** deterministic digest from one consistent read snapshot, signed together with the audit head — sorted (table, key, generation, mac) tuples for MAC'd clear records PLUS (table, primary key incl. version, blake3(header ‖ nonce ‖ ciphertext)) tuples for encrypted-record tables (U2.5's coverage rule) — catches deletion/rollback of clear records AND offline removal of secret versions, which per-record MACs cannot.

**Verify:** `audit verify [--checkpoint <path>] [--state]` — checks the chain against the last registered checkpoint, verifies the anchored state digest, reconciles post-checkpoint mutations from the audit tail, and reports R14's two v0.1 tiers: fully inspected events anchored by a retained checkpoint, and the unanchored active tail. Given divergent continuations of the same chain prefix (post-restore split brain), verify names them as DISTINCT RESTORE INCARNATIONS via U10.3's incarnation marker and reports the divergence point — never a generic break. Off-host mirroring is operator-owned (rsync/cron/systemd path unit — documented in U12); the server speaks no S3/webhooks.

**Staleness posture (R36):** checkpoint staleness is a WARNING and a rotation-closeout limitation — not by itself a data-plane readiness failure (an off-host mirror outage must not take secret reads down); invalid state MACs, broken audit writes, exhausted capacity remain readiness failures. **Staleness predicate (defined HERE; U7.5's closeout guard and doctor consume exactly this):** stale ⇔ age of the last REGISTERED checkpoint (effective time) exceeds `checkpoint.max_age` OR the unanchored tail exceeds `checkpoint.max_unanchored_events`; both config keys documented with defaults; exposed as a stable field in health/doctor output.

**Acceptance:** prepare/sign/register round-trip; crash between steps recoverable + doctor-visible; next successful checkpoint's previous digest references the last REGISTERED checkpoint after an abandoned prepare; verify reports both tiers; re-chaining detected against a registered checkpoint; state-digest rollback detection (with U2.5 scenario 9) including deleted-newest-version detection; staleness predicate flips exactly at the configured thresholds; descriptor/digest vectors committed.

title: U6.5: capacity thresholds + recovery.reserve fail-closed guardrails (R27)
description:
**Build:** Capacity guardrails (R27): audit-log and disk capacity monitoring with WARNING and STOP-ADMITTING thresholds, plus the preallocated recovery reserve.

**Fail-closed before exhaustion:** the data plane stops admitting before storage exhaustion — a documented response, not a crash. In v0.1 every audit event stays in the active database (no segmentation/pruning — deferred post-v0.1); growth at small-team rates is a few hundred MB/year, so thresholds fire long before physical limits, but the guard must exist because R13 forbids silently discarding events.

**The recovery allowance is CONCRETE:** `init` (U1.2) preallocates a same-filesystem `recovery.reserve` file sized above the documented worst-case TOTAL of: a checkpoint prepare+register pair, a backup manifest transaction, diagnostics, orderly shutdown, one reserve-release operation, and a documented allowance of N audited incident-investigation invocations (audit query/export — U6.7 requires them to work exactly then, and every invocation is itself audited, i.e. consumes bytes) at worst-case event size. The data plane stops admitting BEFORE consuming it; checkpoint export, backup, audit query/export, and orderly shutdown remain possible after the data plane has failed closed (via KTD8's reserved control lane). Releasing or recreating the reserve is a local, AUDITED recovery operation — ordinary request handling cannot spend it.

**In-flight headroom rule (the guarantee is only real with it):** the stop-admitting threshold is evaluated at ADMISSION and includes headroom for worst-case queued consumption — data-plane lane depth × documented maximum transaction size (numbers from U2.1 store-facts / U5.5 bounds). Without this, N already-admitted commands pass the check and then write through the threshold into the reserve — voiding fail-closed at exactly the margin it exists for.

**Acceptance:** fill the active audit area to the stop-admitting threshold → data-plane admission stops while checkpoint registration, diagnostics, audit query/export, and orderly shutdown still succeed via the reserved lane + reserve file; saturate the data lane exactly at the threshold boundary → all queued commands complete WITHOUT touching recovery.reserve (headroom rule); warning threshold surfaces in health/doctor before the stop threshold; reserve release requires the audited local operation. Feeds milestone M2 (disk-reserve behavior).

title: U6.6: audit + crash test suites (tests/audit.rs, tests/fault/txn_crash.rs — AE5/AE9/AE10)
description:
**Build:** tests/audit.rs + tests/fault/txn_crash.rs — the coordinator/audit suites. Test-first: this log is the sole record a rotation trusts; the coordinator is the invariant everything leans on. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. AE9 crash atomicity: kill the process at injected points around a secret write AND around a token revocation → after restart, state change and audit entry exist together or not at all.
2. AE5 tamper: edit or remove an entry on disk → verification fails and identifies where the chain breaks.
3. Re-chaining attack: rewrite an entry and recompute ALL subsequent hashes → verification against the exported checkpoint still fails; truncation before the checkpoint detected; truncation after it reported as unanchored-tail loss, never silently passed (R14).
4. AE10: fault-injected audit-commit failure → the triggering read returns an error with NO secret bytes; the mutation does not commit (R26).
5. R25 canary: a secret value passed through a request appears in NO audit entry, error response, or panic output; audit payloads in the DB file are ciphertext (byte scan for canary strings).
6. Failed-auth flood → bounded aggregate audit volume, not one row per packet (R13); overload aggregates flush by piggybacking on the next commit and the crash-loss window is bounded as documented (U6.1).
7. R27 capacity: audit store approaches capacity → warning then fail-closed before exhaustion, checkpoint export + shutdown still possible from the reserve; lane saturated exactly at the stop threshold → queued commands complete without touching recovery.reserve (U6.5's headroom rule).
8. Status-scan anchor: R23-window scan over a SYNTHETIC CORPUS representing YEARS of small-team events returns correct window membership and served-version fields within operator-command latency tolerance — this measurement is the recorded anchor for KTD16's upgrade trigger (R33/R23). (On-current/on-prior/silent-since-write BUCKET correctness is U7.4's logic, asserted in tests/rotation.rs against this same corpus generator — not re-implemented here.)
9. Executor saturation: bounded memory, orderly rejection, reserved control lane serves backup/checkpoint/shutdown (KTD8 — moved from the KTD2 spike, wayfinder T04; shared with U2.8 scenario 13).
10. Cancellation semantics (KTD8/R26 — U6.1's explicit contract, attacked not assumed): client cancel/disconnect BEFORE the command begins → command never executes, no state change, no audit entry; cancel/disconnect AFTER the transaction begins → outcome follows the TRANSACTION result (commit or abort) regardless of the vanished client, and the audit outcome records committed state — never inferred response delivery.
11. Checkpoint lifecycle faults: kill between prepare/sign and between sign/register → recoverable, surfaced in status/doctor output; next successful checkpoint's previous-checkpoint digest references the last REGISTERED checkpoint (abandoned prepare superseded); duplicate register is idempotent-or-rejected and audited; `audit checkpoint register` with a wrong-key signature, or with a stale/forked checkpoint that does not extend previous_checkpoint_digest → each refused and audited.
12. Signing-key rotation: register under a rotated key → old checkpoints still verify via retained lineage; a checkpoint signed by an unregistered key is refused; a tampered audit-export bundle (flipped envelope byte, swapped payload member) fails offline verification naming the member (with U6.7).
13. R28 linearization race: commit an identity disable / grant reduction / credential revocation while N reads are in flight at the coordinator (test-double identity state — no token stack required) → every request whose authorization transaction began after the admin commit is rejected under the new state, each rejection audited; already-linearized requests complete; no interleaving exists where a post-commit-authorized response reflects pre-commit grants.

**Un-ignore ownership:** this bead removes the #[ignore] from tests/store.rs scenarios 9 and 13 and makes them pass against the real U6.1 executor and U6.4 checkpoint flow.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test audit --test txn_crash` green; `<binary> audit verify` detects a hand-tampered log.

title: U3.1: RawTargetGuard + canonical Resource parser — one module, one corpus (KTD9/R8)
description:
**Build:** `RawTargetGuard` + the canonical `Resource` parser — ONE module, ONE test corpus (KTD9/R8). This is the security joint most Vault-alike servers get wrong.

**Why the guard runs FIRST:** axum's `Path` extractor percent-decodes before handlers run, so validation after extraction cannot distinguish a literal separator from an encoded alias (`a%2Fb` vs `a/b`). The outer guard validates `Request::uri()` — the RAW request target — before any route extraction or percent-decoding.

**The guard REJECTS (never normalizes):** malformed escapes; percent-encoded separators and backslashes (%2f, %2F, %5c); encoded percent signs enabling a second decode (%25); encoded or literal dot segments; empty MIDDLE segments and repeated separators (R8's precise rule — see edge cases below); control characters; NUL; overlong targets; duplicate security-sensitive query parameters; noncanonical alternate encodings; invalid UTF-8. Then it performs EXACTLY ONE decode and constructs an `EndpointRequest` carrying the endpoint kind and canonical `Resource {mount, canonical_segments}`.

**Fixture-governed edge cases (do NOT decide by fiat):** a trailing slash on list-form endpoints and the zero-segment mount-root list are accepted or rejected exactly per U0's captured pinned-client fixtures — `vault`/`bao` list operations commonly address `.../metadata/<path>/`, and a blanket reject-all-empty-segments rule would break legitimate captured traffic while a blanket accept would widen the surface. The U0.2 path-encoding matrix is the evidence; the corpus includes both forms either way.

**Contract:** the typed request goes into request extensions and is the ONLY resource representation available to authorization and handlers. Handlers NEVER reconstruct a resource from a framework-decoded path value. The project uses NO path-normalization middleware. One parser converts every endpoint form — data/, metadata/, list, delete, undelete, destroy, ?version=N — to the canonical resource exactly once. Segments compare byte-for-byte after one validated UTF-8 decode — NO Unicode normalization. The supported segment-character contract is published and frozen by U0/U11 evidence.

**Grants shape (consumed by the matcher bead):** {mount, exact_or_subtree, prefix_segments, capabilities} — subtree matches are SEGMENT-prefix, so a grant on apps/foo never reaches apps/foobar.

**Acceptance:** raw corpus — %2f/%2F/%5c/%25/%2e, malformed sequences, repeated separators, double-encoded forms — ALL rejected before route dispatch; noncanonical encodings of an in-grant path rejected rather than authorized as an alias; trailing-slash list form and zero-segment mount-root list behave exactly per captured fixtures; property test: all endpoint forms of one logical path map to one resource.

title: U3.2: identities (kind), grants, closed capability enum, structured matcher (R7/R22/KTD10)
description:
**Build:** Identity records, grant records, the closed capability enum, and the authorization matcher with structured decisions.

**Identities (R7):** every human, application, integration is a distinct identity; `kind` field (human | workload, extensible — schema carries it from day one so phase-3 AI agents land without a data migration; v0.1 attaches NO kind-specific enforcement, display/audit only). Persisted as KTD15 MAC-authenticated clear records. Wire first-management-identity creation into U1.2's init transaction — **together with its BOOTSTRAP GRANT: the admin bundle alias over all management capabilities, committed atomically with the identity and U4.1's credential verifier in init's single transaction.** Without the grant, the fail-closed model means the bootstrap credential authenticates but is denied everything — first boot could not run `identity create` or `grant add` (F1 bricked). Acceptance: fresh init → the bootstrap credential can execute `identity create` and `grant add`.

**Capabilities (R22) — closed, versioned enum, NO policy language:**
- Secret: read-current, read-history, list, write, soft-delete/undelete, destroy, destroy-all (full purge, local control plane only). Reading history or destroying is NEVER implied by read-current or write. **Operation definitions are by REQUEST SHAPE (stable under concurrent writes):** read-current = the unversioned latest read; read-history = ANY explicit `?version=N` read, INCLUDING N equal to the current version — whether N is current is mutable state and must not flip the required capability.
- Management (each distinct): identity+grant management, credential issue/revoke, rotation management, consumer enumeration, audit read/export, backup, key rotation, diagnostics — an audit-pulling integration needs no power to mint credentials.
- Built-in bundles (admin, auditor) are convenience ALIASES over the fixed set.
- Audit records are confidential at rest; query/export/verify require the audit capabilities.

**Grants (R8):** {mount, exact_or_subtree, prefix_segments, capabilities} — exact-path or segment-prefix subtree only. No globs, regex, deny rules. **Management capabilities use the SAME grant shape via a reserved synthetic management mount** (mount=`sys`, exact match, empty prefix_segments — never a KV mount): the matcher maps each control-plane command to a (management resource, capability) pair, so ONE matcher serves both planes and no second authorization model exists (charter rule 6). `grant add` REJECTS cross-plane shapes: secret capabilities on the management mount, management capabilities on a KV mount.

**Matcher (KTD10):** returns {allow, resource, operation, matched_grant | deny_reason} — never a bare boolean. Hot-path handlers consume `allow` only; the full decision feeds the audit entry and a management-gated `authz explain` control-plane command; NEVER echoed to unauthorized clients (R25). Deny reasons discriminate prefix-boundary miss from missing capability. A seam, not a policy debugger.

**Acceptance:** AE6 shape — subtree grant on apps/canvas denies apps/populi/api-key via data, metadata, AND list forms; capability separations hold (read-current ≠ ?version=N — including ?version=<current>; write ≠ destroy; audit-only cannot mint credentials); management-mount grants authorize control commands and cross-plane grant shapes are rejected at `grant add`; fresh-init bootstrap credential can create identities and grants; decisions carry the right deny_reason.

title: U3.3: capability-thin tokens — TTL, revocation, per-request grant reload (R9/R28)
U3.4: strict input hygiene — duplicate-key-rejecting JSON, dup query/header refusal (KTD9)
description:
**Build:** Strict input hygiene at the deserialization boundary (KTD9 extension).

**Rules:**
- JSON request bodies use a DUPLICATE-KEY-REJECTING deserializer: duplicate `cas`, `data`, `versions`, `role_id`, or `secret_id` keys are ERRORS — never last-key-wins (duplicate-key smuggling is how proxies/WAFs and servers get made to disagree about the same request).
- Duplicate `version` query parameters → rejected.
- Multiple `X-Vault-Token` headers → rejected BEFORE authentication.
- Rejections happen before any state lookup or mutation.

**Why:** the authorization boundary is only as strong as the agreement between what was parsed and what was checked. Every ambiguity is resolved by refusal, matching the raw-target guard's philosophy: reject, never normalize.

**Acceptance:** duplicate JSON keys / duplicate version params / multiple token headers each rejected pre-state-access with distinct error reasons; corpus shared with U3.1's module so the two validators cannot drift apart.

title: U3.5: identity/authz test suite — property tests + raw corpus (tests/identity.rs, AE6)
description:
**Build:** tests/identity.rs — the authorization suite. Test-first: an authz bug is a full-store disclosure. Property tests mandatory. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. AE6: identity with subtree grant on apps/canvas requests apps/populi/api-key via data, metadata, and list forms → each denied AND audited; ambiguous encodings (encoded slashes, dot segments, doubled separators) of paths inside AND outside the grant → rejected outright, never authorized as a different resource.
2. Property test: all endpoint forms of the same logical path authorize as the SAME resource; ambiguous encodings (encoded slash, dot segments, doubled separators, control bytes, invalid UTF-8, double-decode) rejected BEFORE grant evaluation.
3. Raw HTTP corpus: %2f, %2F, %5c, %25, %2e, malformed percent sequences, repeated separators, double-encoded forms → all rejected before route dispatch (KTD9); trailing-slash list form and zero-segment mount-root list behave exactly per U0's captured fixtures (both directions covered by the corpus).
4. Noncanonical percent encoding of a granted path → rejected, not aliased.
5. Duplicate JSON keys / duplicate version query params → rejected before any state lookup (KTD9).
6. Prefix-boundary: grant on apps/foo does NOT reach apps/foobar (segment boundaries, not string prefixes).
7. Revoke token before TTL → next request rejected (feeds AE2).
8. Scope reduction with live token → removed path denied on next request; no TTL wait; broad-scope token does not retain scope (R28).
9. read-current does not grant ?version=N — for ANY N, including N equal to the current version (request-shape rule, U3.2); ?version=<current> with read-history → allowed; write does not grant destroy (R22).
10. Read/write grant cannot perform management ops; audit read and consumer enumeration each need their own capability; audit-only identity cannot issue credentials (R22).
11. Deny decisions discriminate prefix-boundary miss from missing capability (KTD10).
12. Expired token → rejected by effective time; denial audited (token-lifecycle acceptance from U3.3, proven here).
13. Identity disable → ALL its outstanding tokens rejected at the next linearized request; each rejection audited (R28).
14. Wall-clock rollback after issuance → token still expires on schedule by effective time (U1.5 high-water mark); no TTL extension or resurrection — the charter's clock-rollback protection, asserted at the token layer.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test identity` green, property tests included. The corpus feeds U11's fuzz targets and the Verification Contract's raw-target-parser gate.

title: U4.1: credential format, keyed verifiers, accessor index, audience+epoch domains (KTD5/R24)
description:
**Build:** Credential encoding + keyed verifiers + accessor index + audience/epoch domains (KTD5/R24). The foundation both listeners authenticate against.

**Format:** tokens and AppRole secret_ids are `<kind>.<audience>.<accessor>.<secret>` in a canonical fixed-length base64url-no-padding encoding — NONCANONICAL ENCODINGS ARE REJECTED (no alternate padding/case aliases).

**Verification path:** the public accessor selects EXACTLY ONE record in O(1); the server-generated 256-bit random secret verifies with a keyed blake3 MAC (key = credential-verifier key from the keyring, U2.3) under CONSTANT-TIME comparison. Unknown accessors run the SAME MAC path against a fixed dummy record with the parsed kind/audience and the store's CURRENT epoch (the wire format carries no epoch — the dummy record must not need one) — timing does not reveal existence.

**Epoch semantics (R32/R41 — the MAC alone does not revoke):** the current credential epoch is a KTD15-MAC'd meta record. An epoch-3 credential still MACs correctly against its intact epoch-3 verifier record after a bump to epoch 4 — revocation therefore requires the separate check record.issue_epoch == current_epoch. Verification ALWAYS computes the MAC (constant-time, even on epoch mismatch) and performs the epoch read + comparison INSIDE the request's coordinator transaction, so epoch bumps linearize with in-flight requests (no middleware-verify TOCTOU; U4.4 scenario 10 asserts "every request linearized after the increment rejects every old token").

**MAC domain (R24):** store id, credential kind, listener audience, accessor, issue epoch, secret bytes. Consequences, each load-bearing: a token cannot be reinterpreted as a secret_id (kind); cannot be used on the wrong listener (audience: `control` vs `data` — the Vault listener accepts only data-audience, the control socket only control-audience); cannot be replayed into another store (store id); cannot survive an epoch change (issue epoch — the R32/R41 primitive).

**NO PASSWORD KDF ANYWHERE:** Argon2 deliberately dropped — it is for low-entropy human secrets and this system has none; on the per-request token path it would turn every `vault kv get` into a CPU burn and a DoS amplifier. It returns only if a human-chosen passphrase ever enters the product. Token verification is O(1) keyed hash — authentication is never the throughput ceiling.

**Storage:** only accessor + verifier + metadata persist (KTD15 MAC'd); raw secrets disclosed once at creation, never persisted/logged/recoverable (R24). Verifiers support revocation and rotation. Credentials may carry an optional stable `consumer_instance_id` (KTD5): workload secret_ids normally REQUIRE one (operator may explicitly accept identity-only tracking); tokens minted from a secret_id INHERIT it; non-secret, MAC-authenticated, recorded in audit events (feeds R33 instance-granular adoption).

**Wire into init (U1.2):** the first management credential (audience control, short bootstrap TTL) is generated here with R17's disclosure-before-commit ordering; the data-plane authenticator rejects it CRYPTOGRAPHICALLY (audience) before grant evaluation — it cannot be converted into a data-plane token.

**Un-ignore ownership:** completes tests/startup.rs credential-authenticates tails (scenarios 7/8 tails).

**Acceptance:** noncanonical encodings rejected; dummy-verifier timing parity (statistical test); audience cross-use rejected on both listeners; KIND cross-use rejected (a valid secret_id presented as a token, and vice versa, fails before authorization — MAC domain separation demonstrated); DB-only attacker cannot verify a guessed credential (MAC key lives in keyring); epoch mismatch fails with the MAC still computed (timing parity across epoch-valid/epoch-stale).

title: U4.2: token middleware + AppRole login + lookup-self + renew-self unsupported (R31)
description:
**Build:** The Vault-compatible auth endpoints (R31) — the ENTIRE supported v0.1 auth surface, nothing more.

**Pieces:**
- `X-Vault-Token` extraction as tower-http middleware → accessor parse → U4.1 verification → identity resolution. Multiple token headers already rejected by U3.4 (explicit dependency — the hygiene layer must exist first).
- `POST /v1/auth/approle/login`: accepts {role_id, secret_id} via U3.4's duplicate-key-rejecting deserializer; returns the Vault wire shape {"auth":{"client_token","lease_duration","renewable":false,...}} — byte-shape asserted against U0's captured fixtures (execution note: START from that failing test). Issues a TTL-bound data-audience token (Flow F3: workload boots with no static key on disk). **Role binding (role_id is not decorative):** login resolves role_id to an existing role AND verifies the presented secret_id was issued under that role; a mismatch, or a secret_id whose role was deleted, returns the SAME 400-class error as an invalid secret_id (no existence oracle) and is audited. **Token TTL source:** the issued token's TTL comes from the role's token_ttl (set at `approle role create` — U4.5), falling back to a bounded server default; login never mints an unbounded token.
- secret_ids carry TTL and bounded use counts, decremented ATOMICALLY with token issuance and the audit entry (one coordinator transaction) — a one-use secret_id under concurrent login succeeds exactly once.
- `GET /v1/auth/token/lookup-self`: identity, TTL remaining, creation time — NEVER credential material.
- `renew-self`: returns R3's explicit unsupported error. v0.1 tokens are non-renewable; login responses advertise renewable:false; workloads re-login via AppRole. (Keeps the token model thin; renewal is a whole lease subsystem Vault needed and this product refuses.)
- Errors use the Vault {"errors":[...]} shape; invalid secret_id → 400-class + audited attempt.

**No bootstrap route exists on any network listener** — F1's first credential is U1.2's local init.

**Acceptance:** F3 flow green (valid role_id+secret_id → token → scoped read); envelope byte-shape matches fixtures; one-use semantics atomic under concurrency; valid secret_id + WRONG role_id rejected identically to invalid secret_id; token TTL equals the role's token_ttl; lookup-self reports TTL; renew-self → explicit unsupported (R31).

title: U4.3: unauthenticated-surface rate/size limits + bounded aggregate audit (R27/R13)
description:
**Build:** Unauthenticated-surface protection (R27) on the auth router — this is where the unauthenticated login surface lives. (The unauthenticated set also includes sys/health and sys/seal-status — U5.3 routes them into these same buckets.)

**Limits (tower-http middleware):**
- request-size limits before the handler;
- attempt-rate limits with BOUNDED limiter state — no per-attacker-address permanent allocation (an attacker rotating source addresses must not grow memory without bound);
- authenticated surfaces get request-size limits, a global concurrency cap, and per-identity rate limits sufficient to bound decrypt-plus-audit write amplification from a stolen token or a looping client (transport-level caps land with U9.2; the per-identity dimension lands here where identity is known).

**Limiter keying (mode-dependent — a wrong key defeats the limiter):** in DIRECT mode the limiter keys on the transport peer address and IGNORES forwarding headers (trusting X-Forwarded-For in direct mode lets an attacker forge unlimited fresh buckets). In U9.3's explicit reverse-proxy mode it keys on the proxy-supplied client address — trusted only in that mode, from the verified proxy peer. Proxy mode WITHOUT forwarded-address configuration degrades to one shared bucket (every client behind the proxy shares fate) — documented loudly, because a login flood then rate-limits legitimate workload re-logins. Wording coordinated with U9.3's peer-verification rules.

**Audit coupling (R13):** requests dropped by the pre-verifier rate limiter are accounted in bounded AGGREGATE entries (source bucket, count, window) — never one durable row per flood packet, so an unauthenticated attacker cannot fill the disk one audit entry at a time.

**Acceptance:** flood of failed auth attempts → rate limit engages, limiter state bounded (memory assertion), aggregate audit entries present (R13); oversized request body rejected before the handler; per-identity limit bounds a looping authenticated client; direct mode ignores forwarded headers (forged X-Forwarded-For does not mint fresh buckets); proxy mode keys on the proxy-supplied client address.

title: U4.4: auth test suite (tests/auth.rs — envelopes vs fixtures, audience, epoch, flood)
description:
**Build:** tests/auth.rs — the auth suite. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. F3: valid role_id + secret_id → TTL-bound token in the Vault auth.client_token envelope; token then reads a scoped path.
2. Invalid secret_id → 400-class {"errors":[...]}; attempt audited.
3. Token lookup touches exactly ONE credential record (accessor index); unknown and known-but-wrong accessors run the SAME verifier work (timing parity); no code path invokes a password KDF (KTD5 — assert by dependency absence + code audit hook).
4. One-use secret_id succeeds exactly once under concurrent login; decrement + token issuance + audit commit atomically.
5. Expired/revoked token on a KV request → rejected (feeds AE2); lookup-self on valid token reports TTL remaining; renew-self → explicit unsupported (R31).
6. Raw secret_id never returned after creation, never in the store as plaintext (keyed verifier only); the database alone cannot verify a guessed credential (R24 — MAC key in keyring).
7. R27: failed-auth flood → rate limit engages with bounded limiter state and aggregate audit entries; oversized body rejected pre-handler.
8. Valid CONTROL-audience token on the data listener → rejected before authorization; valid DATA-audience token on the control socket → rejected (R24/R31).
9. Credential copied into another store → verifier failure (R24 store-id domain).
10. Epoch increment under concurrent requests → every request linearized after the increment rejects every old token and secret_id (R41/R28).
11. Control request from a disallowed Unix peer uid → rejected and audited (R34, with U1.4).
12. KIND cross-use: a valid secret_id presented as X-Vault-Token → rejected before authorization; a valid token presented as the secret_id in AppRole login → the standard invalid-credential error; both audited, timing parity preserved (R24 kind domain — the one MAC axis scenarios 8–10 don't cover).
13. Role binding: valid secret_id + WRONG role_id → rejected identically to an invalid secret_id; valid secret_id after `approle role delete` → rejected; both audited (U4.2's binding rule).

**Verification:** `cargo test --test auth` green; envelope shapes asserted against captured Vault responses (U0 fixtures).

title: U9.1: rustls TLS binding + certificate reload without restart (KTD1 adoption gate, R36)
description:
**Build:** rustls TLS serving via `axum-server` `bind_rustls` with PEM cert/key from config, reloadable WITHOUT restart. **Reload triggers: BOTH SIGHUP and a control-plane `tls reload` command** (the command form rides the owner-only socket — U1.4 dependency; systemd/certbot hooks use the signal, humans use the command and get a structured result).

**Why reload matters (R36):** a restart is expensive while key supply is operator-attended — the age identity arrives via stdin/FD/TTY/credentials-dir at boot, so bouncing the process for a cert renewal would demand a key ceremony. Certificate reload must swap the served cert with no restart and no dropped in-flight request.

**Reload failure is atomic (the worst outage mode is a reload that fails open-ended):** an unparsable PEM, a key/cert mismatch, or a half-written file is rejected WHOLE — the previously served cert continues serving untouched, the error is surfaced to the operator (command result / log naming the file and parse error), and no partial swap is possible. For a server whose restart costs an attended key ceremony, taking the listener down on a bad reload is unacceptable.

**KTD1 adoption gate:** `axum-server` is an independent community-maintained TLS adapter (NOT Tokio-team, unlike axum itself) and is treated as such in dependency review. It is ADOPTED only after this bead proves TLS reload and graceful shutdown work; if it cannot, escalate — the fallback is hand-rolling the accept loop over tokio-rustls. Record the verdict here. (Unix-listener support for the control plane is axum's own `axum::serve` — not axum-server's.)

**Stack choice rationale:** fnox already depends on rustls — one TLS stack across client and server (Dependencies/Assumptions).

**Acceptance:** HTTPS request with X-Vault-Token succeeds; plaintext HTTP to the TLS port rejected; cert reload swaps cert with zero dropped in-flight requests (test drives concurrent load through the swap); reload with an unparsable or mismatched cert/key pair is rejected atomically — the old cert keeps serving and the error is surfaced (tests/transport.rs scenario 3b); both reload triggers work; adapter verdict recorded.

title: U9.2: middleware stack — security-significant ordering, bounds, sensitive headers (KTD9/R25/R27)
description:
**Build:** The tower-http middleware stack with SECURITY-SIGNIFICANT ordering, covered by an integration test.

**Order (outermost → innermost):** raw-target guard (U3.1) → header-count/size bounds → sensitive-header marking → tracing/request-id → rate/concurrency limits → authentication → handler.

**Authentication is ROUTE-SCOPED, not blanket:** the explicit unauthenticated set — AppRole login (U4.2), sys/health, sys/seal-status (U5.3) — bypasses token verification but remains INSIDE the guard/bounds/limit layers (they join U4.3's unauthenticated rate/size buckets). A blanket token gate would break `vault status` and monitoring probes; an unauthenticated route escaping the guard or bounds would be an unprotected surface. Everything else requires a verified token before the handler.

**Why the order is load-bearing:**
- The raw-target guard runs before ANYTHING logs or routes — a request rejected by the guard must never reach tracing with an unmasked token.
- `X-Vault-Token` (and any future credential-bearing header) is marked sensitive via tower-http's sensitive-headers layer BEFORE tracing is invoked (KTD9, R25) — otherwise a debug trace leaks bearer tokens.
- Bounds run before tracing/limits so oversized garbage is cheap to drop.

**Limits (R27 transport dimension):** body size, header counts, path lengths, JSON depth (with U3.4), handshake/request/drain timeouts, global concurrency cap. All bounds documented and failing BEFORE unbounded allocation.

**KTD6 response hygiene:** secret-bearing responses set `Cache-Control: no-store` and are never compressed (compression of secret-bearing bodies invites length-oracle attacks; no-store keeps proxies/browsers from persisting values).

**Acceptance:** integration test asserts the order — a guard-rejected request never reaches tracing with an unmasked token; X-Vault-Token marked sensitive before the trace layer; the unauthenticated set (login, health, seal-status) is reachable tokenless yet still guard/bounds/limit-covered, while every other route rejects tokenless requests; oversized body / too many headers / slow handshake rejected within documented bounds; secret-bearing responses carry no-store and no content-encoding.

title: U9.3: plaintext refusal, loopback allowance, explicit reverse-proxy listener mode (R20)
description:
**Build:** Plaintext refusal + the explicit reverse-proxy listener mode (R20).

**Rules:**
- Any non-loopback bind without TLS fails startup (R18 seam laid in U1.3; this bead supplies the runtime refusal and the TLS path). Loopback-only (127.0.0.0/8, ::1) may run plaintext — ::1 COUNTS as loopback.
- Credentials and secret material never traverse the network in cleartext; the server REFUSES plaintext remote connections rather than warning.
- **Reverse-proxy mode is an explicit listener TYPE, not a boolean relaxation:** binds only a configured Unix socket or loopback address (the backend hop must be loopback or Unix — R20); forwarded client-identity/source headers are accepted only from the verified configured proxy peer in ONE allowlisted syntax — otherwise IGNORED (default posture: forwarded headers ignored); rate-limit source buckets use the verified proxy-derived source ONLY in this mode (otherwise an attacker forges X-Forwarded-For to dodge buckets — U4.3's keying rule).

**Peer verification mechanics differ by backend flavor (say so, or "exact peer" is fiction):** Unix-socket backend → the proxy's identity is verified via SO_PEERCRED uid allowlist (reuse U1.4's machinery) — a REAL check. Loopback TCP backend → only a source-address check is possible, and 127.0.0.1 is satisfiable by ANY local process — that trust boundary is documented explicitly (any local user can then forge forwarded sources into the rate-limit buckets), and the docs recommend the Unix-socket hop for multi-user hosts.

**Context:** Infisical's agent-vault shipped cleartext-HTTP transparent-proxy transport with session tokens unencrypted, hand-waved as "deploy on a trusted network" — the posture this requirement exists to refuse.

**Acceptance:** non-loopback+no-TLS refused at startup; ::1 plaintext allowed; loopback plaintext allowed; forwarded headers from any peer other than the verified configured proxy ignored; forwarded headers FROM the verified peer honored and rate-limit buckets key on the proxy-derived source (accept path, not just ignore path); Unix-backend proxy peer verified by uid; proxy mode refuses non-loopback/non-Unix backend binds; the loopback-TCP trust caveat appears in the listener docs.

title: U9.4: graceful shutdown wiring + transport test suite (tests/transport.rs)
description:
**Build:** Graceful-shutdown wiring at the transport layer + tests/transport.rs. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Shutdown sequence (R36, completing U1.3's skeleton):** stop admission → drain bounded in-flight work within the drain timeout → flush → commit the final UNSIGNED chain-head/shutdown event (audit chain machinery — U6.3 dependency) → zeroize key material. Because the daemon never holds the checkpoint private key (KTD4), shutdown does NOT claim an independently anchored checkpoint; doctor reports the resulting unanchored tail until the next external checkpoint command.

**Drain-window probe semantics ("stop admission" must not blind the supervisor):** during the drain window the listener KEEPS ACCEPTING connections; non-exempt requests receive an immediate 503 (admission stopped) while the liveness/health route continues answering until the final flush. A listener that stops accepting entirely would make every liveness probe a connection failure — the supervisor would kill the draining process, defeating the drain.

**Readiness vs liveness (R36):** readiness false whenever keyring, schema, transaction/audit path, or capacity state cannot uphold the fail-closed guarantees; liveness stays true so the supervisor does not kill a draining process. Checkpoint staleness is a warning, NOT a readiness failure.

**Test scenarios (tests/transport.rs):**
1. Non-loopback listener without TLS → refused at startup; ::1 counts as loopback for the plaintext allowance.
2. TLS configured → HTTPS with X-Vault-Token succeeds; plaintext HTTP to the TLS port rejected.
3. Certificate reload swaps the served cert with no restart and no dropped in-flight request (R36). 3b. Reload with unparsable/mismatched cert-key → rejected atomically, previous cert keeps serving, error surfaced (U9.1).
4. Oversized body / too many headers / slow handshake → rejected within documented bounds (R27).
5. Middleware-order integration test: guard-rejected request never reaches tracing with an unmasked token; X-Vault-Token sensitive before trace (KTD9/R25); unauthenticated set reachable tokenless but guard/bounds-covered (U9.2).
6. Forwarded client-identity headers from a non-configured peer → ignored (R20). 6b. Forwarded headers from the EXACT verified peer (Unix uid allowlist / loopback address) → honored; rate-limit buckets key on the proxy-derived source (U9.3's accept path).
7. Loopback plaintext listener → allowed.
8. Graceful shutdown under load: in-flight completes or aborts deterministically, final shutdown event committed and the chain verifies to it, no new admissions after stop. 8b. Liveness during drain: probe route answers 200 throughout the drain window while non-exempt requests get 503 (the supervisor never sees a dead process mid-drain).

**Verification:** `cargo test --test transport` green.

title: U5.1: KV v2 data endpoints — read/write with CAS, list, versioned read (R1/R11)
description:
**Build:** The KV v2 data endpoints: `GET/POST /v1/:mount/data/:path` with the data.data / data.metadata envelope, LIST on metadata/, versioned read via ?version=N.

**LIST routing trap (know it before the router is shaped):** pinned CLIs emit the literal nonstandard HTTP verb `LIST`, which axum's MethodFilter cannot express (standard-verb bitflags only) — the FINAL router this bead builds must dispatch custom methods (any-method route + manual dispatch or equivalent). Vault also accepts `GET ...?list=true`; handle it per captured fixtures — supported if any pinned client emits it, otherwise explicit-unsupported via U5.4.

**CAS semantics (R11) — the rotation-safety primitive, get every branch right:**
- `options.cas = 0` → create-only: rejected if the path's METADATA exists — regardless of the deletion state of the latest version (a soft-deleted or destroyed latest does not reopen create-only).
- positive `cas` → must equal the CURRENT version or the write is rejected: no version created, no success audit (a failure audit event IS emitted — R13).
- `cas_required` (per-path metadata flag or mount default) → unconditional writes rejected.
- Two operators can never silently clobber each other's cutover; rotation cutover (U7) rides exactly this check inside the coordinator transaction.

**Semantics:**
- Writes are monotonically versioned; KV v2 reads return the latest version — writing a replacement cuts consumers over on their next read; version history keeps prior values readable for rollback under the read-history capability (R22) until rotation closeout retires them.
- Versioned read requires read-history (ANY explicit ?version=N, including N = current — U3.2's request-shape rule); unversioned read requires read-current (distinct capabilities — R22).
- Every handler goes through: canonical Resource (U3.1) → coordinator transaction (U6.2) → grant reload → decrypt → audit-with-served-version → commit → respond. No handler touches storage directly.
- One configured mount `secret/` (KTD12); other mounts or nonempty Vault namespaces fail explicitly (R3).

**Gated by G0:** built against U0's captured client traces — this is the FINAL router the gate exists for.

**Acceptance:** `vault kv get -field=X <path>` issues preflight then data read, both succeed (with U5 sys bead); LIST verb dispatched correctly (and ?list=true per fixtures); stale cas rejected without version/audit-success but WITH a failure audit event; cas=0 on existing metadata rejected (even with soft-deleted latest); cas_required blocks unconditional writes; versioned/unversioned reads enforce their distinct capabilities.

title: U5.2: delete/undelete/destroy + metadata endpoints, retention guard (R29/KTD11)
description:
**Build:** The FOUR deletion wire forms per the captured matrix + `GET/POST /v1/:mount/metadata/:path` (version history, custom_metadata, max_versions, cas_required — KTD11).

**Wire forms (Vault KV v2 has four; naming them prevents a single-version-only implementation):** `DELETE /v1/:mount/data/:path` (soft-delete the LATEST version — what `vault kv delete` without -versions sends); `POST /v1/:mount/delete/:path`, `POST /v1/:mount/undelete/:path`, `POST /v1/:mount/destroy/:path` — each taking a `{"versions":[...]}` ARRAY (a batch of versions per call; array cardinality bounded per R27; unknown/invalid version entries handled per fixtures/differential runs).

**Deletion semantics (R29) — three distinct operations, never conflated:**
- soft-delete: reversible; undelete restores.
- destroy: destroy-capability-gated; removes a specific VERSION's ciphertext from the active store — permanently unavailable through the server and every supported API form. Destruction is LOGICAL, not cryptographic erasure: historical backups, copy-on-write pages, and unreclaimed filesystem blocks may retain encrypted copies until backup expiry/compaction/media replacement — the documentation says so plainly. (Per-version crypto deletion would need a per-version wrapped-DEK hierarchy — explicitly out of v0.1.)
- full purge (metadata + all versions): ONLY the local control-plane `secret purge` — dedicated destroy-all capability (R22), exact resource, explicit confirmation, audited reason. `DELETE /v1/:mount/metadata/:path` is explicitly UNSUPPORTED on the remote listener in v0.1 unless U0 proves a supported client requires it AND a separate safety decision revises the contract.

**Read-shape matrix (implementations classically diverge here; shapes pinned by U0 fixtures + U11.2 differential):** destroyed versions REMAIN enumerated in the metadata version map with `destroyed: true` — destroy removes ciphertext availability, never the version's metadata entry; a soft-deleted version reads back as a 404-class response with `deletion_time` populated and data absent; an unversioned read whose latest is soft-deleted returns 404-class — it NEVER falls back to an older live version; undelete of a DESTROYED version leaves it destroyed. Deletion-state changes are recorded in the MAC'd version-set summary (U2.2) so they are offline-tamper-evident.

**delete_version_after (the CLI sends it unprompted):** `vault kv metadata put` sends ALL metadata fields including `delete_version_after` (default "0s") even when the operator only set -max-versions. Zero/absent values (the pinned CLIs' defaults) are accepted and stored as UNSET; a NONZERO value returns an explicit unsupported-field error naming `delete_version_after` (R3 — silently ignoring it would let a client believe timed destruction is armed while the server never destroys anything). The pinned CLI's default-flags metadata body must round-trip.

**Retention:** per-secret and mount-default max_versions bound retained history; retention NEVER destroys the prior version of an incomplete rotation — the protected version may temporarily exceed max_versions, metadata and doctor report `retention_deferred_by_rotation`, and closeout/cancellation/supersession immediately re-evaluates pruning (R29).

**Metadata endpoints:** custom_metadata is CLIENT-OWNED compatibility data — the remote metadata endpoint can never overwrite or delete server-owned rotation fields (R12; those are control-plane-only, U7.6).

**Acceptance:** all four wire forms round-trip per fixtures (incl. multi-version arrays; oversized arrays rejected per R27); soft-delete→undelete restores; destroy removes the version's data from every read path/API form while its metadata entry persists with destroyed:true; the read-shape matrix above matches fixtures/differential; nonzero delete_version_after → explicit unsupported error, zero accepted-as-unset, CLI default body round-trips; max_versions bounds history except the rotation-protected version; remote metadata DELETE → explicit unsupported; custom_metadata round-trips without touching server rotation fields.

title: U5.3: sys endpoints — mounts preflight (fragile joint), seal-status stub, health/readiness (R1/R36)
description:
**Build:** The sys endpoints unmodified clients require (per U0 evidence): `sys/internal/ui/mounts/<path>`, `sys/seal-status`, `sys/health`.

**Auth posture per endpoint (follows captured fixtures — a blanket token gate breaks the pinned CLIs):** `sys/health` and `sys/seal-status` are served WITHOUT token authentication — that is how `vault status`/`bao status` and monitoring probes work — and therefore JOIN U4.3's unauthenticated-surface rate/size buckets alongside AppRole login; `sys/internal/ui/mounts` requires a valid token. U9.2's middleware stack implements this as a route-scoped authentication layer (the unauthenticated set bypasses token verification but stays inside guard/bounds/limit layers).

**Mount preflight — THE historically fragile compat joint:** `sys/internal/ui/mounts/<path>` must return `options.version:"2"` EXACTLY as the pinned clients expect — asserted byte-shape against U0 fixtures. It is a Vault-INTERNAL endpoint whose contract may change across client releases; that is precisely why R2's configuration-only guarantee holds for tested versions only (KTD14).

**seal-status:** always reports UNSEALED — a compatibility stub answering the client's question, NOT a seal implementation (Scope Boundaries: the boot-supplied age identity is the seal; there is no ceremony to report on).

**health:** reports initialized/unsealed/active with READINESS semantics from R36 — readiness false whenever keyring, schema, transaction/audit path, or capacity state cannot uphold the fail-closed guarantees; checkpoint staleness is a warning here, never a readiness failure.

**Acceptance:** preflight shape matches each pinned client's captured expectation; tokenless `sys/health` and `sys/seal-status` succeed while tokenless `sys/internal/ui/mounts` is rejected per fixtures; the unauthenticated sys endpoints are rate/size-bucketed with U4.3; `vault`/`bao`/fnox complete their preflight against it (full proof in U11); health flips with injected readiness failures; a deliberately mismatched options.version is caught by the harness (guards the joint).

title: U5.4: explicit-unsupported surface + evidence-generated error envelopes (R3/AE1)
description:
**Build:** The explicit-unsupported surface (R3) + error envelope generation from captured evidence.

**Rules:**
- Everything outside the declared surface — PATCH included, `DELETE /v1/:mount/metadata/:path` included (R29), unknown mounts, nonempty Vault namespaces (KTD12) — returns an EXPLICIT unsupported error NAMING the endpoint. Never an empty success (AE1: an empty 200 teaches a client its write worked when it didn't), never a silent approximation as an ordinary write.
- The declared surface is exactly what U0/U11 evidence demonstrated a real client needs — the complexity budget forbids new compatibility endpoints without that evidence.
- Errors use the Vault `{"errors":[...]}` shape. Status codes, error envelopes, and field-presence rules are GENERATED from the captured/differential compatibility contract (U0 fixtures + U11 differential runs) — no exact 404 or empty-list behavior is hard-coded from prose before evidence freezes it. Read-of-nonexistent-path behavior comes from fixtures, not assumption.
- `renew-self`'s unsupported response (U4.2) uses this same machinery.

**Acceptance:** AE1 — unimplemented endpoint → explicit unsupported error naming the endpoint; nonexistent-path read matches captured contract fixtures; every unsupported route is enumerable (feeds docs/compatibility.md generation in U12).

title: U5.5: KV request/value bounds + secret-response hygiene (R27/KTD6)
description:
**Build:** KV-surface request/value bounds (R27) + secret-response hygiene (KTD6).

**Bounds — all documented, all failing BEFORE unbounded allocation:**
- A secret version stores ONE bounded encrypted JSON object — never independently encrypted fields.
- Limits on: total encoded bytes per secret value, field count, field-name length, nesting depth (with U3.4's strict deserializer), list-result cardinality, deletion-endpoint `versions` array cardinality (U5.2).
- custom_metadata bounds: key count, key length, value length — aligned with upstream's limits per U11.2 differential runs (Vault bounds it at 128 keys / 128-char keys / 512-char values); rejected before allocation with a stable error. Without this, the metadata POST path is an unbounded-allocation write hole that also diverges from differential behavior.
- These compose with transport bounds (U9.2: body size, header counts, path lengths, timeouts, concurrency cap) and per-identity rate limits (U4.3) to bound decrypt-plus-audit write amplification from a stolen token or looping client.

**LIST over-bound behavior (no silent truncation — AE1 discipline):** a LIST whose result set exceeds the documented cardinality bound FAILS with an explicit bounded-list error in the {"errors":[...]} envelope — never a silently truncated key set. Upstream KV v2 has no list pagination, so no fixture can license truncation; a silently shortened listing teaches a client a false world-view, which is exactly the silent-approximation failure R3 forbids. Mirrored in U5.6.

**Response hygiene (KTD6):** secret-bearing responses carry `Cache-Control: no-store` and are NEVER compressed.

**Acceptance:** each bound rejects at its documented limit with a stable error; oversized secret object rejected before encryption/storage; over-bound LIST → explicit bounded-list error, never a truncated set; custom_metadata limits enforced pre-allocation and aligned with differential runs; no-store + no compression asserted on every secret-bearing response.

title: U5.6: KV API test suite (tests/api_kv.rs — AE1, CAS matrix, captured-contract envelopes)
description:
**Build:** tests/api_kv.rs — the KV surface suite. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. AE1: unimplemented endpoint → explicit unsupported error naming the endpoint, not empty success.
2. `vault kv get -field=X <path>` issues the preflight then the data read → both succeed against the server (feeds Flow F2; full pinned-CLI proof in U11).
3. Versioned read returns the requested version under read-history; unversioned returns latest under read-current (R11/R22).
4. CAS matrix (feeds AE3): stale cas → write rejected, no version created, no success audit, and a FAILURE audit event exists (R13); cas=0 on existing metadata → rejected — including after soft-delete or destroy of the latest version (the current version NUMBER is unchanged by deletion state, so positive cas must still equal it); cas_required metadata flag → unconditional write rejected; lowering max_versions prunes on the NEXT write, not at metadata-change time (differential-pinned — an eager pruner diverges observably).
5. max_versions bounds retained history; the prior version of an incomplete rotation is never retired by the bound (R29 — retention_deferred_by_rotation; completes with U7).
6. soft-delete then undelete restores; destroy removes a specific version's data from every read path and API form — logical destruction per R29's documented semantics, no cryptographic-erasure claim.
7. Read of a nonexistent path → status + error envelope match the captured contract fixtures (U0), not a hard-coded assumption.
8. Secret-bearing responses carry Cache-Control: no-store and no compression (KTD6).
9. Bounds: oversized value/fields/depth/list rejected at documented limits (R27); over-bound LIST → explicit bounded-list error, never a silently truncated key set (U5.5); oversized custom_metadata rejected pre-allocation; oversized deletion `versions` array rejected.
10. Deleted-vs-destroyed read matrix (shapes vs fixtures/differential): versioned read of a soft-deleted version → 404-class with deletion_time populated; of a destroyed version → 404-class with destroyed:true in metadata; undelete-after-destroy leaves it destroyed; unversioned read with soft-deleted latest → 404-class, never fallback to an older live version.
11. Per-identity rate limit (U4.3) engages against a looping authenticated client on a KV data read — the KV surface actually mounts the limiter; limit events appear as bounded audit aggregates.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test api_kv` green.

title: U5.7: vertical slice — init → serve TLS → pinned fnox read E2E (Milestone M1 evidence)
description:
**Build:** tests/vertical_slice.rs — MILESTONE M1's evidence: `init` → `serve` over TLS → the PINNED fnox binary resolves a secret end-to-end.

**The full path under test, composed for the first time:** local init (exclusive lock, R17 disclosure ordering) → serve with fail-closed validation → TLS listener (U9.1) → fnox configured by documentation only → raw-target guard → canonical resource → data-audience token verify (constant-time keyed MAC) → grant reload inside the coordinator transaction → AEAD decrypt → audit entry with served version commits → response transmits. Plus a write through the same slice.

**Rules:** the fnox binary itself — not a stand-in CLI — is the subject (R2); no fnox change beyond configuration (feeds AE8); the audit chain must carry the read with the served version (proves R26 end-to-end, not just in unit tests).

**Exit:** this test green = M1 reached. A development milestone, not a release — no public claims attach to it.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test vertical_slice` green with the pinned fnox from U0's matrix.

title: U7.1: declared-consumer registry — control-plane CRUD over MAC'd records (R40)
description:
**Build:** The declared-consumer registry (R40) — local-control-plane CRUD over KTD15 MAC-authenticated records.

**Why it exists (fourth-revision reversal, recorded reasoning):** R10's observed list is trustworthy for rotation only once every known consumer has been migrated onto the server — the list is what you need in order to migrate, and migration is what makes the list complete. So the first migration needs a SYSTEM OF RECORD before observation exists. The manual registry ships in v0.1; discovery/import mechanization is v0.2 (this registry + KTD11's metadata record are the reserved seam).

**Record schema (fixed, ordinary records and queries — never a graph database):** stable consumer id, canonical secret resource, owner, environment, source, optional identity id, optional consumer-instance id, lifecycle status, last-verified time, non-secret note. NEVER a secret value, command, script, webhook, or executable configuration (complexity budget: the registry stores facts, not behavior).

**Lifecycle status is a CLOSED enum (the closeout guard keys off it — U7.5):** `declared | migrated | retired`. `retired` requires an audited reason supplied at the registry update (`consumer retire --reason` / `consumer instance retire --reason`); a retired consumer no longer blocks rotation closeout. Reconciliation (U7.2) and the closeout guard (U7.5) consume exactly these values — without the enum, "unresolved declared consumer" has no operator-actionable resolution path (a declared consumer with no identity id could never be on-current and closeout would dead-end on it).

**Access:** control-plane only (R34); registry mutation requires its own management capability (R22); every mutation flows through the coordinator with its audit entry (KTD8).
