**Acceptance:** CRUD round-trips over the control socket; records MAC-verified on read; lifecycle transitions enforce the closed enum and `retired` demands a reason (audited); mutation without the capability → denied and audited; remote listener has no registry routes.

title: U7.2: consumer view — declared/authorized/observed + reconciliation states (R10/R40)
description:
**Build:** The consumer view + reconciliation (R10/R40/R23).

**Three separately labeled sets — NEVER collapsed into one comfortable list:**
- **declared** — registry records (U7.1);
- **authorized** — every identity whose CURRENT grants (R8) permit read on the path;
- **observed** — identities with verified audited reads inside R23's lookback window, each carrying last-read time and the VERSION SERVED (R13's version-on-audit field).

**Rules:** recency and observation ANNOTATE the authorized list — they never filter an identity out of it (an identity with read scope but no read inside the window still appears, annotated stale — non-observation is not non-need). Observed data comes from scanning PRIMARY audit events over the window (KTD16's index is deferred post-v0.1; primary events are always authoritative). The authorized view does not expire.

**Reconciliation states (R40):** declared-but-unauthorized (integration not yet migrated), authorized-but-undeclared (grant exists, nobody claimed it), declared-but-unobserved (migrated but silent), observed-but-undeclared (someone reads it that nobody registered). All four stay separately visible (AE13).

**Access:** consumer enumeration requires its own capability (R22); control-plane surface.

**Acceptance:** AE13 shape — a declared-but-unmigrated integration, an authorized-but-silent workload, and an observed-but-undeclared identity all separately visible; window math correct at boundaries; annotations never filter.

title: U7.3: rotation lifecycle — begin/cutover/cancel, CAS-coupled state transitions (R11)
description:
**Build:** The rotation record lifecycle (R11): `rotation begin` → `rotation cutover` → (only while prepared) `rotation cancel` | `rotation refresh`, plus `rotation rollback`, with states and supersession.

**States:** prepared | cutover | completed | cancelled-before-cutover | superseded.

**Capability + confinement (all lifecycle commands):** control-plane only (R34), gated by the rotation-management capability (R22). Cutover requires NO separate KV write grant — the capability authorizes the write and the audit event records it as a rotation cutover (one authorization model; the matcher maps the command per U3.2's management-mount rule). U7.5's `rotation complete` carries the same gate.

**Transition matrix (closed; every non-listed transition refuses with a state-naming error):**
- At most ONE non-terminal rotation per path: `rotation begin` REFUSES while a prepared or cutover rotation exists on the path.
- `rotation refresh` (prepared only): re-snapshots declared consumers, instance sets, current version, and CAS expectation — audited; the recovery move after a stale-CAS cutover attempt.
- `superseded` is entered only FROM cutover (a plain KV write after cutover, before completion). A plain write during PREPARED merely stales the saved CAS (cutover will then fail → refresh/cancel); it does not supersede.
- `cancel` on non-prepared states, `cutover` on non-prepared states, `complete` on non-cutover states: refuse.
- Cancel and supersession each RE-EVALUATE retention_deferred_by_rotation pruning for the path (R29) in the same coordinator transaction — otherwise deferral holds leak and versions accumulate past max_versions until some unrelated rotation completes.

**`rotation begin`:** creates a prepared record snapshotting — WITHOUT writing a secret — the declared consumers (R40), authorized identities, active consumer-instance sets, the current version, the expected check-and-set value, actor, and time. The snapshot is the closeout contract: a grant added after begin does not alter it (the live authorized view shows the drift).

**`rotation cutover`:** accepts the bounded replacement value through an approved input channel (stdin/FD — R37, never argv), then ATOMICALLY in one coordinator transaction: validates the saved CAS expectation, writes the new version, records target version + cutover time, transitions prepared→cutover, appends the audit event. A CAS conflict leaves the rotation PREPARED and requires explicit refresh/cancel/restart — never a silently changed snapshot (a concurrent write cannot silently clobber the cutover). Cutover atomicity under kill points is owned by the U11 fault suite (U11.4 names the rotation-cutover kill points).

**`rotation cancel`:** the command form of cancellation — permitted ONLY while prepared; audited; transitions prepared→cancelled-before-cutover with no secret written. Once state is cutover it REFUSES, pointing at `rotation rollback`.

**`rotation rollback <path>` (post-cutover recovery — a first-class command, not a documented manual procedure):** control-plane, rotation-management-gated, audited; performs a SERVER-SIDE copy-forward of the pre-cutover version's value into a new highest version — the value never leaves the server (a manual read-out-and-resubmit would transit the operator's terminal/history, violating the disclosure posture); marks the open rotation `superseded`. The current-version pointer NEVER moves backward.

**Hard rules:** cancellation only before cutover; after cutover, recovery is `rotation rollback` (new higher version); a superseding write marks the rotation `superseded` and prevents completion. The staging window lives UPSTREAM: the old SaaS credential stays valid until the operator revokes it after cutover (an action the server records but cannot perform).

**Acceptance:** begin writes nothing; double begin on one path refused; stale-CAS cutover fails leaving prepared; refresh re-snapshots and a subsequent cutover succeeds; cutover atomicity under kill points (U11 fault suite); cancel on prepared → cancelled-before-cutover, audited, secret unwritten; no cancellation path exists post-cutover; superseding write after cutover → superseded; write during prepared → CAS stales, state stays prepared; cancel and supersession release the retention deferral; `rotation rollback` creates a new higher version equal to the prior value server-side and marks the rotation superseded; every lifecycle command absent from the remote listener and denied without the rotation-management capability (audited).

title: U7.4: rotation status — per-instance adoption from audited read versions (R33)
description:
**Build:** `rotation status <path>` — post-write version adoption, management-gated (R33).

**Classification per snapshotted consumer, from R13's versioned read events in the primary store. Scan range: from the rotation record's CUTOVER TIMESTAMP to now** (the rotation record bounds the range — classification must never silently narrow to a lookback window):
- `on-current` — latest audited read since cutover fetched the current (target) version;
- `on-prior` — latest read since cutover fetched a prior version;
- `silent-since-write` — nothing since the write.

**R23's lookback window governs U7.2's OBSERVED view only.** Here it may annotate recency ("last read 45d ago") but NEVER reclassifies: a slow-cadence consumer that fetched the target version once, then went quiet past the window, stays `on-current` — otherwise the U7.5 closeout guard becomes time-sensitive (verified today, re-blocked next month) and adoption is systematically understated for exactly the low-frequency consumers a small team has.

**Granularity — three levels, because one identity's replicas can disagree and an identity-level rollup alone would overstate adoption (KTD5):** declared-consumer, identity, and stable consumer-instance. Credential accessors remain a DIAGNOSTIC drill-down where distinct accessors appear — not the durable identity of a replica (tokens inherit the consumer_instance_id of the secret_id that created them; workload secret_ids require an instance id unless the operator explicitly accepted identity-only tracking — U4.1).

**Language discipline:** status says "fetched version N" — NEVER "using version N". Only an application-level acknowledgement could prove use, and that is out of v0.1. Verification by observation, not guesswork.

**Acceptance:** AE11 shape — two snapshotted consumers, one reads after the write → one on-current, one silent-since-write, from audited read versions, not grant inference; a versioned read of the prior version → on-prior; a consumer whose only post-cutover read predates the R23 window is STILL on-current (recency annotated, class unchanged); two instances under one identity split correctly at instance granularity; scan latency within operator tolerance at years-of-events volume (anchor recorded in U6.6 scenario 8).

title: U7.5: rotation complete — closeout guard, overrides, redacted closeout report (R33)
description:
**Build:** `rotation complete` — the guarded closeout (R33) + redacted closeout report. Control-plane only, rotation-management capability (same gate as U7.3's lifecycle commands).

**Required source state:** `rotation complete` refuses unless the rotation is in state `cutover` — completing a prepared (never-cutover) rotation is undefined and refused with a state-naming error.

**The guard:** completion succeeds ONLY while (a) the written target is still the CURRENT version, and (b) every consumer in the snapshot — evaluated over the snapshot's declared consumers (R40) and active consumer instances — is `on-current`. **The `--acknowledge-unverified` override applies ONLY to condition (b).** A rotation that is superseded, or whose target is no longer current, can NEVER complete — no flag overrides condition (a); the recovery is a new rotation (or `rotation rollback`). Expired or revoked instances are labeled `retired-without-proof` and require a recorded retirement reason or the override — they NEVER silently disappear. **"Resolved" is defined:** a snapshotted declared consumer is resolved iff it is `on-current` OR its registry lifecycle is `retired` with an audited reason (U7.1's enum; `consumer retire --reason` / `consumer instance retire --reason` is the non-override unblock path). An unresolved declared consumer blocks closeout unless covered by the override (R40). The guard evaluates PRIMARY events directly — always authoritative (KTD16's former index-health clause is gone with its deferral). Checkpoint staleness (U6.4's predicate — the exact `checkpoint.max_age` / `max_unanchored_events` thresholds) is surfaced as a closeout WARNING in the report: closing a rotation whose adoption evidence is unanchored is flagged, not blocked.

**On success:** records upstream revocation as an OPERATOR ASSERTION (the server records it, it cannot prove a third-party SaaS revoked anything — no server-attested upstream revocation, Scope Boundaries); retires the prior version (re-evaluating any retention_deferred_by_rotation pruning — R29); **stamps `last_completed_rotation` in the server-owned rotation metadata and clears `changed_since_last_completed_rotation`** (the fields U7.6 reads — this is the data handoff, owned here); emits the redacted closeout report: path, prior + target versions, snapshot with adoption states, write + completion audit sequence numbers, upstream-revocation attestation, any override reason, latest checkpoint reference + staleness state. Report integrity RIDES THE EXISTING AUDIT CHAIN — no separate closeout signature scheme (Scope Boundaries rejected separate signed receipts).

**Beyond this one guard: observation only.** No automatic revocation, no campaign state machine in v0.1 (deferred: multi-step campaigns, per-consumer acks beyond the single override, canaries, drift detection — the server-side salted-fingerprint endpoint is REJECTED OUTRIGHT as a secret-equality oracle; any later drift scan runs client-side over authorized read-history fetches).

**Acceptance:** completion refused while a silent consumer stands; with override + reason it succeeds and the reason lands in the audit entry; completion after a superseding write fails (superseded) EVEN WITH --acknowledge-unverified; completion of a prepared rotation refused; retiring a declared consumer in the registry (with reason) unblocks closeout WITHOUT the override; success stamps last_completed_rotation and clears the changed flag; closeout report carries all named fields incl. checkpoint staleness and NO secret material; retired-without-proof path requires reason or override.

title: U7.6: secret age vs rotation interval — control-plane-owned policy metadata (R12)
description:
**Build:** Secret age + rotation interval (R12).

**Semantics:**
- A secret's age = time since its last COMPLETED rotation, or since creation when never rotated.
- The per-secret rotation interval lives in SERVER-OWNED rotation metadata (KTD11's secret_meta server fields, U2.2), writable ONLY through the local control plane. The commands are `rotation interval set <path> <duration>` and `rotation interval clear <path>` — control-plane only (R34), gated by the rotation-management capability (R22), each an audited coordinator mutation (R26). "No interval set" reported when none configured — never a fake default.
- Vault `custom_metadata` remains client-owned compatibility data and CANNOT alter rotation policy: the control-plane view may project the interval alongside custom metadata, but the remote metadata endpoint can neither overwrite nor delete it (enforced with U5.2).
- A value written OUTSIDE a rotation is labeled `changed_since_last_completed_rotation` — the age display never implies the current value was covered by an older completed rotation (an operator glancing at "rotated 10 days ago" must not miss that someone hand-wrote a new value yesterday).
- Display surface: age, interval, and the changed_since_last_completed_rotation flag are FIELDS on existing control-plane views — `rotation status <path>` (U7.4) and the secret-metadata/consumer views — with human-readable AND stable JSON output (R16). No new display subsystem.

**Acceptance:** interval set/clear round-trip over the control socket and require the rotation-management capability; no-interval secret → "no interval set"; post-rotation unrotated write → flagged changed_since_last_completed_rotation; remote metadata endpoint cannot alter or delete the interval, control plane can; age computed from last COMPLETED rotation only (cutover-without-closeout does not reset it).

title: U7.7: rotation/consumer test suite (tests/rotation.rs — AE3/AE11/AE13)
description:
**Build:** tests/rotation.rs — the consumers/rotation suite (Flow F4 end-to-end). All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. AE3: three identities read a key within the window → all three enumerated; begin → cutover with replacement → consumers get new value on next read; previous version stays readable (read-history) until complete; stale-CAS cutover fails leaving prepared; completion after a superseding write fails (superseded).
2. AE13: declared-but-unmigrated integration + authorized-but-silent workload + observed-but-undeclared identity → all separately visible; the declared consumer enters the rotation snapshot and BLOCKS unqualified completion (R40).
3. Cancellation before cutover → secret unwritten, record cancelled-before-cutover; NO cancellation path after cutover (R11).
4. AE11: after the write, one snapshotted consumer reads → on-current; silent one → silent-since-write; versioned read of prior → on-prior; complete refused while silent stands; with --acknowledge-unverified + reason it succeeds, reason in audit entry, closeout report carries snapshot/adoption/audit-seq/attestation/override with NO secret material (R33); complete on a PREPARED rotation refused; --acknowledge-unverified on a SUPERSEDED rotation still refused (override covers consumer verification only).
5. Two instances under one identity: one fetches new, one keeps fetching prior → instance-granular split visible, identity NOT reported simply on-current; expired instance → retired-without-proof requiring reason or override (R13/R33).
6. Identity with read scope, no read in window → still appears, annotated stale (R10); slow-cadence classification: a consumer whose only post-cutover read predates the R23 lookback window is STILL on-current (recency annotated, never reclassified — U7.4's scan-range rule).
7. Grant added after begin → snapshot unchanged; live authorized view shows drift (R10/R11).
8. `rotation rollback` (the command) → server-side copy-forward creates a new higher version equal to the prior value (value never leaves the server); open rotation marked superseded; current pointer never moves backward (R11).
9. Rotation-protected prior version exceeding max_versions → retention_deferred_by_rotation reported; closeout re-evaluates pruning (R29); CANCEL and SUPERSESSION each also release the deferral and pruning proceeds (U7.3's rule).
10. Remote metadata endpoint cannot alter/delete the rotation interval; control plane can (R12).
11. No interval → "no interval set"; post-rotation unrotated write → changed_since_last_completed_rotation; cutover WITHOUT closeout leaves age unreset — only `rotation complete` stamps last_completed_rotation (R12/U7.5-U7.6 handoff).
12. Capability denials: enumeration, status, registry mutation, AND each lifecycle command (begin/cutover/cancel/complete/rollback/refresh) without its capability → denied and audited (R22); all lifecycle commands absent from the remote listener (R34).
13. Transition matrix: double begin on one path refused; `rotation refresh` re-snapshots (prepared only) and cutover then succeeds; illegal transitions (cutover on cutover, cancel on terminal states, refresh on cutover) refuse naming the state; write during prepared stales CAS without superseding.
14. Adoption-bucket correctness at scale: on-current/on-prior/silent-since-write classification asserted against U6.6's years-of-events synthetic corpus generator; the recorded KTD16 latency anchor re-validated here (owns the bucket-logic assertion that U6.6 scenario 8 deliberately leaves to this suite).

**Verification:** `cargo test --test rotation` green.

title: U8.1: recipient rewrap — atomic keyring envelope swap + recovery-identity path (KTD3/KTD8/R21)
description:
**Build:** Recipient rotation — the ROUTINE key operation, deliberately cheap (this cheapness is what the KTD3 keyring design purchased over per-blob age).

**Flow (offline CLI):** decrypt the current `system_keyring` envelope under the OLD identity → construct and verify the replacement envelope (new recipient set: new server recipient, optional documented offline recovery recipient) → commit the new envelope + incremented keyring generation + audit event in ONE redb transaction (KTD3/KTD8).

**Guarantees:** NO record is touched (the purpose keys inside are unchanged — only the envelope re-wraps); a crash exposes either the old generation with its old audit head or the new generation with its matching audit event — never a cross-file partial state (this is why the envelope lives in a redb table, not a sidecar file).

**Recovery path (R21/R32):** the offline recovery identity can decrypt the envelope and rewrap to a fresh server recipient — the fresh-host restore story depends on this exact operation.

**Acceptance:** post-rotate, keyring opens under the new identity and NOT the old; no record bytes changed (byte-compare); kill at every point around the commit → old-envelope+old-head or new-envelope+its-audit-event, nothing between; valid envelope copied into another store → embedded store-id check rejects before readiness (KTD3); recovery-identity rewrap round-trip works.

title: U8.2: record-key rotation — offline whole-store re-encrypt under lifecycle guard (KTD3/R35)
description:
**Build:** Record-encryption-key rotation — the RARE case (key-compromise response, crypto hygiene). Offline only, whole-store, no reachable half-state.

**Flow (the RENAME is the single commit point):** CLI stamps the ORIGINAL store `lifecycle=reencrypting` (U1.3's serve REFUSES to start against it during the pass — R35) → stream every record into a NEW store file re-encrypted under a new record key, drawing FRESH nonces and fresh associated data for every record (KTD3 — never a reused nonce, even for unchanged plaintext) — the `.new` file is written ALREADY stamped `lifecycle=ready` → fsync → atomic rename over the old file. There is deliberately NO post-rename restamp step: a crash immediately after the rename leaves a fully serviceable ready store (the old design's rename-then-restore-lifecycle ordering had a crash window that stranded an installed store stamped reencrypting with the original gone).

**Interruption contract:** an interrupted pass leaves the ORIGINAL store's data intact (only its lifecycle stamp changed) — recovery is either RERUN (completes the pass) or the audited **`reencrypt --abort`** command: deletes any partial `.new` file and restores `lifecycle=ready` on the original, returning to service without re-encrypting. Deliberately NO resumable-cursor machinery, NO mixed-encryption state to reason about (ops-light applied to key rotation: the simple restartable pass beats a clever incremental one at this scale).

**After the pass:** every secret readable; all records carry the new key id; old-key records gone; decrypt-only predecessor keys retained in the keyring where semantics require (record key predecessors retire after the pass completes). Audit history survives and still verifies.

**Acceptance:** full-pass round-trip (every secret readable, new key ids everywhere); kill mid-pass → original data intact; `reencrypt --abort` after a mid-pass kill → original serves WITHOUT rerunning the pass; kill immediately AFTER the rename → the store serves (no restamp window); `serve` during the pass refuses (lifecycle); abort is audited; nonce freshness asserted under the deterministic-RNG harness; audit chain verifies after the pass.

title: U8.3: per-purpose-key rotation semantics + credential epoch rotate incident command (R41)
description:
**Build:** Per-purpose-key rotation semantics (fixed, documented, enforced), the rotation COMMANDS for the two purpose keys no other bead owns — metadata-integrity key and audit-payload key — plus the `credential epoch rotate` incident command (R41).

**Why fixed here:** so no generic "rotate all keys" command can ever promise the impossible — each purpose key has DIFFERENT correct semantics:
- **secret-record key** → offline whole-store re-encryption — U8.2's command.
- **metadata-integrity MAC key** → offline rewrite + MAC regeneration over every clear record (R39) — IMPLEMENTED HERE, REUSING U8.2's pass machinery (new-file → fsync → atomic-rename commit point, `.new` written already-ready, audited `--abort`, same interruption contract) under the SAME `lifecycle=reencrypting` value (R35's enum is closed — U1.3; a clear progress record names the actual operation so refusal messages and doctor say "metadata re-MAC", not just "reencrypting"). After the pass every clear record verifies under the new key and U6.4's full-state verification (state digest + clear-record MACs + checkpoint verify) passes. **The pass completes ONLY with an immediate `audit checkpoint prepare` + register anchoring the NEW state digest** (U2.5's digest is MAC-dependent by design; without the fresh checkpoint, doctor/`audit verify --state` sit red against the last anchor after a recommended operation).
- **audit-payload key** → FORWARD-ONLY with decrypt-only historical keys retained — IMPLEMENTED HERE: one coordinator transaction adds the new audit-payload key generation to the keyring and commits its audit event; NO ciphertext is rewritten (rewriting audit ciphertext would invalidate anchored chain hashes — KTD4); historical events stay decryptable under retired keys forever.
- **credential-verifier key** → ONLY together with a credential-epoch increment (below).
- **audit-index key** → minted-but-dormant (KTD16 deferred); if the index ever ships it rotates by rebuilding the non-authoritative index from primary events (wayfinder T10).

**`credential epoch rotate --reason <...>` (R41) — the one-command incident response:** increments `current_credential_epoch`; generates a new credential-verifier key; INVALIDATES EVERY OUTSTANDING TOKEN AND SECRET_ID (epoch is in the verifier MAC domain — U4.1 — so old credentials fail cryptographically, no enumeration needed); mints a replacement emergency CONTROL credential using R17's disclosure-before-commit ordering (validate sink → generate → flush → commit). Reuses R32's epoch primitive: one local command ends every remotely usable credential. Available over the owner-only control socket AND offline (R34 confinement either way); audited with the reason.

**Primitive ownership (shared, never reimplemented):** the epoch-bump-plus-emergency-credential primitive — increment epoch, new credential-verifier key where applicable, mint + disclose a replacement emergency control credential under R17 sink ordering — is OWNED HERE and REUSED by restore (U10.3, bead olss-charter-qul.11.3) and offline `clock repair` (U1.5). Any operation that bumps the credential epoch kills every outstanding credential including the operator's control credential, and no bootstrap route exists on any listener — so every epoch-bumping command must invoke this primitive or it strands the operator. Those beads call it; they do not rebuild it. **Retrofit duty (this bead owns it):** U1.5 ships earlier with clock repair NOT bumping the epoch (its text says so); THIS bead wires the primitive into clock repair and un-ignores tests/startup.rs scenario 11 — done only when that scenario runs un-ignored and a shared-symbol/integration test proves clock repair and restore invoke THIS implementation.

**Acceptance:** metadata-key rotation and audit-payload-key rotation implemented here per the semantics above (tested in U8.4 scenarios 9–10, including the post-pass checkpoint anchor); recipient rewrap routes to U8.1's command and record-key re-encryption to U8.2's; epoch rotate kills all outstanding credentials (U4.4 scenario 10 covers concurrency); emergency credential disclosed exactly once with R17 ordering; command works over the control socket and offline, absent from the remote listener; the primitive is exposed for U10.3/U1.5 reuse with the shared-symbol test (one implementation, three callers); tests/startup.rs scenario 11 runs un-ignored.

title: U8.4: key-rotation test suite (tests/reencrypt.rs — interruption paths, store-id transplant, all five purpose keys)
description:
**Build:** tests/reencrypt.rs — the key-rotation suite. Execution note: test the INTERRUPTION path explicitly — the original store must remain intact and no mixed-key state may ever be servable. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. Rotate recipient → keyring opens under the new identity, not the old; no record bytes changed.
2. Kill at every point around the recipient-rewrap commit → after restart, either old envelope + old audit head, or new envelope + its matching audit event (KTD8).
3. Valid keyring envelope copied into another store → embedded store-id validation rejects it before readiness (KTD3).
4. Recover the keyring with the offline recovery identity and rewrap to a fresh recipient (R21/R32 recovery path).
5. Rotate record key → every secret readable; all records carry the new key id; old-key records gone.
6. Interruption matrix for the record-key pass (U8.2's rename-commit flow): kill mid-pass → original data intact, `serve` against it refuses (lifecycle), RERUN completes; kill mid-pass then `reencrypt --abort` → partial .new deleted, original restored to ready and SERVES without re-encrypting; kill immediately AFTER the atomic rename → the new store serves (no restamp window); abort is audited.
7. Audit history survives BOTH rotations and still verifies (chain + checkpoints).
8. `credential epoch rotate` → all pre-rotate tokens/secret_ids rejected; emergency control credential authenticates; audit records the reason.
9. Rotate metadata-integrity MAC key (offline rewrite, U8.3) → every clear record re-MACs under the new key and U6.4's full-state verification passes; the pass ends with a fresh checkpoint prepare+register anchoring the new state digest (U8.3's rule — `audit verify --state` green against the NEW anchor); same interruption matrix as scenario 6 (kill mid-pass → rerun or abort; kill after rename → serves); `serve` during the pass refuses (R39/R35) and the refusal names the metadata re-MAC operation (progress record).
10. Rotate audit-payload key (forward-only, U8.3) → events appended after the rotation encrypt under the new key id; historical events remain decryptable via `audit query` under the retained decrypt-only key; prior event ciphertext bytes unchanged (byte-compare); chain + registered checkpoints still verify (KTD4).

**Verification:** `cargo test --test reencrypt` green.

title: U10.1: logical backup format + recovery-epoch rules — design & freeze (Gate G3, KTD17)
description:
**Design-and-freeze bead — Gate G3.** Design the logical backup format + restore/audit-epoch rules (KTD17), review, FREEZE. One of M0's four gates: the backup format is persistent format, so it freezes with the others. (Gate G2 depends on this bead for the recovery-family event types — the audit schema must freeze WITH them, not before.)

**Format principles (KTD17):**
- Owned by the APPLICATION and versioned INDEPENDENTLY from redb — one consistent read transaction (redb MVCC reads run beside the writer, proven by U2.1's spike) produces versioned canonical frames. NEVER a copy of the live redb file as the normative format. This keeps release-fixture restores stable across redb file-format changes (R35/R38).
- Frame set (R32): versioned canonical frames per table; encrypted record bytes UNCHANGED; authenticated clear-record bytes; the current keyring envelope; a recovery keyring rewrapped to configured backup recipients (their non-secret fingerprints recorded); ALL audit events; per-table counts and digests; a signed manifest (store id, schema version, keyring generation, chain head, latest included checkpoint, checksums) — signed via KTD4's external flow under a DISTINCT domain-separation label.
- **Manifest signing is a TWO-STEP ceremony mirroring checkpoint prepare/sign/register (the daemon never holds the signing key — KTD4), which means an UNATTENDED nightly `backup` produces an archive whose manifest is checksummed but NOT YET signed.** The format therefore defines both states explicitly: `backup` emits the archive + an unsigned manifest with full checksums; `backup manifest sign` (operator/timer, offline signing key) completes the signature like a checkpoint. Restore policy for unsigned-but-checksummed archives: restore WARNS and requires explicit `--allow-unsigned-manifest`; doctor reports backups whose manifests remain unsigned. Restore verifies a signed manifest's signature against the operator-supplied checkpoint-verification PUBLIC key from the recovery package — NEVER a key carried inside the archive (an attacker who can rewrite the archive could rewrite an embedded key).
- The archive NEVER contains a private age identity or the checkpoint signing key. The documented recovery package = archive + offline copies of those keys (incl. the checkpoint/manifest public key for offline verification); a store backup without them is NOT a recovery, and the docs must say so plainly.

**Epoch rules (KTD17/R32):**
- Every restore increments `current_credential_epoch` (pre-restore bearer credentials die cryptographically).
- Every restore mints and discloses a replacement emergency CONTROL credential as part of activation (R41's primitive, owned by U8.3, R17 sink-validated disclosure ordering). This is a FROZEN rule, not an implementation nicety: the epoch bump kills every pre-restore credential including control ones, and no bootstrap route exists on any listener — without this rule a restored store is unauthenticatable by design.
- A normal restore continues the existing audit epoch only when its head is consistent with the supplied checkpoint set.
- A ROLLBACK recovery (restoring behind the newest checkpoint IN THE SUPPLIED SET — see U10.4 for why the guard's basis is the supplied set) requires explicit `--start-recovery-epoch` + reason; increments credential AND audit epochs; opens the new epoch with a first event recording restored head, missing anchored range, supplied checkpoint digest, archive digest, actor, reason (the fork-record event type — defined jointly with Gate G2 so the frozen audit schema includes it). Verification forever reports the historical anchored epoch and the recovery fork SEPARATELY — never a continuous chain.

**Exit:** format document + frame/manifest schema committed (both manifest states + signing ceremony + restore policy included); recovery-family event types handed to G2; reviewed; frozen. Later changes are R35 forward-only migrations with upgrade notes. Feeds M0; blocks U10 implementation beads.

title: U10.2: backup — consistent logical archive + recovery keyring rewrap + signed manifest (R32)
description:
**Build:** `backup` — produce KTD17's logical archive from ONE consistent redb read transaction while the server runs (R32).

**Contents per the frozen G3 format:** versioned canonical table frames; encrypted record bytes unchanged; authenticated clear-record bytes; current keyring envelope; recovery keyring rewrapped to the CONFIGURED BACKUP RECIPIENTS (which may differ from the live server recipient) with their non-secret fingerprints recorded in the manifest; all audit events; per-table counts + digests; the signed manifest (store id, schema version, keyring generation, chain head, latest included checkpoint, checksums).

**Why the recipient rewrap matters:** a fresh-host restore needs the archive recipient identity and can rewrap the recovered keyring to a NEW live server recipient before activation — it does NOT require the failed host's active identity. That is the difference between a backup and a recovery story.

**Encryption:** archive encrypted to one or more configured backup recipients. Private keys never enter the archive. Backup requires its own management capability (R22); runs through the reserved control lane so it works even when the data plane has failed closed (KTD8/R27).

**Acceptance:** backup taken during concurrent writes restores to a transactionally consistent point (no torn record); manifest fields complete and signed; recovery-recipient fingerprints present; archive contains no private key material (scan); works via control socket while serving.

title: U10.3: restore — verify-everything temp-sibling build, atomic install, credential-epoch bump (R32)
description:
**Build:** `restore` — construct a NEW database from an archive; verify EVERYTHING before activation; make credential resurrection impossible (R32).

**Flow:** under `lifecycle=restoring` (R35), build a new database in a TEMPORARY SIBLING of the EMPTY target directory (a non-empty target refuses, directory untouched) → verify every frame, record MAC, ciphertext (authenticated decrypt), audit relationship, schema, count, AND the manifest signature — verified against the operator-supplied checkpoint-verification public key from the recovery package under the backup domain label (U10.1); an unsigned-but-checksummed manifest proceeds only with explicit `--allow-unsigned-manifest` (warned, audited) → fsync the database and directory → ATOMICALLY install only after ALL verification succeeds. A tampered or corrupt archive leaves the target directory inert (AE7 tamper case).

**Credential epoch (the resurrection defense):** restore atomically increments `current_credential_epoch`. Every restored credential record retains its ORIGINAL issue_epoch, so ALL pre-restore tokens and secret_ids fail epoch validation (R24's MAC domain) even where verifier and TTL would otherwise pass — a restored snapshot cannot revive credentials revoked after it was taken. Identities, grants, secrets, and audit history survive; bearer credentials are reissued. The restore is recorded as a recovery event in the first new audit entry.

**Post-restore access bootstrap (the lockout defense):** the epoch bump also kills every pre-restore CONTROL credential, and no bootstrap route exists on any listener — so restore mints and discloses a replacement emergency control credential during activation, invoking R41's primitive (owned by U8.3, bead olss-charter-qul.10.3 — an explicit dependency: restore CALLS that implementation, never rebuilds it) with R17's disclosure-before-commit ordering: validate the credential output sink → generate → write+flush to the sink → only then complete the atomic install whose transaction carries its verifier. A failed sink write aborts activation with the target inert. The first new audit entry records the recovery event and the emergency issuance. The operator uses this credential to reissue normal operator/workload credentials — without it, a restored store would be unauthenticatable (G3 frozen rule, U10.1).

**Restore-incarnation marker (the split-brain defense):** the recovery event records a fresh restore-incarnation nonce; all subsequent audit entries and checkpoint descriptors carry it. If the "failed" source host is in fact still alive and appending, two live stores with the same store id extend the same chain prefix divergently — each internally valid; the incarnation marker lets `audit verify` (U6.4) name them as distinct incarnations and report the divergence point instead of a generic chain break. docs/operating.md's restore sequence therefore BEGINS with "confirm the source host is stopped/decommissioned".

**Inputs:** requires the archive recipient age identity; rewraps the recovered keyring to a fresh live server recipient before activation (U8.1's primitive). Restore without a usable identity fails with NO partial initialization.

**Acceptance:** AE7 core — fresh-host restore: every secret readable, identities/grants intact, chain verifies against the last off-host checkpoint, pre-restore bearer credentials rejected (the revoked token STAYS revoked); the emergency control credential is disclosed exactly once, authenticates, and is what issues the fresh working credential (which then works); a shared-symbol/integration test proves restore invokes U8.3's primitive; tampered member OR bad/missing manifest signature → verification fails before activation, target inert; unsigned manifest without --allow-unsigned-manifest → refused; restore into a non-empty target → refused, directory untouched; broken credential sink → activation aborts, target inert; the recovery event carries the incarnation nonce and subsequent entries/checkpoints carry it; redb-version upgrade between backup and restore succeeds without opening an old DB file (KTD17).

title: U10.4: rollback recovery — explicit audit-epoch fork (--start-recovery-epoch, KTD17)
description:
**Build:** Rollback recovery — restoring BEHIND the newest checkpoint in the SUPPLIED set (KTD17/R32).

**Why it is special:** a restore whose audit head predates a supplied checkpoint means anchored history is being abandoned — that must never look routine, and the abandoned range must never silently vanish.

**Guard basis (stated honestly):** checkpoints live off-host; the restore process can only evaluate the checkpoint set the operator SUPPLIES. "Behind the newest checkpoint" therefore means the newest IN THE SUPPLIED SET. An operator (or attacker with archive access) who withholds the newest checkpoint gets a routine-looking restore — the guard's protection is only as strong as the completeness of the supplied mirror, and docs/operating.md must say so. The backstop is R14, not this guard: a subsequent `audit verify` given the complete mirror flags the missing anchored range — the dodge leaves permanent tamper evidence, it does not erase history.

**Behavior:**
- Without `--start-recovery-epoch`: REFUSED.
- The flag REQUIRES a reason — `--start-recovery-epoch` without a reason is refused.
- With flag + reason: increments BOTH the credential epoch and the AUDIT epoch; opens the new epoch with a first event recording: the restored head, the missing anchored range, the supplied checkpoint digest, the archive digest, the actor, and the reason (the fork-record event type frozen at G2 via U10.1).
- Future checkpoints identify the new epoch. `audit verify` forever reports the historical anchored epoch and the recovery fork SEPARATELY — never presenting the chain as continuous (epoch-scoped sequences + epoch genesis entries, U6.3, make the two ranges unambiguous).

**Acceptance:** archive behind the newest SUPPLIED checkpoint without the flag → refused; flag without reason → refused; with flag + reason → verifier reports old anchored history, the missing range, and the new audit epoch as distinct facts; fork event carries all named fields; post-fork checkpoints chain within the new epoch; a restore performed with the newest checkpoint WITHHELD proceeds as a normal restore, and a subsequent `audit verify` against the complete mirror flags the missing anchored range (the R14 backstop, tested explicitly).

title: U10.5: backup verify + --full disaster-recovery rehearsal with recorded age (R32)
description:
**Build:** `backup verify` (manifest/frame check without restoring) + `backup verify --full` — the STANDING DISASTER-RECOVERY REHEARSAL (R32).

**--full flow:** restore into an isolated temporary directory → validate schema and tables → authenticate and DECRYPT EVERY ENCRYPTED RECORD without emitting any plaintext → verify the audit chain and checkpoint relationship → tear the temporary store down → record the result.

**Two result kinds — the distinction IS the DR guarantee:** a control-lane rehearsal against the running server decrypts via the LIVE keyring envelope and records **"integrity-verified (live path)"**; an OFFLINE rehearsal takes an archive-recipient identity file and MUST unwrap via the RECOVERY keyring envelope, recording **"DR-rehearsed (recovery path)"**. Only the second proves the fact disaster recovery depends on — that the configured backup-recipient key actually unwraps this archive on a host with no live server. A backup could pass live-path rehearsals forever and still be unrecoverable on a real fresh host (wrong/rotated/lost recipient key); doctor therefore reports BOTH ages separately and flags a backup-recipient rotation that invalidates prior DR rehearsals.

**Rehearsal record:** binds the rehearsed archive's MANIFEST DIGEST (a rehearsal of last month's archive proves nothing about last night's). Control-lane runs store it in server meta (audited); offline runs emit a signed-checksum receipt registerable via `backup rehearsal record` over the control socket. Doctor flags when the LATEST backup's digest has no rehearsal of either kind.

**Posture (docs must say it plainly):** a backup that has never survived a rehearsal is treated as UNPROVEN; a backup never rehearsed through the RECOVERY envelope is unproven as DR. The manifest-only `backup verify` cannot catch a corrupted encrypted record; only the rehearsal decrypts everything.

**Acceptance:** --full on a good archive → passes, temp store removed, NO plaintext emitted or logged (canary scan), recorded rehearsal age queryable; offline --full with the archive-recipient identity unwraps via the RECOVERY envelope and records DR-rehearsed; offline --full with a MISMATCHED archive-recipient identity → fails naming the recovery envelope (not a generic decrypt error); with ONE corrupted encrypted record → the rehearsal FAILS naming the record, where manifest-only verify cannot; control-lane rehearsal records integrity-verified (live path), distinct from DR-rehearsed; `backup rehearsal record` registers an offline receipt; doctor distinguishes both ages and flags an unrehearsed latest backup.

title: U10.6: backup/restore test suite (tests/backup.rs — AE7, epoch, fork, rehearsal)
description:
**Build:** tests/backup.rs — the backup/restore suite. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. AE7: backup a running server; restore onto a fresh data directory → every secret readable, identities + grants intact, audit chain verifies against the last off-host checkpoint, pre-restore bearer credentials rejected (a token revoked AFTER the backup stays revoked — epoch, not luck); the replacement emergency control credential disclosed during restore authenticates and is what issues the fresh credential, which then authenticates (no other issuance path exists — every pre-restore credential is dead).
2. Backup during concurrent writes → restores to a transactionally consistent point, no torn record.
3. Restore a backup predating a token revocation → that token stays invalid (credential epoch).
4. Tamper with one archive member → verification fails BEFORE activation; target directory stays inert.
5. Archive behind the newest supplied checkpoint without --start-recovery-epoch → refused; with it → verifier reports old anchored history, missing range, and the new audit epoch — never a continuous chain (KTD17).
6. Upgrade redb while the logical archive version stays supported → restore succeeds into the new redb version without opening an old database file (KTD17 — this test is what keeps R38's prior-release smoke fixture viable).
7. Restore without a usable age identity → fails, no partial initialization.
8. Restore with a broken/unwritable emergency-credential sink → activation aborts, target directory inert (R17 ordering applied to restore — disclosure failure must not produce an installed-but-unauthenticatable store).
9. backup verify --full on a good archive → passes, temp store removed, no plaintext emitted (canary scan), rehearsal age queryable; with one corrupted encrypted record → fails naming the record where manifest-only verify cannot (R32).
10. AE7 fresh-host variant, RECOVERY-envelope path: executed in an environment containing ONLY the backup-recipient identity — the original server identity deleted first — proving restore has no hidden reliance on the live identity (U10.2's entire rewrap rationale).
11. `--start-recovery-epoch` without a reason → refused (U10.4's required-reason rule).
12. Restore into a non-empty target directory → refused, directory untouched.
13. Manifest tamper matrix: signature stripped → restore refuses without --allow-unsigned-manifest (and warns/audits with it); manifest re-signed under a DIFFERENT key → refused before any frame processing (verification is against the operator-supplied public key — U10.1/U10.3).
14. Split-brain divergence: restore while the source store continues appending → two continuations of one prefix; `audit verify` given both identifies the divergence point and names the two restore incarnations distinctly (U10.3's incarnation marker + U6.4).

**Verification:** `cargo test --test backup` green; the documented operator sequence exercised end-to-end.

title: U11.1: compat harness — pinned fnox/vault/bao drive the real server (R2)
U11.2: differential suite vs pinned OpenBao reference + reasoned divergence allowlist (R1)
description:
**Build:** The differential suite — replays a normalized request corpus against BOTH a pinned OpenBao reference server and this implementation, comparing status class, error envelope shape, and field presence.

**Why:** the fixture suites prove what clients SEND; the differential suite proves this server's INTERPRETATION matches the reference rather than its own fixtures' assumptions — the guard against self-consistent wrongness (R1 fidelity).

**Corpus:** reads, writes, CAS conflicts, deletes/undeletes/destroys, metadata operations, lists, missing paths, auth errors.

**Mechanics:** volatile fields (request ids, timestamps) normalized; DELIBERATE divergences — R3's explicit-unsupported surface, and anything the contract intentionally refuses — are ALLOWLISTED WITH A STATED REASON each. An unexplained divergence fails the suite. A seeded deliberate divergence must be caught by the comparator (tests the comparator itself).

**Files:** tests/differential/.

**Acceptance:** corpus agreement across the pinned OpenBao reference; seeded divergence caught; every allowlist entry names its reason; suite wired into CI (gates R1 in the Verification Contract).

title: U11.3: acceptance suite — AE1–AE13 automated (tests/acceptance/)
description:
**Build:** tests/acceptance/mod.rs — AE1–AE13 as automated integration tests asserting PRODUCT-level outcomes (each already has unit-level cousins; these prove the composed system). All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a). AE9 and AE10 run on U11.4's consolidated named kill points (explicit dependency) — the acceptance suite never duplicates injection machinery.

1. AE1 unimplemented endpoint → explicit unsupported naming the endpoint.
2. AE2 revoked-before-TTL token → rejected + rejection audited.
3. AE3 staged rotation → three consumers enumerated; begin/cutover; prior version readable until complete; stale-cas fails; supersession blocks completion.
4. AE4 missing key material at boot → non-zero exit naming the setting.
5. AE5 tampered audit log → verification fails identifying the break.
6. AE6 scope denial across path forms + ambiguous encodings → denied/rejected + audited.
7. AE7 backup/restore → secrets/identities/grants survive; chain verifies vs off-host checkpoint; pre-restore credentials dead; fresh credential works.
8. AE8 clean-host onboarding → documented sequence (with whatever prerequisites U0 proved) reaches a real fnox read, configuration-only.
9. AE9 crash atomicity → U11.4's named kill points around write + revocation: state and audit together or not at all.
10. AE10 audit-commit failure → injected via U11.4's harness: error response, zero secret bytes.
11. AE11 rotation adoption → on-current vs silent-since-write from audited read versions; completion refused without override.
12. AE12 management-not-remote → routes ABSENT on the remote listener (explicit rejection, not authz failure) while the same ops succeed over the control socket.
13. AE13 pre-migration reconciliation → declared/authorized/observed separately visible; unmigrated declared consumer blocks unqualified completion.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test acceptance` green in CI against the pinned matrix. This bead is the Definition-of-Done's AE gate.

title: U11.4: fault suite — kill points, disk-full, short-write injection (tests/fault/, gates R26)
description:
**Build:** The standing fault suite (tests/fault/) — the invariants are ATTACKED, not assumed. Suites log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Injections:**
- child-process KILL POINTS around: secret write, token revocation, backup creation, restore install, recipient rewrap, record-key re-encrypt, **rotation cutover** (prepared→cutover + CAS write + audit commit atomic — after restart the rotation is either still prepared with no new version, or cutover with the version and its audit entry; U7.3's core transition), **init's disclosure window** (R17: crash after sink flush / before commit → no store, the disclosed credential unusable; crash after commit → store exists, credential authenticates), **checkpoint phase boundaries** (crash between prepare/sign and sign/register → re-prepare idempotent, verify unaffected, previous digest references last REGISTERED — U6.4), and **epoch-rotate** (crash mid-bump → epoch validation is all-old or all-new, never mixed; the emergency credential is disclosed-and-valid or the bump did not commit — U8.3's R17 ordering). Feeds AE9.
- The per-unit suites (U6.6/U8.4/U10.6) land FIRST and may seed local kill-point helpers; this bead CONSOLIDATES them into ONE shared harness with named kill points, retrofits the per-unit suites onto it (the sweep is tracked and completed here), and owns its CI wiring. U11.3's AE9/AE10 acceptance tests run on these named kill points.
- DISK-FULL injection: hitting R27's thresholds and beyond — data plane fails closed, reserve keeps checkpoint/backup/shutdown alive.
- SHORT-WRITE injection at the storage layer: torn writes never produce a readable-but-wrong record (redb + MAC + AEAD must jointly refuse).

**Cadence:** kill-point and disk-full suites run PER PR (Verification Contract); they gate R26.

**Acceptance:** consolidated harness provides named kill points covering every family above; per-unit suites (U6.6/U8.4/U10.6) run on it with no duplicated injection machinery left behind; all injections runnable locally and in CI; each failure mode produces the documented outcome (refusal/recovery), never silent corruption. Feeds milestone M2 (crash tests).

title: U11.5: fuzz targets — token, path, JSON, record, checkpoint decoders (fuzz/)
U11.6: secret-output canary scan across all artifacts, wired into CI (R25)
description:
**Build:** The secret-output canary scan (R25) — seeded secret values must appear in NO log, trace, panic output, or test artifact, anywhere, ever.

**Mechanics:** tests seed distinctive high-entropy canary values as secrets/credentials; after every suite run, a scanner sweeps ALL captured artifacts — server logs, trace output, panic/crash text, test stdout/stderr, U0's committed fixtures, backup archives' plaintext regions, doctor output — for any canary substring. One hit fails CI.

**Scope notes:** U0 fixtures are in scope (normalization must have stripped capture credentials); the ONLY permitted appearance of a secret is the body of an explicitly authorized successful secret-read response (R25's single exception); audit payloads in the DB file must be ciphertext (U6.6 scenario 5 byte-scan composes here).

**Acceptance:** scanner wired into CI over every artifact class; a deliberately leaked canary (test-only fault) is caught; the Verification Contract's canary gate is green.

title: U11.7: performance baseline — audited read/write benchmark on a named host (benches/)
description:
**Build:** The performance baseline benchmark (benches/) — Benchmark honesty (Performance & Capacity).

**What it records:** authenticated, AUDITED reads and writes (the real path: token verify → grant reload → decrypt → audit commit → respond) on a NAMED reference host with durability settings STATED. p50/p95/p99 queue and commit latency; headroom over the documented expected peak (tens of concurrent clients, low hundreds of req/s).

**Rules:** no throughput number is a release promise until this measures it (U2.1's spike seeds the envelope; this bead measures the composed system). Later changes cannot materially regress the recorded baseline without EXPLICIT review — the baseline is the tripwire against someone "optimizing" R26's durable-write-per-read into a fast broken design. Every audited op is a durable write; that ceiling is accepted and documented, not a bug to fix.

**Acceptance:** benchmark runs reproducibly on the named host; baseline committed (numbers + host + settings); CI or documented cadence compares against it; regression procedure documented.

title: U11.8: CI pipeline — pinned clients, every Verification Contract gate wired (ci.yml)
U12.1: doctor — operator health checks with stable JSON + exit codes
U12.2: docs/operating.md + tested hardened systemd example units
U12.3: docs/compatibility.md generated from contract tests (normative R1 artifact)
description:
**Build:** docs/compatibility.md as a GENERATED artifact — the normative compatibility claim (R1).

**Rules:**
- The method/path/status/body matrix is GENERATED FROM CONTRACT TESTS (U11's compat + differential suites over U0's fixtures) — prose summaries never add an endpoint absent from the tests.
- Per pinned client version: required endpoints, prerequisite binaries, token/TLS supply, known caveats (the preflight contract, non-renewable tokens, explicit-unsupported list including PATCH and remote metadata DELETE).
- The BUSL exit-ladder status (from U0.4) with the honest licensing note if the `vault` CLI remains a documented path — never claiming a fully-OSI toolchain while it is the only path.
- States R2's boundary honestly: configuration-only compatibility holds for the TESTED versions, best-effort outside the matrix until captured and promoted by contract tests.

**Acceptance:** regenerating from a passing test run reproduces the committed doc byte-stable (or with a documented volatile header); a contract-test change without regeneration fails CI; matrix names every pinned version + checksum provenance.

title: U12.4: README positioning, MIT LICENSE, CONTRIBUTING, SECURITY.md, deny.toml (R19/KTD13/R38)
description:
**Build:** README + LICENSE + CONTRIBUTING.md + SECURITY.md + deny.toml.

**README:** leads with the settled positioning paragraph (working name until OQ3's before-any-public-artifact deadline): a single-binary, Vault-KV-v2-compatible secrets server for teams too small to run Vault; exists for ONE workflow — rotating a credential without an outage; every read audited atomically so before revoking upstream you can see exactly who fetched the old value and who picked up the new one — declared, authorized, observed, never collapsed; the ops-light claim is a LIST OF REFUSALS (no cluster, no external DB, no policy language, no plugins, no remote management, no unseal ceremony); management on a local socket; one operator can run backup/restore/key-rotation/incident-response alone; works with unmodified vault/bao CLIs and fnox; MIT, whole tree, nothing held back. README also says "outgrow it → OpenBao" OUT LOUD (T01's accepted consequence). No public artifact claims recovery-readiness before M2 passes on the published binary.

**LICENSE:** MIT, entire tree, no ee/ carve-out (R19/KTD13 — revised from MPL-2.0, wayfinder T13, Robert's call; closed forks knowingly permitted; the anti-BUSL posture is the product's own openness, not copyleft). The existing repo LICENSE (MIT © 2026 Robert Guss) stands.

**SECURITY.md:** the documented vulnerability-reporting process (R38).

**CONTRIBUTING.md:** including the fnox-CONTRIBUTING-checks proxy (Success Criteria: the codebase passes the checks in fnox's own CONTRIBUTING.md, applied directly).

**deny.toml:** cargo-deny config for licenses + advisories (license config follows MIT) — gives the Verification Contract's `cargo deny check` gate an owner.

**Acceptance:** cargo deny check passes; README positioning matches the settled paragraph; SECURITY.md process actionable; no public-artifact naming before OQ3 is resolved.

title: U12.5: release pipeline — signed checksums, SBOM, provenance, published-binary smoke (R38)
description:
**Build:** The release pipeline (.github/workflows/release.yml) — attestable releases (R38).

**Every release artifact ships with:** signed checksums; a software bill of materials (SBOM); build provenance. MSRV pinned; Cargo.lock committed; `--locked` enforced everywhere; dependency advisories + licenses checked (deny.toml); upgrade notes for every storage-format change. Artifacts carry the FINAL project name (OQ3 bead) — the working-name grep gate runs here.

**The release smoke test consumes the ACTUAL PUBLISHED BINARY — not a local build:** it drives `scripts/e2e.sh` (U11.9's gauntlet, checksum-matched against the published artifact) on BOTH claimed architectures, covering init → serve → doctor → fnox read → rotation lifecycle → checkpoint/verify → backup → `backup verify --full` → restore → recipient rewrap → offline re-encrypt → `credential epoch rotate` → secret purge → rollback fork restore → audit export → `doctor --full` — AND additionally restores a store fixture from at least one prior release (R35's migration fixture; U10.6 scenario 6 keeps that fixture viable across redb versions).

**First-release bootstrap (v0.1 has no prior release):** the pipeline GENERATES and commits the store fixture from the release candidate itself; the restore-from-prior step is skipped exactly once, the skip recorded in the release notes; every subsequent release must restore the previous release's fixture.

**Format-change detection (the gate needs a signal):** the pipeline compares the persisted schema/format version constants (R35 — store format_version, audit schema version, backup archive version, checkpoint descriptor version) against the previous release tag; a delta without a matching upgrade-notes entry fails the job.

**Claims discipline:** release artifacts and claims name the supported OS, architecture, filesystem, and deployment mode (Linux x86_64 + aarch64, host service, local ext4/XFS); cross-compilation alone does not promote another target into the supported matrix — the smoke runs on both claimed architectures or the claim is cut.

**Acceptance:** the workflow emits signed checksums, SBOM, and provenance for each artifact; the smoke test provably runs against the published artifact (checksum match) on both architectures, including doctor exit-0 after serve and doctor --full after the final restore/verify; prior-release fixture restore green (or the recorded one-time bootstrap skip); a release with a storage-format change and no upgrade notes fails the pipeline; no working-name strings in any public artifact.

title: U12.6: CLI surface coherence — stable JSON, no argv secrets, audited destructive commands (R16/R37)
U6.7: audit query & export — capability-gated control-plane commands (R22/R23/R25)
description:
**Build:** `audit query` + `audit export` over the control socket / offline CLI — the read surface that R22's audit-read and audit-export capabilities exist to gate (AE12 names "audit query" as a control operation; U6.3 built the events and the scan path, but no bead owned the operator-facing commands).

**`audit query`:** scans PRIMARY events (KTD16's index is deferred; the scan is the only query path and always authoritative — R23) with bounded filters: time range, identity, canonical path prefix, outcome, operation class, sequence range. Event payloads decrypt under the keyring's audit-payload key ONLY for callers holding audit-read (R22); results are paginated/bounded (R27 posture — no unbounded result allocation). Fields follow R13's schema; credential material never appears (R25 — accessors are the public, non-secret form).

**`audit export`:** exports a sequence range as an ENCRYPTED bundle by default — v0.1 ships NO plaintext bulk export (Product Contract; same posture as backup). The bundle encrypts to configured recipients (age, reusing U2.3 machinery), requires the audit-export capability, and carries chain envelopes + encrypted payloads + **the signed covering checkpoint FILE embedded in the bundle (not merely its digest)** so a recipient can verify integrity offline (KTD4) — offline verification uses the checkpoint public key obtained out-of-band (the recovery package / operator channel, never the bundle itself). A requested range with no covering REGISTERED checkpoint still exports, but the bundle labels that range UNANCHORED — mirroring R14's two verification tiers, never implying anchorage that does not exist.

**Caller model (no second authorization model — charter rule 6):** identical to U3.6's control-plane commands — the SO_PEERCRED-verified socket peer resolves to a local operator identity whose grants are evaluated against the R22 closed capability enum inside the authorization transaction. This bead introduces no new mapping; it reuses U3.6's mechanism.

**Rules:** both are management ops: control socket or offline only (R34); every invocation is itself audited — attributability applies to auditors too (R13); query/export retain admission through the reserved control lane when the data plane has failed closed (KTD8/R27) — incident investigation must work exactly then (U6.5's reserve sizing budgets N such invocations).

**Acceptance:** filters return correct bounded pages against a seeded event corpus; caller without audit-read → denied + audited; export produces an encrypted bundle that verifies offline against its EMBEDDED checkpoint (public key supplied out-of-band); an uncovered range exports labeled unanchored; no plaintext-export flag exists; both commands absent from the remote router (feeds AE12); both work via the reserved lane while data-plane admission is stopped (with U6.5); canary scan covers query/export output (R25); scenario tests land in tests/audit.rs with step-level logging (U11.9a harness).

title: U3.6: identity & grant management + authz explain — control-plane commands (R16/R22/R28/R34/KTD10)
description:
**Build:** The operator-facing identity and grant lifecycle commands over the control socket (R34), with CLI client subcommands (R16): `identity create|list|show|disable` (kind: human|workload — R7) and `grant add|remove|list` using U3.2's exact shape {mount, exact_or_subtree, prefix_segments, capabilities} (R8/R22). Plus `authz explain` — the management-gated diagnostic command (KTD10) that evaluates a named identity against a resource/operation and returns the matcher's FULL structured decision: {allow, resource, operation, matched_grant | deny_reason}, deny reasons discriminating prefix-boundary miss from missing capability.

**Why this bead exists:** the plan names these operations everywhere — AE12 attempts "identity creation" against the remote listener, Flow F1 issues operator/workload credentials right after init, U3.3's acceptance proves grant reduction takes effect — but no unit owned the COMMANDS themselves (U12.6 is a coherence pass over existing commands, not their implementation). This bead is the owner. Same for `authz explain`: U3.2 builds the structured-decision seam (KTD10) and names the management-gated command, but no other bead implements it — it lands here with the identity/grant surface it diagnoses.

**Rules:**
- Control-plane only (R34): reachable via the owner-only Unix socket (U1.4) or offline CLI; ABSENT from the remote router — a remote attempt gets explicit route-not-found (AE12).
- Caller must hold the identity+grant management capability (R22), presented via a control-audience credential (U4.1); capability check + mutation + audit entry commit in ONE coordinator transaction (R26/R28).
- `authz explain` is management-gated and itself audited; the full decision is NEVER echoed to unauthorized clients (R25/KTD10) — a diagnostic seam, not a policy debugger.
- Identity disable and grant reduction take effect at the next linearized request (R28 — U3.3 proves the effect; this bead supplies the operator levers).
- Human-readable AND stable JSON output (R16); no secret material in any input or output.
- Identities/grants persist as KTD15 MAC-authenticated clear records (U2.5/U3.2).

**Acceptance:** create/list/show/disable + grant add/remove/list round-trip over the socket; `grant add` rejects cross-plane shapes (management capabilities on a KV mount, secret capabilities on the synthetic `sys` management mount — U3.2's rule); the same routes are explicitly absent on the remote listener (feeds AE12); op without the capability → denied AND audited; disable/reduction observable at the next linearized request (with U3.3); `authz explain` returns matched_grant on allow and the discriminated deny_reason on deny, requires its management capability, is audited, and is absent from the remote router; JSON output schema-stable (seed test); scenario tests land in tests/control_mgmt.rs with step-level logging (U11.9a shared harness).

title: U4.5: credential management commands — token issue/revoke, AppRole role & secret_id lifecycle (R16/R24/R34)
description:
**Build:** Control-plane credential lifecycle commands (R34/R16): `token issue` (for an identity; TTL; audience data|control per R31's audience rules), `token revoke` (by public accessor), `token list` (accessors + metadata — never secrets); `approle role create|list|delete` (binds a role_id to an identity; sets the role's token_ttl consumed by login — U4.2), `secret-id issue` (TTL, bounded use count, consumer_instance_id — required for workloads unless the operator explicitly accepts identity-only tracking, KTD5), `secret-id revoke` (by accessor), `secret-id list` (per role: accessors, TTL, remaining uses, consumer_instance_id — NEVER secret material).

**Why this bead exists:** F1's "issue normal operator/workload credentials over the control socket" and AE2's revoke-before-TTL need these commands; U4.1 built the credential MACHINERY (format, keyed verifiers, accessor index) but no bead owned the operator surface that mints and revokes.

**Why `secret-id list` is load-bearing:** the disclosure-once recovery story ("issue a fresh credential, revoke the orphan via its accessor") only works if orphan accessors are DISCOVERABLE for every credential kind. `token list` covers tokens; without `secret-id list`, a lost secret-id issuance response leaves a live, effectively unrevocable credential until an epoch bump — the exact failure mode the recovery story exists to close.

**Rules:**
- Disclosure-once (R24): the command response is the ONLY disclosure of a raw credential; only accessor + keyed verifier + metadata persist. A lost response is recovered by issuing a fresh credential and revoking the orphan via its accessor (`token list` / `secret-id list` make orphans visible) — deliberately NO re-disclosure path.
- Issuance and revocation are coordinator transactions with their audit entries (R26); revocation linearizes (R28).
- Capability: credential issue/revoke (R22), distinct from identity management — an audit-pulling integration can never mint credentials.
- Control-plane only (R34); raw values returned once and never logged (R25); nothing accepts secrets via argv (R37).

**Acceptance:** issue → authenticate → revoke round-trip (drives AE2); revoked accessor fails at the next linearized request; secret-id issued with use count + instance id is consumed by AppRole login (with U4.2); an orphaned secret_id (issuance response discarded) is discovered via `secret-id list` and revoked by accessor; raw secret unrecoverable after issuance (R24); wrong-capability attempts denied + audited; JSON schema-stable; tests in tests/control_mgmt.rs with step-level logging (U11.9a harness).

title: U5.8: secret purge — local-only full deletion command (R29/R22/R16)
description:
**Build:** `secret purge` — full deletion (metadata + ALL versions) of one secret path. LOCAL control plane ONLY; the remote `DELETE /v1/:mount/metadata/:path` stays in R3's explicit-unsupported set (U5.4) — this command is the only full-purge path in v0.1 (R29).

**Why this bead exists:** R29 and U5.2 name "the local control-plane `secret purge`" as the only full-deletion route, and U12.6 lists it in the CLI families — but no bead owned its implementation.

**Rules:**
- Requires the dedicated destroy-all capability (R22 — distinct from destroy, never bundled into read/write), the EXACT resource identifier, an explicit confirmation, and an audited reason (U12.6's destructive-command rule; the reason lands in the audit entry).
- One coordinator transaction: purge + audit entry commit atomically (R26).
- Destruction is LOGICAL, same documented boundary as destroy (R29): historical backups, copy-on-write pages, and unreclaimed filesystem blocks may retain ciphertext until backup expiry/compaction/media replacement — no cryptographic-erasure claim.
- Fail-safe interaction with rotation (design decision recorded in this bead): purge is REFUSED while the path's rotation state (KTD11 secret_meta server field, U2.2) shows an active record (prepared | cutover) — cancel or complete the rotation first. Rationale: R29 forbids retention pruning from retiring a rotation's protected prior version; an explicit purge must not silently do what retention is forbidden to do. Reads secret_meta's rotation-state field only, so this bead does not depend on U7's lifecycle machinery.
- After purge, reads/lists of the path behave per captured-contract semantics for a nonexistent path (U5.4 fixtures).

**Acceptance:** purge removes metadata + every version from every read path and API form; remote metadata DELETE remains explicitly unsupported (regression guard with U5.4); missing capability / wrong resource / missing confirmation / missing reason each refused with distinct errors; active-rotation path refused; audit entry carries the reason; command absent from the remote router; scenario tests land in tests/control_mgmt.rs with step-level logging (U11.9 harness convention).

title: U11.9: e2e gauntlet script + shared test-observability harness (detailed logging)
description:
**Build:** `scripts/e2e.sh` (or `cargo xtask e2e`) — the from-scratch assembled-system gauntlet. (The shared test-observability harness itself is U11.9a, olss-charter-qul.12.10, built FIRST so every earlier suite already logs through it; this bead EXTENDS it for shell-script stages — stage banners, per-stage timing, run-directory collection — never introduces a second logging convention.)

**The gauntlet:** numbered stages, per-stage PASS/FAIL + duration, and a timestamped run directory collecting every log:
build `--locked` → full test suites → `init` (credential captured via sink) → `serve` over TLS → health/seal-status/preflight probes → `doctor` (exit 0, schema-valid JSON) → identity/grant/AppRole-role provisioning + token issue over the control socket (the M3 falsifiable-claim workflow: provision → issue → later revoke; exercises U3.6/U4.5) → consumer declare + reconciliation view check (declared-but-unauthorized / authorized-but-undeclared / observed-but-undeclared each separately visible — R40/AE13's operator surface; the declared consumer makes the later closeout guard actually bite) → pinned `vault`, `bao`, and fnox round-trips (put/get/list, configuration-only) → KV lifecycle stage: delete → undelete → destroy + metadata read-back per the U5.2 read-shape matrix → AppRole login → scoped read + denial probe (+ `authz explain` on the denial) → token revoke mid-TTL recheck → rotation begin/cutover/status/complete (completion first REFUSED while the declared consumer is silent, then the override path exercised with audited reason — R33/R40) → audit checkpoint prepare/sign/register → `audit verify` → `audit query` spot-check against operations the run just performed (U6.7 e2e) → `backup` → `backup verify --full` → restore into a temp dir + pre-restore-credential rejection check + restore-disclosed emergency credential authenticates (R32/U10.3) → **recovery-stage block (M2's CLI-drivable evidence, all via U8/U10 commands): `key recipient rewrap` + re-read check → graceful stop → offline record `re-encrypt` → restart → re-read (exercises R36 shutdown + U8.2) → `credential epoch rotate --reason` (all prior tokens rejected; the emergency control credential authenticates) → `secret purge` + nonexistent-path recheck → rollback restore into a second temp dir with `--start-recovery-epoch` (verifier reports the fork, never a continuous chain) → `audit export` + offline bundle verify** → `doctor --full` → canary scan over the ENTIRE run directory → summary table.
Non-zero exit at the first failing stage, naming it. CI (U11.8) runs the gauntlet and uploads the run directory as an artifact on failure; the release smoke (U12.5) drives THIS script against the published binary.

**Why:** per-unit suites prove parts; the acceptance suite proves outcomes; this proves the ASSEMBLED system on a real box with logs detailed enough to localize any break to a stage and step. The charter's test-observability convention points at U11.9a; this bead is its e2e capstone.

**Acceptance:** clean run green locally and in CI; an injected failure at any stage stops there, names the stage, retains artifacts; canary scan over the run directory is clean; every recovery-stage command above actually executes in the run (no stage skipped silently — the summary table lists all stages); harness adoption verified across the acceptance, compat, differential, and fault suites AND each per-unit integration suite (adoption asserted here as the final sweep; the harness itself predates every suite via U11.9a).

title: U11.9a: shared test-observability harness — scenario/step/failure logging for all suites (R25-safe)
description:
Extract the harness half of U11.9 (olss-charter-qul.12.9) so it exists BEFORE any integration suite lands — the charter Conventions bind EVERY integration/e2e suite to log through this shared harness, which is only satisfiable if it is built first (four reviewer passes independently flagged the original sequencing, where the harness bead sat second-from-last on the critical path, as unsatisfiable).

**Build.** A common test-support module (used as a dev-dependency path by every suite) providing: scenario begin/end banners with wall-time + monotonic duration; per-step logging (step name, inputs summary, outcome) with timestamps; failure-context dump on assertion failure or panic — captured server stderr/log tail, data-dir listing with sizes/modes, last N audit envelope summaries (sequence, type-id, timestamp — envelope fields only, payloads stay encrypted), and the exact reproduction command line; environment fingerprint (rustc/OS/arch, pinned client versions where relevant) logged once per suite run.

**R25 discipline.** The harness must never become the leak: it logs envelope/metadata fields only, never secret values, credentials, or keyring material; harness-produced logs are themselves inputs to the U11.6 secret-output canary scan (the canary corpus applies to test logs too). Redaction helpers (mask-accessor, digest-only) live here so suites cannot casually print sensitive values.

**Consumers.** tests/startup.rs (U1.6), store.rs (U2.8), audit.rs + fault/txn_crash.rs (U6.6), identity.rs (U3.5), auth.rs (U4.4), api_kv.rs (U5.6), transport.rs (U9.4), rotation.rs (U7.7), reencrypt.rs (U8.4), backup.rs (U10.6), compat harness (U11.1), differential (U11.2), acceptance (U11.3), fault (U11.4), doctor tests (U12.1), and the e2e gauntlet (U11.9, which EXTENDS this harness for shell-script stages rather than introducing a new one).

**Acceptance.** Harness API documented in the module header; one demo test exercises begin/end + step + failure dump and its output is human-readable in CI logs; canary scan over harness-produced logs is green; every suite bead lists this bead as a dependency and uses the harness from its first landed scenario.

title: U11.5b: fuzz targets — backup archive frame/manifest decoders + audit-export bundle verifier
description:
Split from U11.5 (olss-charter-qul.12.5) so the five early fuzz targets are not serialized behind restore. Adds the two remaining attacker-controlled-bytes surfaces once they exist:

**Targets.** (a) Backup archive frame + manifest decoders (the restore read path, U10.3): archives live off-host where an attacker can modify them — restore explicitly treats them as untrusted (AE7 tamper case; threat-model offline-theft scenario), making this the canonical hostile-bytes-at-rest surface. (b) Audit-export bundle offline verifier (U6.7, olss-charter-qul.4.7): parses the same class of attacker-controllable bytes (bundle members, embedded checkpoint file, chain segments) outside the server.

**Rules (same as U11.5).** No panics, no unbounded allocation on malformed frames/manifests/bundles, no accept-then-misinterpret; corpora seeded from U10.6 tamper fixtures and the frozen G3 archive format plus U6.7 export bundles; findings triaged with the same severity discipline as U11.5.

**Acceptance.** Both targets build and run under the same fuzz tooling as U11.5; seed corpora committed; a deliberately corrupted frame, manifest, and bundle each produce a clean structured error (no panic) under the fuzzer; wired into the CI fuzz job (U11.8, olss-charter-qul.12.8).

title: Resolve OQ3: choose the real project name and apply it across public artifacts
description:
The charter requires a real name before ANY public artifact (OQ3) — a GitHub release is the definitive public artifact, and the crate/binary name is baked into release artifact names, systemd ExecStart lines (U12.2), and the README (U12.4). Without an owning bead the graph permits shipping v0.1 under the working name `ops-light-secrets-server` and renaming everything post-release.

**Do.** (1) Choose and record the final project name (decision note in-repo; charter provenance line updated). (2) Apply it across: Cargo.toml package + binary name, README positioning copy, docs/operating.md + tested systemd example units, docs/compatibility.md header, release artifact naming scheme, repository description. (3) Add a grep gate to the release pipeline (U12.5) proving no working-name strings remain in any public artifact (README, docs/, release tarballs, --help output).

**Acceptance.** Final name recorded; all public artifacts carry it; the grep gate runs in the release pipeline and is green; `br` graph note: U12.4 (README/licensing) and U12.5 (release) depend on this bead, so neither can ship under the working name.
